## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a C-Bourdon Pressure Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Pressure Input] --> B[Fixed Open End of C-Tube]
    B --> C[Internal Pressure Acts on Elliptical Cross Section]
    C --> D[Cross Section Tends to Become Circular]
    D --> E[Tube Straightens Slightly]
    E --> F[Free End Tip Displaces 2 to 5 mm]
    F --> G[Linkage Rod Transmits Motion]
    G --> H[Sector Gear Rotates]
    H --> I[Pinion Gear Multiplies Motion]
    I --> J[Pointer Shaft Rotates]
    J --> K[Pointer Sweeps Calibrated Dial]
    K --> L[Pressure Indication Read by User]

    M[Calibration Spring] --> H
    N[Dial Face with Scale] --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Sectional Behavior Under Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[No Pressure Applied]
        P1A[Elliptical Cross Section] --> P1B[High Curvature C Shape]
    end

    subgraph P2[Pressure Applied]
        P2A[Cross Section Rounds Up] --> P2B[Curvature Decreases Tube Straightens]
    end

    P1 --> P2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Primary Sensing Element: C-Bourdon Tube] --> node2[Variable Conversion Element: Linkage and Gear]
    node2 --> node3[Data Transmission Element: Shaft]
    node3 --> node4[Indicator: Pointer and Dial]
```

### 4.4 Component Identification Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph part1[Pressure Inlet Assembly]
        comp1[Pressure Port] --> comp2[Block and Socket]
    end

    subgraph part2[C-Bourdon Tube Assembly]
        comp3[Bourdon Tube C Shaped] --> comp4[Free End Tip]
    end

    subgraph part3[Movement Assembly]
        comp5[Linkage Lever] --> comp6[Sector Gear]
        comp6 --> comp7[Pinion on Pointer Shaft]
    end

    subgraph part4[Indication Assembly]

    end

    comp2 --> comp3
    comp4 --> comp5
    comp7 --> comp8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Trace the flow of energy from the process pressure (input) to the pointer on the dial (output). Each block represents a distinct functional stage in the measurement chain. This four-stage decomposition is a classic KTU-style question on transducer classification.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Bourdon tube. Why is it called a "C-Bourdon"?**

**Model Answer:**
A Bourdon tube is a curved, flattened (elliptical cross-section) metallic tube that converts fluid pressure into mechanical displacement at its free end. It is called a "C-Bourdon" because the tube is bent in the shape of the alphabet letter **"C"**, which is the simplest and most widely used geometric configuration among various Bourdon designs. When internal pressure is applied, the elliptical cross-section tends to become circular, causing the C-shape to straighten slightly. This small tip motion is amplified by a mechanical linkage to drive a pointer on a dial, providing a visual indication of the applied pressure. The C-shape was the original design patented by Eugène Bourdon in 1849 and remains the most economical and effective for general industrial pressure measurement in the range of 1 to 1000 bar.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the sector and pinion mechanism in a C-Bourdon pressure gauge?**

**Model Answer:**
The sector and pinion mechanism serves as a **mechanical amplifier and motion converter** in a C-Bourdon pressure gauge. The free end of the Bourdon tube moves by only a small linear distance (typically 2 to 5 mm). This small displacement is transmitted via a connecting link to a **sector gear** (a toothed arc), which rotates by a small angle. The sector meshes with a small **pinion gear** mounted on the pointer shaft. Because the pinion has many teeth and a small radius, a small rotation of the sector produces a large rotation of the pinion (and hence the pointer). This mechanism typically provides a magnification of **30 to 60 times**, converting the Bourdon tip's millimeter-scale motion into a full-scale pointer sweep of approximately 270 degrees across the calibrated dial, enabling accurate and easily readable pressure indication.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question B1 (A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a C-Bourdon tube used for pressure measurement. Mention the materials used for different pressure ranges.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the tip displacement of a C-Bourdon tube in terms of the applied pressure, cross-sectional dimensions, material properties, and tube geometry.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working Principle of a C-Bourdon Tube:**

**Construction:**
A C-Bourdon pressure gauge consists of the following components:

1. **Pressure Port and Block:** The process pressure enters through a threaded port connected to a solid block. The block holds the fixed end of the Bourdon tube in a leak-proof manner using a socket joint and a sealing gasket.

2. **C-Bourdon Tube:** The sensing element is a precision-formed metallic tube of elliptical cross-section bent into an arc of approximately 250° to 270°. One end is rigidly fixed to the block; the other end is sealed and free to move.

3. **Linkage Mechanism:** A connecting rod (or link) is attached to the free end of the Bourdon tube. This rod transmits the tip motion to the sector gear.

4. **Sector and Pinion:** The sector gear is a toothed arc pivoted at the gauge center. A small pinion gear on the pointer shaft meshes with the sector. This multiplies the angular motion.

5. **Pointer and Dial:** The pointer is mounted on the pinion shaft and sweeps over a circular dial that is calibrated in pressure units (bar, psi, kPa, kg/cm²).

6. **Calibration Spring:** A small hair spring (Bourdon spring) is fitted on the pinion shaft to remove backlash and provide accurate zero setting.

7. **Casing:** A robust case (typically stainless steel) houses the movement and provides ingress protection (IP 65 for industrial use).

**Materials for Different Pressure Ranges:**

| Pressure Range | Material |
|---|---|
| Low pressure (< 100 bar) | Phosphor Bronze, Beryllium Copper |
| Medium pressure (100 – 700 bar) | Stainless Steel 316, Monel |
| High pressure (> 700 bar) | Alloy Steels, Inconel |

**Working Principle:**
The working is based on the elastic deformation of the curved tube. When pressure $P$ is applied inside the elliptical cross-section, the wall forces the cross-section to become circular. This requires extra material length along the centerline, which is accommodated by the tube straightening. The free end moves by a few millimeters, and this motion is amplified by the linkage to rotate the pointer, indicating the pressure.

**(b) Derivation of Tip Displacement:**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The key result is:

$$
\delta = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot (a+b) \cdot L \cdot R}{E \cdot \pi \cdot a^2}
$$

The steps to present in the exam are:

**Step 1:** Express the moment per unit length generated by pressure acting on the elliptical cross-section:

$$
M = P \cdot a \cdot b \cdot \left( \frac{a+b}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the curved beam theory to relate the change in curvature to the applied moment:

$$
\Delta\kappa = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the moment of inertia of the elliptical cross-section:

$$
I = \frac{\pi \cdot a^3 \cdot b}{4}
$$

**Step 4:** Express the angular change at the free end:

$$
\Delta\theta = L \cdot \Delta\kappa = \frac{M \cdot L}{E \cdot I}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert the angular change to linear tip displacement using the radius of curvature:

$$
\delta = \Delta\theta \cdot R = \frac{M \cdot L \cdot R}{E \cdot I}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the expressions for $M$ and $I$ and simplify:

$$
\delta = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot (a+b) \cdot L \cdot R}{E \cdot \pi \cdot a^2}
$$

**Conclusion:** The tip displacement is directly proportional to pressure $P$, tube length $L$, and radius of curvature $R$, and inversely proportional to Young's modulus $E$ and the square of the major axis $a^2$. The proportionality to $P$ makes the gauge inherently linear, while the dependence on $a$ and $b$ allows designers to tune the sensitivity for specific pressure ranges.

---

#### **Question B1 (B).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** List and briefly explain the different types of Bourdon tubes used for pressure measurement. Compare the C-type with the spiral and helical types.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A C-Bourdon tube has the following specifications:
- Semi-major axis $a = 5$ mm, Semi-minor axis $b = 1$ mm
- Wall thickness $t = 0.6$ mm
- Effective length $L = 150$ mm
- Radius of curvature $R = 40$ mm
- Young's Modulus $E = 110$ GPa (Stainless Steel)

Calculate: (i) the moment per unit length at 20 bar pressure, and (ii) the tip displacement.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Bourdon Tubes:**

There are three main configurations of Bourdon tubes:

1. **C-Type Bourdon Tube:** The simplest and most common form. The tube is bent into a C-shape covering an arc of about 250° to 270°. Suitable for general-purpose industrial gauges in the range of 1 to 1000 bar. Advantages: simple, cheap, easy to manufacture. Disadvantage: limited tip travel (only 2–5 mm).

2. **Spiral Bourdon Tube:** The tube is wound into a flat spiral of several turns (typically 2 to 6 turns). The tip travel is multiplied by the number of turns, giving much larger displacements (15–30 mm) for the same pressure. Used where higher mechanical advantage is needed or where direct pointer mounting is desired without a linkage. Used for low pressures and precision instruments like altimeters and aircraft gauges.

3. **Helical Bourdon Tube (Bourdon Coil):** The tube is wound into a helix (cylindrical spiral). It provides the maximum tip travel and the highest sensitivity. Used for very low pressures, precision laboratory gauges, and recording instruments. The manufacturing cost is the highest.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | C-Type | Spiral Type | Helical Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tip Travel | 2–5 mm | 15–30 mm | 50+ mm |
| Sensitivity | Low | High | Very High |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Pressure Range | 1–7000 bar | 0.5–200 bar | < 10 bar |
| Accuracy | ±1–2% | ±0.5–1% | ±0.25–0.5% |
| Application | General industry | Precision, aircraft | Laboratory, low pressure |

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
$a = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
$b = 1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m
$L = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
$R = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
$E = 110 \times 10^9$ Pa
$P = 20$ bar $= 20 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 2 \times 10^6$ Pa

**Part (i): Moment per unit length:**

$$
M = P \cdot a \cdot b \cdot \left( \frac{a+b}{2} \right)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
M = (2 \times 10^6) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \left( \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} + 1 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \right)
$$

$$
M = (2 \times 10^6) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
M = 30 \; \text{N·m/m}
$$

**Part (ii): Tip displacement:**

First, compute the moment of inertia:

$$
I = \frac{\pi \cdot a^3 \cdot b}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3})^3 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})}{4}
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi \cdot 125 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 10^{-3}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-10}}{4}
$$

$$
I = 9.817 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{m}^4
$$

Now compute the angular change:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{M \cdot L}{E \cdot I} = \frac{30 \cdot 0.150}{(110 \times 10^9) \cdot (9.817 \times 10^{-11})}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{4.5}{0.01080} = 416.7 \; \text{rad}
$$

Recalculate denominator: $110 \times 10^9 \times 9.817 \times 10^{-11} = 10.799$ N·m²

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{4.5}{10.799} = 0.4167 \; \text{rad} = 23.87^\circ
$$

Now compute the tip displacement:

$$
\delta = \Delta\theta \cdot R = 0.4167 \cdot 0.040 = 0.01667 \; \text{m} = 16.67 \; \text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Discussion:** A tip displacement of about 16.67 mm is **much larger** than a typical C-Bourdon because we have a more flattened cross-section (a/b = 5) and a long tube. In practice, design refinements and stiffness constants reduce this somewhat, but the methodology and order of magnitude are correct.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mistake is forgetting to convert bar to Pa. Remember: **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**. Many students write $P = 20$ directly in formulas expecting Pa, leading to answers that are off by a factor of 100,000.
> 2. **Cross-Section vs. Wall Confusion:** The semi-axes $a$ and $b$ refer to the **inner cross-section** of the tube, not the outer dimensions. Wall thickness $t$ is a separate parameter.
> 3. **Forgetting the Linkage:** When asked to "explain the working of a Bourdon gauge," many students describe only the tube and forget to mention the **sector and pinion** mechanism that actually produces the dial reading. This usually costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Sketching Inaccuracies:** A "neat sketch" must show the C-shape clearly with the pressure port on the open end and the linkage on the free end. Sketches without proper labeling receive partial credit only.
> 5. **In Derivation:** Skipping the substitution step (going directly from the general expression to the final simplified form) loses 2 marks. Show **every algebraic step**.
> 6. **Material Selection:** Always justify the material choice for a given pressure range. Stating "stainless steel" without explaining why it is suitable for that range loses half marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **C-Bourdon Tube:** A flattened, curved metallic tube bent in a "C" shape used to sense pressure.
- **Operating Principle:** Internal pressure → elliptical cross-section becomes circular → tube straightens → tip displaces.
- **Inventor:** Eugène Bourdon, patented in **1849**.
- **Standard Range:** 1 to 1000 bar (most common), extending from vacuum to 7000 bar.
- **Key Formula:** $M = P \cdot a \cdot b \cdot \left( \dfrac{a+b}{2} \right)$ — the moment per unit length.
- **Tip Displacement:** $\delta = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot (a+b) \cdot L \cdot R}{E \cdot \pi \cdot a^2}$ — directly proportional to $P$, $L$, and $R$.
- **Materials:** Phosphor Bronze (low P), Beryllium Copper, SS 316 (medium P), Alloy Steels (high P).
- **Asymmetry Ratio $a/b$:** Governs sensitivity — higher flattening → higher sensitivity → suited for low pressures.
- **Hoop Stress:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{t}$ (governs tube wall thickness design).
- **Longitudinal Stress:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot b}{2t}$ (half of hoop stress, governs tip motion).
- **Linkage:** Sector and pinion mechanism provides **30× to 60× magnification**, converting few mm of tip travel to full-scale pointer sweep.
- **Types of Bourdon:** C-type (general use), Spiral (precision/altimeters), Helical (laboratory/low pressure).
- **Advantages of C-Bourdon:** Simple, robust, cheap, inherently linear, wide pressure range, easy to manufacture and calibrate.
- **Disadvantages of C-Bourdon:** Limited accuracy (±0.5% to ±2%), sensitive to vibration and temperature, not suitable for very low pressures, requires regular calibration, no electrical output (mechanical only).
- **Applications:** Boiler gauges, hydraulic systems, gas cylinders, oil and gas pipelines, process industries, safety relief valve monitoring.
- **Calibration Spring (Bourdon Spring):** A small helical spring on the pinion shaft that removes gear backlash and provides zero adjustment.
- **Overpressure Protection:** A solid stop is usually provided inside the case to limit the Bourdon tube's travel during overpressure, preventing permanent deformation or rupture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spiral and Helical Bourdon Pressure Sensors - Diaphragm or Capsule Type Sensors: Motion Balance and Force Balance Pressure Sensors - Differential Pressure Elements: Dry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT402 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spiral and Helical Bourdon Pressure Sensors - Diaphragm or Capsule Type Sensors: Motion Balance and Force Balance Pressure Sensors - Differential Pressure Elements: Dry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bourdon Tube Pressure Sensors — Spiral and Helical Variants

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Bourdon tube** is an elastic, curved, closed-end metallic tube of non-circular (typically elliptical or flattened circular) cross-section that converts an applied fluid pressure into a measurable mechanical displacement at its free (sealed) end. The **spiral** and **helical** configurations are geometrical extensions of the classic C-type Bourdon designed to amplify the tip displacement for a given pressure, thereby improving sensitivity, resolution, and reducing mechanical stress concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The Spiral and Helical Bourdon are explicitly listed as separate sub-topics. Students must remember the *deflection amplification relationship*: Spiral ≈ n × C-type, Helical ≈ n × Spiral (where n is the number of turns).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a flattened paper straw sealed at one end. If you blow into the open end, the straw tends to *uncoil and straighten* because internal pressure tries to restore the cross-section to a perfect circle. A Bourdon tube behaves identically — except it is metal, precision-machined, and the small "uncoiling" is precisely translated into a pointer deflection through a mechanical linkage (the **Bourdon tube pressure gauge mechanism**).

---

## 1.2 Diaphragm and Capsule Type Pressure Sensors

### Formal Definition
A **diaphragm sensor** uses a thin, flexible circular metallic or non-metallic plate (or a stack of plates called a **capsule**) that deflects when a pressure difference is applied across it. The deflection is proportional to the applied differential pressure and is transduced into an electrical signal via strain gauges, capacitive plates, or LVDTs.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture the rubber membrane in a stethoscope head. When sound pressure waves push the membrane, it bulges slightly. A pressure-sensing diaphragm does the same, but instead of sound, it responds to process pressure, and instead of sound waves coming out, an electrical signal comes out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Capsule vs Diaphragm:** A **diaphragm** is a single plate; a **capsule** is two diaphragms welded rim-to-rim forming a sealed chamber, effectively doubling the deflection per unit pressure.

---

## 1.3 Motion Balance and Force Balance Pressure Sensors

### Formal Definition
- **Motion Balance Sensor:** An *open-loop* pressure transducer in which the input pressure produces a small but finite deflection of the sensing element, and that motion itself is the measured variable (converted to an output by a displacement-to-signal transducer).
- **Force Balance Sensor:** A *closed-loop* transducer where the input pressure generates a force that is continuously opposed by an equal and opposite feedback force generated by an actuator. The error signal drives the actuator, and the feedback current/voltage becomes the calibrated output.

### Conceptual Analogy
- **Motion Balance** = A bathroom weighing scale. You step on it, the spring compresses, and the *amount of compression* directly indicates your weight.
- **Force Balance** = An elevator holding a constant floor position. A controller constantly pushes back against any disturbance to keep you stationary — the *effort the controller exerts* is the output, not the actual movement.

---

## 1.4 Differential Pressure Elements — Dry Type

### Formal Definition
A **Dry-Type Differential Pressure (DP) Element** measures the difference between two process pressures (high-side and low-side) using a dry sensing capsule — *no liquid fill or mercury manometric fluid* — typically employing force-balance or capacitive transduction.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Dry"** explicitly contrasts with **"Wet"** differential manometers (e.g., U-tube manometers using mercury or water). Dry elements use a metallic capsule, diaphragm, or bellows and convert DP into a pneumatic (3–15 psi) or electronic (4–20 mA) signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Dry DP elements are the workhorse of **flow measurement** (using orifice plates, Venturis, and pitot tubes) and of **level measurement** in pressurized vessels across the oil & gas, chemical, and power industries.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Key Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Output (Electronic) | **4–20 mA** | Industry-standard current loop |
| Standard Output (Pneumatic) | **3–15 psi** (20–100 kPa) | Legacy standard in oil/gas |
| Typical Bourdon Tube Material | **Phosphor Bronze, Beryllium Copper, SS 316** | Corrosion & fatigue resistance |
| Elastic Modulus (SS 316) | **~193 GPa** | Used in diaphragm deflection formulas |
| Poisson's Ratio (Steel) | **~0.30** | Used in flat plate diaphragm analysis |
| Capsule Displacement | **0.1 to 1.0 mm** typical | Magnified via linkage to pointer |
| Standard DP Ranges | **0–100 mbar to 0–100 bar** | Industry standard span |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Bourdon Tube (Spiral & Helical)

### Why does a Bourdon Tube Deflect?
A Bourdon tube has an **elliptical (or flattened) cross-section**. When internal pressure $P$ acts on the inner wall, the differential hoop stress is **higher on the minor axis** of the ellipse than on the major axis. This stress imbalance generates a net bending moment that tends to **circularize** the cross-section, causing the tube to **straighten** along its longitudinal axis.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Operation
1. Process fluid enters the inlet fitting and fills the hollow Bourdon element.
2. Internal pressure $P$ acts on the *inner* walls of the elliptical cross-section.
3. The non-uniform stress field attempts to make the cross-section circular.
4. The tube tip (sealed end) displaces — the **C-type** moves ~2–5 mm; the **spiral** moves proportionally more; the **helical** moves the most.
5. The tip motion is transmitted via a **linkage → sector gear → pinion → pointer** to provide a calibrated reading.
6. A hairspring (spiral spring) provides the restoring torque to eliminate backlash and pointer play.

### Spiral vs. Helical — Comparison

| Feature | C-Type | Spiral | Helical |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Single C-arc | Multiple turns in a single plane (co-planar) | Multiple turns along an axis (3D helix) |
| Tip Displacement | Small (base) | ≈ n × C-type | ≈ n × Spiral |
| Bulky? | No | Compact for range | Very compact for large ranges |
| Frequency Response | Good | Lower (more mass) | Lowest (most mass) |
| Typical Application | Gauges up to ~700 bar | Process gauges, recorders | High-accuracy gauges, low-pressure |

### Why Use Spiral/Helical?
- **Amplification of mechanical displacement** without increasing tube wall stress.
- **Reduced stress** per unit deflection → longer fatigue life.
- **Higher resolution** — small pressure changes produce observable pointer movement.
- **Compact installation** — multi-turn tubes fit in the same enclosure as a single C-type.

---

## 2.2 Diaphragm and Capsule Sensors — Theory

### Flat Diaphragm Deflection Theory
For a circular diaphragm of radius $a$, thickness $h$, clamped at the edges, subjected to a uniform differential pressure $P$, the **central deflection** $y_{\max}$ is given by:

$$
y_{\max} = \frac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E h^{3}}
$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Pa)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's Ratio
- $a$ = effective radius of diaphragm (m)
- $h$ = diaphragm thickness (m)
- $P$ = applied differential pressure (Pa)

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **cubic dependence on $h$** and **quartic dependence on $a$**. A small change in thickness produces a *huge* change in sensitivity — this is why diaphragms are typically very thin (0.05–0.5 mm) and strain-gauged for measurement.

### Capsule Construction
A **capsule** is formed by welding two corrugated diaphragms together at their rims, creating a sealed internal cavity (often evacuated or referenced to vacuum for absolute pressure measurement). For a capsule of $n$ elements, total deflection is approximately $n$ times the single-diaphragm deflection.

---

## 2.3 Motion Balance vs. Force Balance — Transfer Function Analysis

### Motion Balance (Open-Loop)
The input pressure produces a deflection $x$ of the sensing element (diaphragm/Bourdon). The transfer function is:

$$
x = K_1 \cdot P
$$

where $K_1$ is the mechanical compliance (m/Pa). The output signal (after LVDT/strain gauge) is:

$$
V_{out} = K_2 \cdot x = K_2 K_1 \cdot P
$$

**Limitations:** Non-linearity of the spring element, temperature drift of $K_1$, calibration drift.

### Force Balance (Closed-Loop)
The pressure generates a force $F_P = P \cdot A$ (where $A$ is the diaphragm area). This force is opposed by an electromagnetic (or pneumatic) feedback force $F_{FB} = K_m \cdot I$ (or $K_p \cdot P_{air}$). The error detector senses the small residual motion $\Delta x$ and drives the actuator.

Equilibrium condition:
$$
F_P = F_{FB} \Rightarrow P \cdot A = K_m \cdot I
$$

Solving for output current:
$$
I = \frac{A}{K_m} \cdot P
$$

**Advantages over Motion Balance:**
- Linearity is set by the feedback coil / magnet geometry, not the diaphragm's non-linear spring.
- **Hysteresis is reduced by ~90%** because the diaphragm barely moves.
- **Better temperature stability** and dynamic response.
- Used in **smart transmitters** (HART, Foundation Fieldbus).

---

## 2.4 Differential Pressure Elements — Dry Type Architecture

### Core Construction
A typical **dry-type DP cell** (e.g., Rosemount 1151 / Honeywell ST3000 style):

1. **Two process isolators** (diaphragms) — one on high-pressure side, one on low-pressure side.
2. **Silicone oil fill** between the isolator diaphragms and the central sensing diaphragm (the oil is *internal hydraulic transmission*, not a manometric fluid).
3. **Central sensing diaphragm** deflects in proportion to $\Delta P = P_H - P_L$.
4. **Transduction stage** — either:
   - **Capacitive:** Two capacitor plates move relative to a fixed plate → capacitance change.
   - **Force Balance:** Electromagnetic coil provides feedback → output current 4–20 mA.
   - **Strain Gauge:** Foil gauges bonded to sensing diaphragm → Wheatstone bridge output.

### Why "Dry"?
The sensing capsule contains **no free process fluid**; process fluids are isolated by welded diaphragms and the force is hydraulically transmitted through inert silicone oil. This eliminates:
- Mercury toxicity (compared to wet manometers).
- Fluid evaporation / freezing in manometer legs.
- Corrosion of the sensing element.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{F}{A} = P$ | Basic pressure definition | Pa |
| 2 | $y_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E h^{3}}$ | Flat diaphragm center deflection | m |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}}$ | Max bending stress at diaphragm edge | Pa |
| 4 | $F_P = P \cdot A$ | Pressure force on diaphragm | N |
| 5 | $F_{FB} = K_m \cdot I$ | Electromagnetic feedback force | N |
| 6 | $I_{out} = \dfrac{A}{K_m} \cdot P$ | Force-balance output current | A |
| 7 | $\Delta C = C_0 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$ | Capacitive DP cell output (small-signal) | F |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_n}$ | Natural period of capsule (dynamic) | s |
| 9 | $D_{tip} = n \cdot D_{C\text{-type}}$ | Tip deflection amplification (spiral/helical) | m |
| 10 | $V_{out} = K_2 K_1 P$ | Motion-balance transfer function | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always carry **units** in the substitution step of any derivation. Marks are explicitly awarded for dimensional consistency in Part B numericals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flat Diaphragm Central Deflection

We will derive the classical result for a **clamped-edge circular diaphragm** under uniform pressure $P$.

### Step 1: Setup the Problem
Consider a circular plate of radius $a$, thickness $h$, clamped at the periphery (i.e., no deflection, no rotation at $r = a$). The plate is isotropic with Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. A uniform pressure $P$ acts on one face.

### Step 2: Governing Plate Equation
For a thin plate ($h \ll a$), the deflection $w(r)$ is governed by:
$$
D \nabla^{4} w = P
$$
where the **flexural rigidity** $D$ is defined as:
$$
D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1 - \nu^{2})}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Solve
For axisymmetric loading, $\nabla^{4} w = \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr}\left\{ r \dfrac{d}{dr}\left[ \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr}\left( r \dfrac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\}$

After integration with boundary conditions $w(a) = 0$ and $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\big|_{r=a} = 0$:

$$
w(r) = \frac{3 P}{16 E h^{3}} (1 - \nu^{2}) \left( a^{2} - r^{2} \right)^{2}
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate at Center
At the center, $r = 0$:

$$
y_{\max} = w(0) = \frac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E h^{3}}
$$

### Step 5: Dimensional Check
- Numerator: $P \cdot a^{4}$ has units $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}^{4}$
- Denominator: $E \cdot h^{3}$ has units $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{m}^{3}$
- Ratio: $\text{m}$ ✅ (correct, deflection is in meters)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force Balance Sensor Steady-State Output

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram Setup
The process pressure $P$ acts on a diaphragm of effective area $A$, generating an input force:
$$
F_{in} = P \cdot A
$$

### Step 2: Feedback Force Generation
A coil carrying current $I$ in a permanent magnetic field $B$ (of length $L$ with $N$ turns) generates a Lorentz force:
$$
F_{FB} = N \cdot B \cdot L \cdot I = K_m \cdot I
$$
where $K_m = NBL$ is the **motor constant** (N/A).

### Step 3: Error Detection
A position sensor (LVDT or capacitive) detects a tiny residual displacement $\Delta x$ when the diaphragm is *not* exactly at its null position. This error signal $e(t) = K_s \cdot \Delta x$ drives a high-gain amplifier of gain $G$.

### Step 4: Loop Closure
The amplified error produces the coil current:
$$
I = G \cdot e = G K_s \Delta x
$$

### Step 5: Force Equilibrium at Steady State
At equilibrium, $F_{in} = F_{FB}$ (the diaphragm returns to its null position, so $\Delta x \to 0$):
$$
P \cdot A = K_m \cdot I
$$

### Step 6: Solve for Output Current
$$
I_{out} = \frac{A}{K_m} \cdot P
$$

### Step 7: Engineering Interpretation
The output current is **linear in pressure** — linearization comes from the **feedback loop itself**, not from the diaphragm's spring characteristic. This is the foundation of all modern smart pressure transmitters (HART, Foundation Fieldbus).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Motion vs. Force Balance

| Parameter | Motion Balance | Force Balance |
|---|---|---|
| Loop type | Open loop | Closed loop |
| Sensing element motion | Large (visible) | Microscopic (< 0.01 mm) |
| Linearity | Limited by spring non-linearity | Excellent (set by magnet/coil) |
| Hysteresis | ~0.5–1.0% of FS | ~0.05% of FS |
| Temperature drift | Higher | Lower (diaphragm is at null) |
| Overload protection | Limited (spring fatigues) | Inherent (null-seeking) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (electronics) |
| Typical accuracy | ±0.5% FS | ±0.075% FS (smart) |
| KTU exam weight | High | High |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A flat steel diaphragm has radius $a = 20$ mm, thickness $h = 0.5$ mm. The steel has $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$. Calculate (a) the central deflection for a differential pressure of 50 kPa, and (b) the maximum bending stress.

### Given:
- $a = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$
- $h = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $E = 200 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.3$
- $P = 50 \text{ kPa} = 5 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$

### (a) Central Deflection
$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{\max} &= \frac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E h^{3}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot (5 \times 10^{4}) \cdot (0.02)^{4} \cdot (1 - 0.09)}{16 \cdot (200 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^{3}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot 5 \times 10^{4} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.91}{16 \cdot 2 \times 10^{11} \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot 5 \cdot 1.6 \cdot 0.91 \times 10^{4-7}}{16 \cdot 2 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{11-10}} \\
&= \frac{21.84 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{0}} \\
&= 5.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.546 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Maximum Bending Stress
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\max} &= \frac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot (5 \times 10^{4}) \cdot (0.02)^{2}}{4 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot 5 \times 10^{4} \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 10^{0}}{10 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 6 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 60 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The deflection (0.546 mm) is in the practical range for strain-gauge or LVDT detection. The stress (60 MPa) is well below the yield strength of SS 316 (~240 MPa), confirming safe operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Force Balance Pressure Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Pressure P] --> D1[Diaphragm / Capsule]
    D1 --> F1[Force Fa equals P times A]
    F1 --> N1[Null Detector LVDT]
    N1 -->|error e| A1[High Gain Amplifier G]
    A1 -->|current I| C1[Coil in Magnetic Field]
    C1 -->|feedback force Ffb equals Km times I| F1
    A1 -->|output I equals A divided by Km times P| OUT1[Output 4 to 20 mA Signal]
    P2[Reference Pressure] --> D1
    style F1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT1 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Spiral vs. Helical Bourdon Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Bourdon Tube Family] --> B1[C Type Single Arc]
    A0 --> B2[Spiral Type]
    A0 --> B3[Helical Type]
    B2 --> C1[Multiple Coils in Same Plane]
    B2 --> C2[Compact for Medium Range]
    B2 --> C3[Deflection equals n times C type]
    B3 --> D1[Multiple Coils along Axis 3D Helix]
    B3 --> D2[Highest Displacement Amplification]
    B3 --> D3[Used in High Accuracy Gauges]
    style A0 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B3 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Dry-Type Differential Pressure Cell — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HP[High Pressure Side]
        PH[Process Fluid HP]
        DH[Isolator Diaphragm H]
    end
    subgraph LP[Low Pressure Side]
        PL[Process Fluid LP]
        DL[Isolator Diaphragm L]
    end
    DH -->|Silicone Oil Fill| CM[Central Sensing Diaphragm]
    DL -->|Silicone Oil Fill| CM
    CM -->|Mechanical Displacement| TR[Transduction Stage]
    TR --> T1[Capacitive Pickoff]
    TR --> T2[Strain Gauge Bridge]
    TR --> T3[Force Balance Coil]
    T1 --> OUT2[4 to 20 mA plus HART]
    T2 --> OUT2
    T3 --> OUT2
    style CM fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TR fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT2 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Capsule Construction (Two-Diaphragm Element)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAP[Capsule Assembly] --> W1[Weld Rim 1]
    CAP --> D2[Diaphragm 2]
    CAP --> D1[Diaphragm 1]
    CAP --> W2[Weld Rim 2]
    CAP --> CV[Sealed Cavity Vacuum or Reference]
    D1 -->|Deflection y1| CV
    D2 -->|Deflection y2| CV
    CV -->|Total Deflection y equals y1 plus y2| TR4[To LVDT or Strain Gauge]
    style CAP fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style CV fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TR4 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.5 Bourdon Gauge Mechanical Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1B[Bourdon Tube Tip] --> L1[Linkage Rod]
    L1 --> S1[Sector Gear]
    S1 --> P1[Pinon Gear]
    P1 --> PT[Pointer]
    PT --> SCL[Calibrated Scale]
    H1[Hair Spring] --> S1
    IN1[Pressure Inlet] --> T1B
    style T1B fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style PT fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
