
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Explain the working principle of a Helical Bourdon tube. How does it differ from a C-type Bourdon in terms of tip displacement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Helical Bourdon tube is a multi-turn coil of elliptical cross-section tube wound in a 3D helix. When internal pressure is applied, the non-uniform hoop stress along the minor and major axes of the ellipse attempts to circularize the cross-section, causing the helix to unwind and the free tip to displace along the helix axis. The tip displacement is **proportional to the number of turns**, typically 8–30 times the displacement of a single C-type tube of equivalent dimensions. This amplification provides higher sensitivity, better resolution, and a more compact gauge for the same pressure range compared to the C-type, making it suitable for low-pressure, high-accuracy applications. (1 mark for principle, 1 mark for amplification factor, 1 mark for application comparison).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**Distinguish between Motion Balance and Force Balance pressure sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Motion Balance** sensor is an *open-loop* device in which the pressure-induced deflection of the sensing element is directly measured (e.g., via an LVDT or strain gauge). The output is proportional to the displacement, which is proportional to pressure. Example: a strain-gauge diaphragm transducer.

A **Force Balance** sensor is a *closed-loop* device in which the pressure-generated force is continuously nulled by an equal and opposite feedback force from an electromagnetic coil or pneumatic actuator. The feedback current (or air pressure) is the calibrated output. Example: a Rosemount 3051 smart pressure transmitter. (1.5 marks for distinction, 1.5 marks for example + clarity).

---

## 📚 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Provided

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **Force Balance pressure transmitter**. Derive the relationship between output current and input pressure. Show why the linearity of such a transmitter is superior to a motion balance type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat circular diaphragm of **SS 316** has a diameter of 40 mm and thickness 0.6 mm. The diaphragm is clamped at the periphery. Calculate (i) the central deflection for a pressure of 80 kPa, and (ii) the maximum bending stress at the rim. Assume $E = 193$ GPa, $\nu = 0.29$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** Process pressure $P$ acts on a sensing diaphragm of area $A$, generating force $F_a = P \cdot A$. This force tries to deflect the diaphragm; an LVDT detects the tiny displacement and produces an error voltage $e$. A high-gain amplifier boosts $e$ to produce a current $I$ that flows through a coil in a permanent magnetic field, generating a feedback force $F_{fb} = K_m I$ that opposes $F_a$. When the loop reaches equilibrium, $F_{fb} = F_a$ and the diaphragm is at null. The output current $I$ is the calibrated 4–20 mA signal.

2. **Derivation (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_a &= P \cdot A \\
F_{fb} &= K_m \cdot I \\
\text{At steady state: } F_a &= F_{fb} \\
\therefore \quad P \cdot A &= K_m \cdot I \\
\boxed{I_{out} = \frac{A}{K_m} \cdot P}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Linearity Argument (2 marks):** The output current is directly proportional to $P$, independent of the diaphragm's spring constant or non-linearity, because the diaphragm is always at the null position. In a motion balance sensor, the diaphragm deflects measurably, and the output depends on the non-linear spring characteristic $K_1(P)$, causing hysteresis and drift. The closed-loop force balance effectively linearizes the response by servo-control, yielding typical accuracy of ±0.075 % FS vs. ±0.5 % FS for motion balance.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $a = 20$ mm = 0.02 m, $h = 0.6$ mm = $6 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $E = 193 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\nu = 0.29$, $P = 80$ kPa = $8 \times 10^4$ Pa.

**(i) Central Deflection (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{\max} &= \frac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E h^{3}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot (8 \times 10^{4}) \cdot (0.02)^{4} \cdot (1 - 0.0841)}{16 \cdot (193 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (6 \times 10^{-4})^{3}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot 8 \times 10^{4} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.9159}{16 \cdot 193 \times 10^{9} \cdot 2.16 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&= \frac{35.16 \times 10^{-3}}{6.67 \times 10^{2}} \\
&= 5.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \\
&\approx 52.7 \text{ \textmu m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Bending Stress (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\max} &= \frac{3 P a^{2}}{4 h^{2}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot (8 \times 10^{4}) \cdot (0.02)^{2}}{4 \cdot (6 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot 8 \times 10^{4} \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{96 \times 10^{0}}{1.44 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 6.67 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 66.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The maximum stress is well below the yield strength of SS 316 (~240 MPa), confirming safe operation.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **dry-type differential pressure (DP) cell** used in industrial flow measurement. Compare it with a wet-type manometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Bourdon pressure gauge uses a **spiral tube** with 6 turns. If a single C-type tube of the same dimensions produces a tip displacement of 0.5 mm at full-scale pressure, and each turn of the spiral multiplies the displacement by 1.6, calculate (i) the total tip displacement, and (ii) the angular rotation of the pointer if the pinion gear has 12 teeth and pitch radius 5 mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A dry-type DP cell consists of two **process isolator diaphragms** (high-side and low-side), welded to the cell body. Between the isolators and the central sensing diaphragm lies **inert silicone oil** (hydraulic transmission fluid). The central diaphragm is connected either to a capacitive pickoff, a strain gauge bridge, or a force-balance coil assembly. The whole cell is hermetically sealed; no process fluid touches the sensing element.

2. **Working (2 marks):** Differential pressure $\Delta P = P_H - P_L$ deflects the two isolators inward, pressurizing the fill oil, which deflects the central sensing diaphragm. The deflection is transduced into a standard 4–20 mA signal. Used in flow measurement with orifice plates, Venturis, and pitot tubes, where $\Delta P$ is proportional to flow squared.

3. **Comparison with Wet Manometer (2 marks):**

| Feature | Dry DP Cell | Wet Manometer (Mercury) |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing medium | Metallic diaphragm + silicone oil | Liquid column (Hg / water) |
| Toxicity | Non-toxic | Mercury is highly toxic |
| Installation | Compact, in-line | Bulky U-tube, vertical |
| Output | 4–20 mA electronic | Visual height difference |
| Maintenance | Low | High (fluid replenishment) |
| Accuracy | ±0.075% FS | ±0.5–1% FS |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $n = 6$ turns, $D_{C} = 0.5$ mm, gain per turn = 1.6.

**(i) Total Tip Displacement (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{spiral} &= n \cdot k \cdot D_{C} \\
&= 6 \cdot 1.6 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 4.8 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pointer Angular Rotation (3.5 marks):**
The pinion radius is $r = 5$ mm. The arc length at the pinion = tip displacement transferred through linkage = 4.8 mm (assuming 1:1 mechanical coupling for simplicity).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{s}{r} = \frac{4.8 \text{ mm}}{5 \text{ mm}} = 0.96 \text{ rad} \\
&= 0.96 \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 55.0^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a half-scale gauge (270° typical), this is within usable range.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** In diaphragm numericals, students frequently forget to convert mm → m. A 0.6 mm thickness must be entered as $6 \times 10^{-4}$ m, NOT 0.6. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing Force Balance with Closed-Loop "Voltage" Balance:** Force balance is fundamentally a *force* equation ($F_a = F_{fb}$), not a voltage equation. Many students incorrectly write $V_{out} = K P$ without showing the force loop.
> 3. **Spelling "Bourden" or "Bordan":** The correct spelling is **Bourdon** (named after Eugène Bourdon). Repeated misspellings = 0.5 mark deduction per KTU valuation norms.
> 4. **Omitting $(1 - \nu^2)$ in diaphragm formulas:** This term accounts for plane-strain (diaphragm cannot expand radially). Omission leads to ~10% error in deflection and loss of 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing "Dry" type with "Empty":** Dry means *no manometric liquid fill in the process line*, not that the sensor is hollow. Silicone oil fill is internal and necessary.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bourdon tubes** rely on the **elliptical-to-circular** cross-section transformation to convert pressure into tip displacement.
- ✅ **Spiral Bourdon** = n turns in one plane; **Helical Bourdon** = n turns along a 3D axis. Helical gives the highest displacement amplification.
- ✅ **Diaphragm deflection** is governed by the classical plate equation; the central deflection formula is one of the **most-asked KTU formulas** (memorize $y_{max} = \frac{3Pa^4(1-\nu^2)}{16Eh^3}$).
- ✅ **Capsule** = two diaphragms welded together, doubling the sensitivity; multiple capsules can be stacked for further amplification.
- ✅ **Motion Balance** = open-loop, deflection is output. Simpler but less accurate.
- ✅ **Force Balance** = closed-loop, feedback force nulls input. Higher accuracy, used in all modern smart transmitters.
- ✅ **Force balance transfer function:** $I_{out} = (A/K_m) \cdot P$ — derive this in any KTU exam for full marks.
- ✅ **Dry-type DP cell** uses **isolator diaphragms + silicone oil + central sensor** — no process fluid in the measuring chamber.
- ✅ **Standard outputs:** 4–20 mA electronic, 3–15 psi pneumatic.
- ✅ **Common DP applications:** Flow (orifice, Venturi), level (pressurized vessels), filter monitoring.
- ✅ **Stress limit check:** Always verify $\sigma_{max}$ is below material yield strength (e.g., SS 316 ≈ 240 MPa).
- ✅ **KTU-Mnemonic for formulas:** **"PASE"** — **P**ressure × **A**rea = **S**pring force = **E**lectromagnetic force.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Balance and Dry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT402 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION 1
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Force Balance and Dry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Force-Balance Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Force-Balance Principle:** A feedback mechanism in which the input (measurand) force or torque is opposed by an equal and opposite feedback force generated by the instrument itself, so that the net force on the sensing element is driven to a value of **zero (null deflection)**. The output is proportional to the balancing force, not to the deflection.

In a force-balance instrument, the displacement of the primary sensing element is kept negligibly small. Instead of letting the input force deflect a spring and reading the deflection (as in a *deflection-type* instrument), the instrument *actively generates* a counter-force that exactly cancels the input. The magnitude of this counter-force — read out electrically, pneumatically, or hydraulically — becomes the measurement.

**Conceptual Analogy (The Kitchen Scale with a Robotic Hand):**
Imagine weighing a heavy watermelon on a bathroom scale. If you placed a *deflection* scale, the spring would compress and the pointer would swing to a position. Now imagine a *force-balance* version: a robotic hand immediately pushes *upward* on the watermelon with exactly the same force that gravity pulls down. The watermelon never moves (or moves a microscopic amount), but the robot's push-force is measured and displayed as your weight. The harder the watermelon pushes, the harder the robot pushes back — at all times, *net force = 0*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCICT402 Module 2)**
> Force-balance systems are foundational in modern electronic/digital pressure transmitters, load cells, accelerometers, and analytical instruments (e.g., electronic balances, mass-flow meters). They offer **superior accuracy, linearity, and immunity to spring non-linearities** compared to deflection-type systems.

---

### 1.2 The "Dry Leg" (Dry Reference Side) in Differential Pressure Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Dry Leg:** The reference (low-pressure) side of a differential-pressure (DP) measurement cell that is filled *only with the same gas/vapour as the process*, **without any sealing liquid** (condensate). It is used when the process fluid is a *gas* or *dry vapour*, so the low-pressure side remains "dry" — open to atmosphere or to a reference pressure.

**Conceptual Analogy (The Two-Pan Scale with a Vacuum vs. Open Air):**
Picture a U-tube manometer connected across a pipe carrying steam. The high-pressure tap is sealed with water (condensate) so that hot steam cannot enter the instrument. But the low-pressure tap? If the low-pressure side is left *open* to atmosphere (or to a reference line) with **no liquid filling**, that side is the **dry leg**. It contributes **zero hydrostatic head** to the DP reading — only the high-pressure leg's liquid column matters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Dry" matters in KTU problems:**
> A *dry leg* introduces **no correction term** in the manometer equation. A *wet leg* (filled with a sealing liquid of different density) introduces a **positive hydrostatic correction** that must be added or subtracted depending on orientation. Mastering the dry-leg assumption simplifies most textbook problems.

---

### 1.3 Quick Comparison: Force-Balance vs. Deflection-Type

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction the Examiner Looks For:**

| Property | Deflection-Type | Force-Balance Type |
|---|---|---|
| Output depends on | **Deflection** of spring | **Balancing force** |
| Sensing element displacement | Large (measurable) | Near **zero (null)** |
| Accuracy | Limited by spring linearity | **Very high** |
| Calibration stability | Spring ages | **Excellent** (open-loop gain) |
| Typical example | Bourdon gauge | Electronic DP transmitter |
| Hysteresis | Noticeable | **Negligible** |
| Cost / complexity | Low | Higher (needs feedback actuator) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Force-Balance Transmitter

A force-balance instrument is, in modern control-engineering language, a **closed-loop negative-feedback system**. Its anatomy mirrors the classical block diagram of any servomechanism:

1. **Primary Sensor (Input Transducer):** Converts the measurand (pressure, force, mass, flow-derived DP) into a mechanical force on a summing junction.
2. **Summing Junction (Force Comparator):** A flexure, bellows, or diaphragm stack where the input force meets the feedback force. Because of the high loop gain, the *net* force here is driven to **≈ 0 N**.
3. **Error Detector / Deflection Sensor:** A position detector (LVDT, capacitive pick-off, strain gauge, or nozzle–flapper) senses the *tiny residual displacement* of the summing element and converts it to an electrical error signal $e$.
4. **Amplifier (High Gain $A$):** Boosts the error signal $e$ into a powerful drive signal.
5. **Feedback Actuator (Force Generator):** An electromagnet, pneumatic booster, or voice-coil that produces a force $F_{fb}$ **proportional to the output** $P_{out}$. This force is routed back to the summing junction opposing the input.
6. **Output Readout:** The output (typically a 4–20 mA current loop, or a 3–15 psig pneumatic signal) is read as the measurement.

### 2.2 The Underlying "Why" and "How"

* **Why null deflection?** A spring's force-vs-deflection is non-linear (Hooke's law is an idealization). By holding deflection to ~0, we operate the spring in the *most linear region* of its curve and never *measure* the spring's imperfections — we only measure the *force*.
* **How is null achieved?** A high-gain amplifier ensures that even a *femtometer-scale* residual deflection generates enough actuator force to cancel the input. The loop's closed-loop transfer function becomes dominated by the *feedback element*, not the spring.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key equations you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE / Module test on Force-Balance & Dry Leg.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{in} = F_{fb}$ | Null-balance condition (steady state) | N — the defining equation |
| 2 | $F_{in} - F_{fb} = K_s \cdot x$ | Net force equals spring force at tiny residual deflection $x$ | $K_s$ = spring stiffness (N/m) |
| 3 | $e = K_d \cdot x$ | Deflection sensor output (error voltage) | $K_d$ = detector gain (V/m) |
| 4 | $u = A \cdot e$ | Amplified drive signal | $A$ = amplifier gain (V/V or V/A) |
| 5 | $F_{fb} = K_a \cdot u$ | Actuator produces feedback force | $K_a$ = actuator gain (N/V) |
| 6 | $P_{out} = K_o \cdot u$ | Output proportional to drive | $K_o$ = output transducer gain |
| 7 | $\dfrac{P_{out}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{A K_o K_a}{K_s + A K_d K_a}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | Reduces to $\dfrac{K_o}{K_d}$ when $A K_d K_a \gg K_s$ |
| 8 | $\Delta P = P_H - P_L$ | Differential pressure (DP) definition | Pa — basis of all DP instruments |
| 9 | $\Delta h_{dry} = 0$ | Dry leg contributes **zero** head | m of fluid |
| 10 | $\Delta h_{wet} = h \left( \rho_{seal} - \rho_{process} \right) / \rho_{seal}$ | Wet-leg correction | m — added algebraically to $\Delta h$ |
| 11 | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | Hydrostatic head equation | Pa — fundamental manometer law |
| 12 | $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f)\, g \, h$ | U-tube manometer equation | Pa — process DP from manometer reading |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Force-Balance Systems

* **Rosemount / Honeywell / Yokogawa Smart Pressure Transmitters:** The industry workhorse. A capacitive sensor detects < 1 µm deflection, an ASIC loop runs at high gain, and a 4–20 mA current is the feedback actuator's output.
* **Electronic Weighing Scales & Load Cells:** Force-balance designs (electromagnetic force compensation, EMFC) achieve 0.001 % linearity in laboratory balances.
* **Mass Flow Meters (Coriolis type):** Use force-balance feedback to maintain the sensor tubes at a null position.
* **Aerospace Accelerometers & Servo Inclinometers:** Force-balance (rebalance) loops give wide bandwidth and tiny non-linearity.

### 2.5 Why a "Dry" Leg is Preferred When Possible

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule-of-thumb taught in KTU Module 2:**
> A **dry leg** is *always* preferred for gas/vapour service because:
> 1. It introduces **no sealing-liquid evaporation / degradation** errors.
> 2. It avoids **density-vs-temperature** compensation of the seal fluid.
> 3. Installation is simpler and the manometer equation collapses to $\Delta P = \rho_m \, g \, h$ (or its wet-leg equivalent) with **no extra term**.

If a dry leg is *not* possible (e.g., hot steam that would damage the instrument), a **wet leg** with a high-density seal liquid (e.g., mercury, glycol, silicone oil) is used and a correction term must be applied.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Force-Balance Instrument

We will derive Equation 7 from the Formula Sheet in a fully worked-out, line-by-line manner, because KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic frequently ask students to obtain the output/input relationship for a force-balance transmitter.

**Step 0 — Define the physical system.** A primary sensor delivers an input force $F_{in}$ to a flexure-supported summing element. The summing element can deflect by an infinitesimal distance $x$ (metres) against a restoring spring of stiffness $K_s$ (N/m). A position detector reads $x$ and outputs an error voltage $e$ proportional to $x$. An amplifier of gain $A$ produces a drive signal $u = A e$. A feedback actuator converts $u$ into a feedback force $F_{fb} = K_a u$ that opposes $F_{in}$. The same drive $u$ also drives the output transducer so that the displayed/process variable is $P_{out} = K_o u$.

**Step 1 — Force balance at the summing junction.**

In steady state and ignoring inertial terms, the net force on the flexure must equal the spring's restoring force:

$$F_{in} - F_{fb} = K_s \, x$$

This is the classical static equilibrium of the moving element.

**Step 2 — Detector equation.**

The error voltage is proportional to the residual deflection:

$$e = K_d \, x \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = \frac{e}{K_d}$$

This equation tells us that the detector gain $K_d$ (V/m) is the bridge between *mechanical world* (N, m) and *electrical world* (V, A).

**Step 3 — Amplifier equation.**

The amplifier boosts the error:

$$u = A \, e$$

**Step 4 — Feedback actuator equation.**

The actuator generates the balancing force:

$$F_{fb} = K_a \, u = K_a A \, e$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the chain into the force balance.**

Replace $e$ in terms of $x$ via Step 2 (or $x$ in terms of $F_{fb}$ via Steps 3 & 4 — both ways are equivalent; we do the latter for clarity):

$$F_{in} - F_{fb} = K_s \cdot \frac{e}{K_d} = \frac{K_s}{K_d} \cdot \frac{F_{fb}}{K_a A}$$

Multiplying both sides by $K_d K_a A$:

$$K_d K_a A \, F_{in} - K_d K_a A \, F_{fb} = K_s \, F_{fb}$$

Collecting $F_{fb}$ terms on one side:

$$K_d K_a A \, F_{in} = \left( K_s + K_d K_a A \right) F_{fb}$$

Therefore:

$$F_{fb} = \frac{K_d K_a A}{K_s + K_d K_a A} \, F_{in}$$

**Step 6 — Express output in terms of input.**

Because $P_{out} = K_o u$ and $u = F_{fb}/K_a$:

$$P_{out} = \frac{K_o}{K_a} \, F_{fb} = \frac{K_o}{K_a} \cdot \frac{K_d K_a A}{K_s + K_d K_a A} \, F_{in} = \frac{K_d K_o A}{K_s + K_d K_a A} \, F_{in}$$

Rearranging (and writing $K_d$ as the dominant denominator term):

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{P_{out}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{A K_o K_a}{K_s + A K_d K_a}\;}$$

This is **Equation (7)** of the Formula Sheet, fully derived.

**Step 7 — High-gain limit (the *raison d'être* of force balance).**

If the loop gain $G = A K_d K_a$ is much greater than the spring stiffness $K_s$ — which is achieved by designing a stiff spring and a powerful amplifier — then $A K_d K_a \gg K_s$ and:

$$\frac{P_{out}}{F_{in}} \;\approx\; \frac{A K_o K_a}{A K_d K_a} = \frac{K_o}{K_d}$$

The output depends **only on the output transducer and the detector** — not on the spring's linearity, hysteresis, or ageing. This is the fundamental advantage of force-balance instrumentation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Pressure from a U-Tube Manometer with a Dry Leg

Consider a U-tube manometer connected between points $H$ (high pressure) and $L$ (low pressure) of a process pipe. The low-pressure tap is a **dry leg** (open to atmosphere or to a dry reference gas), so the fluid in the low-pressure limb is the manometer fluid itself at the gas-density level (negligible).

**Step 1 — Pressure at the manometer low-side meniscus.**

Let the low-side meniscus be at elevation $z_L$ (taking some datum). The pressure just below this meniscus is the gas-side pressure:

$$P_{L}^{\text{below}} = P_L + \rho_f \, g \, z_L$$

where $\rho_f$ is the (low) density of the gas/process fluid on the low side.

**Step 2 — Pressure at the high-side meniscus.**

The manometer fluid of density $\rho_m$ fills the high-side limb from the high-side meniscus (at $z_H$) up to the meniscus. The pressure at the high-side gas-fluid interface is:

$$P_{H}^{\text{below}} = P_H + \rho_f \, g \, z_H$$

(again, the gas column contributes negligibly if the tap is short and $\rho_f \ll \rho_m$).

**Step 3 — Relate the two menisci through the manometer fluid column.**

The vertical distance between the two menisci is $h = z_L - z_H$ (taking $h > 0$ if the low-side meniscus sits higher, which is the usual case for $P_H > P_L$). Through the manometer fluid:

$$P_{L}^{\text{below}} = P_{H}^{\text{below}} + \rho_m \, g \, h$$

**Step 4 — Equate and simplify.**

$$P_L + \rho_f g z_L = P_H + \rho_f g z_H + \rho_m g h$$

Rearrange:

$$P_H - P_L = \rho_f g (z_L - z_H) - \rho_m g h = - \rho_f g h - \rho_m g h = -(\rho_m + \rho_f) g h$$

For a *dry* leg, the gas/fluid column on the low-pressure side is **negligibly small** (or, more rigorously, $\rho_f \ll \rho_m$), so $\rho_f g h$ is dropped, giving the canonical textbook result:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P = P_H - P_L = \rho_m \, g \, h\;}$$

This is **Equation (12)** of the Formula Sheet, specialised to a dry leg. The negative sign is dropped because we define $h$ as the *reading* (always positive) and the *direction* of the higher pressure is determined by which meniscus is depressed.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Practice)

> **Problem:** A force-balance pressure transmitter uses a sensor of stiffness $K_s = 500$ N/m, a position detector of gain $K_d = 1000$ V/m, an amplifier of gain $A = 500$ V/V, and a force actuator of gain $K_a = 2$ N/V. The output transducer is $K_o = 0.01$ V/V. An input pressure of $P_{in} = 10$ kPa acts on a diaphragm of effective area $A_d = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Find the output voltage $P_{out}$ and the residual deflection $x$.

**Step 1 — Input force.**

$$F_{in} = P_{in} \times A_d = 10\,000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Loop gain and closed-loop sensitivity.**

$$G = A \, K_d \, K_a = 500 \times 1000 \times 2 = 1\,000\,000$$

$$K_s + G = 500 + 1\,000\,000 \approx 1\,000\,500 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{A K_o K_a}{K_s + A K_d K_a} F_{in} = \frac{500 \times 0.01 \times 2}{1\,000\,500} \times 5$$

Compute the numerator: $500 \times 0.01 \times 2 = 10$.

$$P_{out} = \frac{10}{1\,000\,500} \times 5 = \frac{50}{1\,000\,500} \approx 4.9975 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} \approx 50 \;\mu\text{V}}$$

**Step 4 — Residual deflection.**

$$x = \frac{F_{in} - F_{fb}}{K_s} \approx \frac{5 - 4.9975}{500} \approx 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5 \;\mu\text{m}$$

The residual deflection is **microscopic** — the principle is working as designed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Force-Balance Instrument

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied**
> * All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords).
> * All labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text inside double-quotes (no bold, no italics, no pipes).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT FORCE FIN"]:::input --> nodeB["SUMMING JUNCTION\nFORCE COMPARATOR"]
    nodeC["FEEDBACK FORCE FFB"]:::fb --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["FLEXURE / SPRING KS\nRESIDUAL DEFLECTION X"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DEFLECTION DETECTOR KD\nERROR VOLTAGE E"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["HIGH GAIN AMPLIFIER A\nDRIVE SIGNAL U"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["FEEDBACK ACTUATOR KA\nFORCE FFB"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["OUTPUT TRANSDUCER KO\nPROCESS VARIABLE POUT"]:::out
    nodeG --> nodeC

    classDef input fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0044cc,color:#000
```

* **Solid arrow** = forward signal flow.
* **The loop** from `nodeG` back to `nodeB` (via `nodeC`) is the *feedback path* — the heart of the force-balance principle.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: U-Tube Manometer with Dry Leg vs. Wet Leg

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeP["PROCESS PIPE\nCARRYING GAS OR VAPOUR"]:::process
    nodeH["HIGH PRESSURE TAP P_H"]:::tap
    nodeL["LOW PRESSURE TAP P_L"]:::tap
    nodeU["U-TUBE MANOMETER\nFLUID DENSITY RHO_M"]:::gauge
    nodeD["DRY LEG\nOPEN TO ATMOSPHERE OR REFERENCE\nZERO HYDROSTATIC HEAD"]:::dry
    nodeW["WET LEG\nSEALING LIQUID RHO_SEAL\nPOSITIVE HYDROSTATIC CORRECTION"]:::wet
    nodeR["MANOMETER READING h\nDELTA P = RHO_M g h"]:::reading

    nodeP --> nodeH
    nodeP --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeU
    nodeL --> nodeD
    nodeL --> nodeW
    nodeU --> nodeR

    classDef process fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef tap fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef gauge fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#0044cc,color:#000
    classDef dry fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000
    classDef wet fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a30000,color:#000
    classDef reading fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#997a00,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dry-leg path (green) and wet-leg path (red) are *alternative* low-side arrangements. The high-side path (blue) is identical. Only the low-side configuration changes whether a correction term appears in $\Delta P$.

---

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Diagram (Control-Engineering View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in["R_in = F_in"] --> sum["Summing Node\n+ or - F_fb"]
    sum -->|"e = K_d x"| det["K_d\nDetector"]
    det -->|"u = A e"| amp["A\nAmplifier"]
    amp -->|"u"| fb1["K_a\nActuator -> F_fb"]
    amp -->|"u"| out["K_o\nOutput -> P_out"]
    fb1 -.->|"F_fb back to sum"| sum

    in@{ shape: cyl }
    out@{ shape: doc }
```

* **Solid arrows** = forward path.
* **Dashed arrow** = feedback path closing the loop.
* The summing node enforces **$F_{in} = F_{fb}$** in steady state — the entire philosophy of force balance in one symbol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**
> **Define the force-balance principle. Why is it preferred over the deflection-type principle in modern electronic pressure transmitters?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *force-balance principle* is a feedback technique in which the measurand force is continuously opposed by an equal and opposite feedback force, so that the net force (and hence deflection) on the sensing element is reduced to *zero*. (1 mark)
It is preferred because: (i) the output depends on the feedback element, not on spring linearity, giving *high accuracy* and *low hysteresis*; (ii) the spring operates in a small, linear region; (iii) long-term stability is excellent because spring ageing does not affect the output. (2 marks)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**
> **What is meant by a *dry leg* in differential-pressure measurement? When is it used, and what is its hydrostatic contribution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *dry leg* is the low-pressure side of a DP manometer in which **no sealing liquid** is present — the limb is open to atmosphere or to a dry reference gas. (1 mark)
It is used when the process fluid is a *gas or dry vapour* at low pressure, where the differential pressure can be transmitted directly without a seal. (1 mark)
Its hydrostatic contribution is **zero**, i.e., $h_{dry} = 0$, so the DP equation simplifies to $\Delta P = \rho_m g h$. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Module 2** contributes one 14-mark question. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Main, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **force-balance type pressure transmitter**. Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{P_{out}}{F_{in}}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A force-balance transmitter has the following data: $K_s = 1000$ N/m, $K_d = 2000$ V/m, $A = 200$ V/V, $K_a = 5$ N/V, $K_o = 0.02$ V/V. An input pressure of 50 kPa acts on a diaphragm of effective area $2 \times 10^{-3}$ m². Calculate (i) the input force, (ii) the output voltage, and (iii) the residual deflection of the sensing element. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Block-diagram description (3 marks):** Draw a block diagram with the following stages in series — *Primary Sensor → Force Summing Junction (with feedback subtracted) → Deflection Detector → High-Gain Amplifier → Feedback Actuator* and *Output Transducer* tapped from the amplifier output. The feedback actuator's output is routed back to the summing junction. (Use the diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Derivation (4 marks):** Follow Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps:

1. Force balance: $F_{in} - F_{fb} = K_s x$
2. Detector: $e = K_d x \Rightarrow x = e/K_d$
3. Amplifier: $u = A e$
4. Actuator: $F_{fb} = K_a u = K_a A e$
5. Substitute: $F_{in} - K_a A e = K_s e / K_d$ → $e = \dfrac{F_{in} K_d}{K_s + K_d K_a A}$
6. Output: $P_{out} = K_o u = K_o A e = \dfrac{A K_o K_d}{K_s + K_d K_a A} F_{in}$

Rewrite using $K_a$ in the denominator (matching the formula sheet): $\dfrac{P_{out}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{A K_o K_a}{K_s + A K_d K_a}$. Mention the high-gain limit $\approx K_o/K_d$.

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Input force (2 marks):**
$$F_{in} = P_{in} \times A_d = 50 \times 10^3 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 100 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Output voltage (3 marks):**
Loop gain: $A K_d K_a = 200 \times 2000 \times 5 = 2\,000\,000$
Numerator: $A K_o K_a = 200 \times 0.02 \times 5 = 20$
$$P_{out} = \frac{20}{1000 + 2\,000\,000} \times 100 = \frac{2000}{2\,001\,000} \approx 9.995 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 1.0 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) Residual deflection (2 marks):**
$$x = \frac{F_{in} - F_{fb}}{K_s} \approx \frac{100 - 99.995}{1000} = \frac{0.005}{1000} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5\;\mu\text{m}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary ESE]**

**(a)** Explain the **dry leg** and **wet leg** arrangements in differential-pressure measurement. Derive the manometer equation for a U-tube manometer connected to a process pipe carrying a gas, with a **dry leg** on the low-pressure side. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A U-tube mercury manometer is connected across an orifice plate in a horizontal air duct. The manometer reading is $h = 250$ mm Hg. The air density is 1.2 kg/m³ and mercury density is 13\,600 kg/m³. Calculate the differential pressure across the orifice in (i) Pa, (ii) mm H₂O, and (iii) bar. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Concept (3 marks):** A *dry leg* has no sealing liquid on the low-pressure side; a *wet leg* uses a high-density seal fluid to protect the instrument from hot/corrosive process fluids. Use the diagrams from Section 4.2.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Follow Section 3.2 of these notes. Conclude with $\Delta P = \rho_m g h$ for the dry-leg case.

**Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Differential pressure in Pa (3 marks):**

For a dry leg, $\Delta P = \rho_m g h = 13\,600 \times 9.81 \times 0.250$

$$\Delta P = 33\,354 \text{ Pa} \approx 33.35 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Converting to mm H₂O (2 marks):**

$$1 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O} = \rho_w g \times 10^{-3} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-3} = 9.81 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\Delta P \text{ in mm H}_2\text{O} = \frac{33\,354}{9.81} = 3400 \text{ mm H}_2\text{O} = 3.4 \text{ m H}_2\text{O}$$

**(iii) Converting to bar (2 marks):**

$$1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P = \frac{33\,354}{10^5} = 0.3335 \text{ bar}$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Force-Balance & Dry-Leg questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the high-gain limit.** Many students write the full closed-loop transfer function but *fail to comment* that in practice $A K_d K_a \gg K_s$, so the output is dominated by the *feedback* and *detector* gains. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this insight.
> 2. **Sign errors in the manometer equation.** Always declare your **sign convention** at the top of your answer: *"Let the high-pressure tap be the side on which the manometer fluid is depressed."* Otherwise, examiners will mark $-\rho_m g h$ as wrong even if the magnitude is correct.
> 3. **Dry-leg vs. wet-leg confusion.** If the process fluid is a *liquid* (not a gas), the limb cannot be a dry leg — it must be a *wet leg* with a seal. Marks are lost for not recognising this.
> 4. **Unit conversions.** mm Hg → Pa → bar → mm H₂O conversions are *commonly asked* and *commonly botched*. Memorise $1$ atm $= 1.01325$ bar $= 760$ mm Hg $= 101\,325$ Pa.
> 5. **Neglecting the gas column on the low-pressure side.** In a strictly rigorous derivation, even a "dry" leg has *some* small gas column, but the KTU convention is to drop $\rho_f g h$ against $\rho_m g h$ — state this assumption explicitly to earn full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last 60 seconds before the exam):**
> - **Force-Balance Principle** = continuous null deflection; output is the *balancing force*, not the spring deflection.
> - **Force-Balance Loop Components:** Primary Sensor → Summing Junction → Deflection Detector ($K_d$) → High-Gain Amplifier ($A$) → Actuator ($K_a$, feedback) + Output Transducer ($K_o$).
> - **Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**
> $$\dfrac{P_{out}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{A K_o K_a}{K_s + A K_d K_a} \;\xrightarrow{\;A K_d K_a \gg K_s\;}\; \dfrac{K_o}{K_d}$$
> - **Why force-balance is better:** output independent of spring linearity, hysteresis, ageing; high accuracy; negligible deflection.
> - **Dry Leg:** low-pressure side of a DP manometer with **no sealing liquid**; used for gases/dry vapours; contributes **zero** hydrostatic head.
> - **Wet Leg:** low-pressure side filled with seal fluid; contributes a **positive** correction $h \left( \rho_{seal} - \rho_{process} \right) / \rho_{seal}$.
> - **Manometer Equation (dry leg):** $\Delta P = \rho_m g h$
> - **Manometer Equation (general U-tube):** $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$
> - **Hydrostatic Law:** $P = \rho g h$ — applies to every liquid column.
> - **Typical KTU-asked conversions:** mm Hg ↔ Pa ↔ bar ↔ m H₂O. Memorise $1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa $= 750.06$ mm Hg.
> - **Real-world examples to quote in answers:** Rosemount 3051 smart pressure transmitter, electronic laboratory balance, Coriolis mass flow meter — all use force-balance (EMFC) feedback.
> - **Final exam mantra:** *"In a force-balance instrument, the spring never gets a chance to misbehave — the loop nulls it out before the output is taken."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Balance - Electronic Pressure Sensors: Strain Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT402 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motion Balance - Electronic Pressure Sensors: Strain Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Gauge**: A strain gauge (or strain gage) is a passive electrical transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a change in electrical resistance, based on the **piezoresistive effect**. When a metallic or semiconductor conductor is subjected to mechanical stress, its geometric dimensions (length and cross-sectional area) and intrinsic resistivity change, producing a measurable fractional change in resistance (ΔR/R) that is directly proportional to the applied strain (ε = ΔL/L).

> [!NOTE]
> **Motion Balance Principle**: In motion-balance (force-balance) electronic pressure sensors, the small mechanical displacement produced by the applied pressure on a primary sensing element (such as a diaphragm with bonded strain gauges) is detected by the strain-gauge bridge, and a feedback force generated by an electromagnetic actuator (force motor) is applied in the opposite direction to restore the diaphragm to its null position. The feedback current required to maintain null deflection is the output signal, and it is linearly proportional to the input pressure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

**The "Rubber Band & Ruler" Analogy:**

Imagine stretching a thin metallic wire (like a guitar string) and gluing it firmly onto a thin, flexible steel plate. Now, if you gently press your finger on the plate from one side:

- The plate **bends** slightly → the glued wire **stretches** along with it.
- As the wire stretches, it becomes **longer** and **thinner** (like pulling on a rubber band).
- A longer, thinner wire has **more electrical resistance** (just like water flowing through a narrow pipe).
- We can measure this tiny change in resistance using a sensitive **Wheatstone bridge circuit**, and convert it into a pressure reading.

**The "Spring Scale with a Brain" Analogy (Motion Balance):**

Think of a traditional bathroom scale — when you stand on it, a spring compresses and a needle moves. In a **motion-balance system**, the needle's movement is detected instantly, and an electromagnet pushes it *back* to the original position with exactly the right force. The amount of electricity needed to do this "pushing back" is your **pressure reading**. The advantages are perfect linearity, infinite resolution, and immunity to spring aging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Constants Used:**
> - Poisson's ratio for constantan alloy: **ν ≈ 0.30 – 0.33**
> - Gauge factor for metallic (constantan) gauges: **G ≈ 2.0 – 2.2**
> - Gauge factor for semiconductor gauges: **G ≈ 100 – 200**
> - Standard gauge resistance: **R = 120 Ω, 350 Ω, or 1000 Ω**

---

## 1.3 Classification of Strain Gauges Used in Pressure Sensors

| Class | Sub-type | Construction | Typical Application |
|-------|----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Metallic** | Bonded (wire / foil) | Grid etched or wound on a carrier, bonded to diaphragm | Industrial pressure transmitters |
| **Metallic** | Unbonded | Wires stretched between fixed posts and a moving armature | High-precision force/pressure transducers |
| **Semiconductor** | Diffused / Piezoresistive | Silicon die with doped resistors diffused in a Wheatstone pattern | MEMS pressure sensors, high-sensitivity applications |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Piezoresistive Effect — Why Resistance Changes Under Strain

When a metallic conductor of original length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is stretched:

1. **Length increases** by $\Delta L$ → resistance increases (directly proportional).
2. **Cross-sectional area decreases** due to the **Poisson effect** (the material squeezes sideways as it stretches axially) → resistance increases (inversely proportional).
3. **Resistivity changes** due to alteration of the crystal lattice spacing → piezoresistive contribution.

The fundamental resistance equation is:
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

Differentiating and converting to fractional changes:
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

## 2.2 The Gauge Factor (G) — The Master Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gauge Factor Definition**: The gauge factor $G$ is the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance to the fractional change in length (mechanical strain).
> $$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L}$$

For a metallic strain gauge, where the resistivity change is negligible ($\Delta\rho/\rho \approx 0$) and the cross-section change is governed by Poisson's ratio $\nu$:
$$G = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

- The term **$1 + 2\nu$** is the *geometric* contribution (≈ 1.6 for constantan).
- The term $\frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ is the *piezoresistive* contribution (small in metals, dominant in semiconductors).

## 2.3 The Wheatstone Bridge — Signal Conditioning

Because $\Delta R/R$ is extremely small (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$), a Wheatstone bridge is used to convert it into a measurable voltage.

For a single active arm (one strain gauge) with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 + G\varepsilon/2}\right) \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

For a **full bridge** with 4 active gauges (two in tension, two in compression):
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon$$

This configuration provides **4× higher sensitivity**, automatic temperature compensation, and **zero initial output** at null strain.

## 2.4 Motion-Balance (Force-Balance) Pressure Sensor — Operating Logic

The motion-balance architecture eliminates non-linearity caused by diaphragm deflection:

1. **Input stage**: Pressure $P$ acts on a sensing diaphragm, producing a force $F_{in} = P \cdot A_d$ (where $A_d$ is the effective diaphragm area).
2. **Sensing stage**: The diaphragm displaces by a tiny amount $\delta$. Bonded strain gauges in a Wheatstone bridge detect this displacement.
3. **Amplification stage**: The bridge output $V_{out}$ is amplified by a high-gain servo amplifier.
4. **Feedback stage**: The amplifier output drives a current through the coil of a permanent-magnet force motor. The resulting electromagnetic force $F_{fb} = K_m \cdot I_{fb}$ pushes the diaphragm back toward null.
5. **Null balance condition**: $F_{fb} = F_{in}$ → $I_{fb} = \dfrac{P \cdot A_d}{K_m}$

The output current $I_{fb}$ is therefore a **linear, exact analog of the input pressure $P$**, independent of diaphragm stiffness, aging, or material non-linearity.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistance of a conductor | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{6 P r^2 (1-\nu^2)}{E t^2}$ | Strain on a circular diaphragm (edge-clamped, radius $r$, thickness $t$, Young's modulus $E$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{out}^{(1/4)} \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} G \varepsilon$ | Quarter-bridge output voltage | V |
| 5 | $V_{out}^{(\text{full})} = V_{ex} G \varepsilon$ | Full-bridge output voltage | V |
| 6 | $F_{in} = P \cdot A_d$ | Input force on diaphragm | N |
| 7 | $I_{fb} = \dfrac{P \cdot A_d}{K_m}$ | Feedback current (motion-balance output) | A |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{P} = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot 6 r^2 (1-\nu^2)}{4 \cdot E \cdot t^2}$ | Pressure sensitivity (quarter bridge) | V/Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip**: For numerical problems on pressure sensors, you will *always* be expected to calculate the maximum strain at the diaphragm edge using the formula $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6Pr^2(1-\nu^2)}{Et^2}$ and then combine it with the bridge equation to get the output voltage. Memorize Equation 3 in the table — it is the single most-tested formula in this module.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DPT (Differential Pressure Transmitters)** used in oil & gas, refineries, and flow orifice plates.
- **Aerospace altimeters** and flight-control pressure transducers (silicon MEMS).
- **Biomedical devices** such as invasive blood-pressure sensors and respirator pressure monitors.
- **Hydraulic and pneumatic test rigs** requiring millibar resolution.
- **Process control loops** (4–20 mA current output) where the strain-gauge bridge is the front-end sensor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gauge Factor from First Principles

We start with the conductor resistance equation:
$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides:
$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

Differentiating (to obtain infinitesimal changes):
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

For a circular cross-section wire of diameter $D$, the area is $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. The lateral strain is related to axial strain through Poisson's ratio:
$$\frac{dD}{D} = -\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

Since $A \propto D^2$:
$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dD}{D} = -2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L}$$

Substituting back:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} + 2\nu \cdot \frac{dL}{L} = \frac{dL}{L} \left( 1 + 2\nu + \frac{d\rho/\rho}{dL/L} \right)$$

Recognizing the mechanical strain $\varepsilon = dL/L$ and defining the gauge factor $G$:
$$\boxed{\,G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic**: The term $1 + 2\nu$ arises purely from geometry (length increase + area decrease). For metals, $\Delta\rho/\rho$ is negligible, giving $G \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1 + 2(0.32) \approx 1.6$ to 2.0. For semiconductors, $\Delta\rho/\rho$ dominates and $G$ can reach 100–200.

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Strain on a Circular Pressure-Sensing Diaphragm

Consider a thin, edge-clamped circular diaphragm of radius $r$ and thickness $t$, loaded by a uniform pressure $P$ on one side. The maximum bending stress at the clamped edge (from thin-plate theory) is:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 P r^2}{t^2}$$

(We drop the $(1-\nu^2)$ factor for the simplified edge-stress expression; the KTU board usually accepts this form.)

Using Hooke's law in the radial (or tangential) direction at the edge:
$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \frac{6 P r^2}{E \, t^2}$$

If the full correction factor for plane strain is required:
$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 P r^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{E \, t^2}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic**: The strain grows with the **square of the radius** and the **inverse square of the thickness**. Doubling the diaphragm radius quadruples the strain; halving the thickness quadruples it. This is why KTU problems typically ask you to compute $\varepsilon$ first and then find the bridge output.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Full-Bridge Output Voltage

A full Wheatstone bridge has four active strain gauges — two on the top surface of the diaphragm (tangential, in tension) and two on the bottom surface (radial, in compression), arranged so that adjacent arms experience opposite-signed resistance changes.

For each active arm: $R_i = R_0(1 \pm G\varepsilon)$.

For a quarter-bridge approximation (one active arm, three fixed):
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_0(1+G\varepsilon)}{R_0(1+G\varepsilon) + R_0} - \frac{R_0}{R_0 + R_0} \right)$$

Simplifying:
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{1 + G\varepsilon}{2 + G\varepsilon} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{G\varepsilon}{4 + 2G\varepsilon}$$

For small strains ($G\varepsilon \ll 1$):
$$\boxed{\,V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} G \varepsilon\,}$$

For a full bridge with four active arms (two tensile, two compressive), the contributions add:
$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon\,}$$

> **Conversion logic**: The full bridge gives **4× the voltage** of a quarter bridge, **automatically cancels** the common-mode temperature drift, and yields **zero output** at zero pressure. This is the configuration used in all industrial-grade pressure transmitters.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Motion-Balance Output Current

The force balance equation on the diaphragm is:
$$F_{in} - F_{fb} - k \cdot \delta = m \cdot \ddot{\delta}$$

At steady state ($\ddot{\delta} = 0$) and at null ($\delta \to 0$, the high-gain servo forces the deflection toward zero):
$$F_{in} = F_{fb}$$

Substituting $F_{in} = P \cdot A_d$ and $F_{fb} = K_m \cdot I_{fb}$:
$$\boxed{\,I_{fb} = \frac{P \cdot A_d}{K_m}\,}$$

Where:
- $A_d$ = effective diaphragm area (m²)
- $K_m$ = motor force constant (N/A)
- $I_{fb}$ = feedback current (A) — the **output** of the transmitter

> **Conversion logic**: The output current is **directly proportional** to pressure with a constant of proportionality $A_d / K_m$. This linearity is the central advantage of the motion-balance architecture — diaphragm non-linearity, hysteresis, and creep are completely suppressed by the negative feedback loop.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Given:**
- Diaphragm radius $r = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- Diaphragm thickness $t = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa $= 2 \times 10^{11}$ Pa
- Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$
- Gauge factor $G = 2.0$
- Excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 10$ V
- Applied pressure $P = 1$ MPa $= 10^6$ Pa
- Bridge configuration: **full bridge**

**Find:** (a) Maximum strain at the diaphragm edge. (b) Output voltage of the bridge.

**Solution:**

### Part (a): Maximum Strain

We use the diaphragm strain formula:
$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 P r^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{E \, t^2}$$

Substituting the numerical values:
$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 \cdot (10^6) \cdot (0.020)^2 \cdot (1 - 0.30^2)}{(2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^2}$$

Step 1 — Compute the numerator:
$$6 \cdot 10^6 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4} \cdot (1 - 0.09) = 2.4 \times 10^{3} \cdot 0.91 = 2.184 \times 10^{3}$$

Step 2 — Compute the denominator:
$$2 \times 10^{11} \cdot 25 \times 10^{-8} = 5 \times 10^{4}$$

Step 3 — Divide:
$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{2.184 \times 10^{3}}{5 \times 10^{4}} = 0.04368 = 4.368 \times 10^{-2}$$

So $\varepsilon_{max} \approx 43.68 \times 10^{-3} = 4368 \; \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain).

> **Sanity check**: A strain of ~0.044 (4.4%) is high but physically plausible for a thin metal diaphragm near its elastic limit. In practice, designers keep $\varepsilon < 0.001$ to avoid plastic deformation.

### Part (b): Output Voltage (Full Bridge)

We use the full-bridge equation:
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon_{max}$$

Substituting:
$$V_{out} = 10 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 0.04368 = 0.8736 \text{ V}$$

So the bridge produces approximately **$V_{out} \approx 0.874$ V** (or **874 mV**) at 1 MPa input pressure — comfortably within the 0–10 V input range of a typical instrumentation amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure sensitivity (S)** for this sensor would therefore be $S = 0.874 \text{ V} / 1 \text{ MPa} = 0.874 \text{ mV/kPa}$.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Quarter Bridge | Half Bridge | Full Bridge |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| Active gauges | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Output voltage | $V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 4$ | $V_{ex} G \varepsilon / 2$ | $V_{ex} G \varepsilon$ |
| Temperature compensation | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Non-linearity error | Highest | Medium | Negligible |
| Sensitivity | $1\times$ | $2\times$ | $4\times$ |
| Typical KTU exam weightage | 30% | 20% | 50% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Working Principle of a Bonded Foil Strain Gauge Pressure Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Pressure P] --> B[Deflection of Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Strain on Bonded Foil Gauges]
    C --> D["Change in Resistance: ΔR = G·ε·R"]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge Imbalance]
    E --> F[Output Voltage V_out]
    F --> G["Conditioning: Amplifier + Filter"]
    G --> H[Standardized Output: 4-20 mA or 0-10 V]

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFE8B0,stroke:#A0522D
    style C fill:#FFFAE0,stroke:#A0522D
```

```mermaid
    D -->|Bridge voltage| AMP["High-Gain Servo Amplifier"]

    FM -->|Restoring force F_fb| D
    D -->|Null deflection| NULL["Null Condition: δ → 0"]
    NULL --> AMP
    AMP -->|Tap-off| OUT["Output: I_fb = P·A_d / K_m"]

    style P fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Wheatstone Bridge with Four Active Strain Gauges (Full-Bridge Layout on a Diaphragm)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        R3["R3 = R0(1 - G·ε)"]

    A --> R1
    R1 --> B["Node B (+)"]
    B --> R3
    R3 --> GND1["V_ex (-)"]
```
