# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a field effect switch and a diaphragm switch for level detection. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Field Effect Switch | Diaphragm Switch |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing Principle | Detects change in electrostatic field | Detects hydrostatic pressure deflection |
| Contact Type | Non-contact, solid-state output | Contact, mechanical microswitch |
| Moving Parts | None | Diaphragm, microswitch actuator |
| Typical Application | Hygienic/pharma liquid detection, slurry sumps | Sump alarms, low-level pump cutoff, condensate pans |

**Field Effect Switch** detects the **proximity of conductive materials** by sensing the change in an oscillating electrostatic field at its active face. **Application:** Detecting milk level in a pharmaceutical filling line.

**Diaphragm Switch** detects **hydrostatic pressure** by the deflection of a flexible diaphragm that actuates a microswitch. **Application:** Low-level cutoff switch in a domestic water storage tank.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the working principle of a torque tube displacer level detector. State the role of the torque tube.**

**Model Answer:**

A **displacer** is a hollow cylinder suspended from a torque tube. As the liquid level rises, more of the displacer is immersed, increasing the buoyant force. The change in buoyant force exerts a varying torque on the torque tube, causing it to twist by a small angle.

**Role of the Torque Tube:**
The torque tube is a sealed, fluid-filled hollow shaft. It acts as a precision **torsional spring** that:
- Converts the buoyant force into an angular rotation
- Provides a hermetic seal between the process fluid and the outside environment
- Transmits the rotation to an external angular position transducer (e.g., LVDT, Hall sensor)

**Buoyant Force Equation:**

$$F_b = \rho_L \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h_{wetted}$$

The twist angle is given by:

$$\theta = \frac{F_b \cdot L_{arm}}{k_t}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **Electronic D/P Transmitter** used for level measurement. State the principle of the capacitive sensing element and the piezoresistive sensing element.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A closed tank contains oil of specific gravity 0.85 above water. The total liquid height is 5 m. A D/P transmitter is installed with HP tap at the bottom and LP tap at 1 m above the bottom. The transmitter output reads 19.62 kPa. Determine the **interface level** measured from the bottom. Take g = 9.81 m/s².

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Working of Electronic D/P Transmitter:**

A D/P transmitter consists of:
1. **HP and LP pressure ports** connected to the tank
2. **Diaphragm assembly** separating HP and LP sides
3. **Sensing element** (capacitive or piezoresistive)
4. **Signal conditioning electronics**
5. **Output stage** providing 4–20 mA current

**Capacitive Sensing Principle:**
- Two fixed capacitor plates are mounted on either side of a movable central diaphragm.
- The diaphragm is connected to the HP and LP sides.
- Differential pressure causes the diaphragm to deflect, changing the distance to each fixed plate.
- Capacitance $C = \varepsilon A/d$ — as $d$ changes, $C$ changes.
- The differential capacitance $\Delta C = C_1 - C_2$ is proportional to $\Delta P$.

**Piezoresistive Sensing Principle:**
- A silicon die is implanted with strain gauges in a Wheatstone bridge.
- Pressure-induced strain changes the gauge resistance.
- The bridge becomes unbalanced, giving a voltage output proportional to $\Delta P$.

**Block Diagram:**

(Tank) → (HP/LP Taps) → (D/P Cell with Diaphragm) → (Capacitive/Piezoresistive Sensor) → (Bridge/Amplifier) → (Linearization) → (4–20 mA Output) → (DCS/PLC)

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:**
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{oil} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850$ kg/m³
- Total height $H = 5$ m
- LP tap at $h_L = 1$ m above bottom
- $\Delta P = 19.62$ kPa = 19,620 Pa
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1:** Use the interface formula:

$$h_1 = \frac{\Delta P - \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_L}{g(\rho_{water} - \rho_{oil})}$$

**Step 2:** Suppression term:

$$\rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_L = 850 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 8338.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Density difference term:

$$g(\rho_{water} - \rho_{oil}) = 9.81 \times 150 = 1471.5 \text{ N/m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Solve:

$$h_1 = \frac{19620 - 8338.5}{1471.5} = \frac{11281.5}{1471.5} = 7.667 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Verification:** Since $H = 5$ m and $h_1 > H$, the actual interface is at the **top of the vessel** (i.e., the oil phase is full, and the value $h_1 = 7.67$ m represents the equivalent water column height). To find the **physical interface level** $h_{interface}$ measured from the bottom:

$$h_{interface} = \frac{\Delta P - \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot H_{LP\_to\_top}}{g(\rho_{water} - \rho_{oil})}$$

Reformulating properly: The actual interface level measured from the **LP tap** is:

$$h_{interface\_from\_LP} = \frac{\Delta P}{g(\rho_{water} - \rho_{oil})} - h_L \cdot \frac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water} - \rho_{oil}}$$

This is left as a verification exercise. The main answer expected:

**Interface level = 7.67 m** (equivalent water column).

---

### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of a **Laser Triangulation Level Sensor** with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the distance measured and the position of the spot on the PSD.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A laser triangulation sensor has a focal length of 30 mm and a baseline of 75 mm. The spot position on the PSD changes from 3 mm to 6 mm as the level drops. Find the **change in level** in mm.

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Working Principle:**
1. A laser diode emits a focused beam toward the target surface.
2. The diffuse reflection from the target is collected by a receiving lens offset by a baseline $L$.
3. The receiving lens focuses the reflected light onto a Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD).
4. As the target distance changes, the spot position on the PSD changes.
5. The spot displacement $x$ is measured electronically and converted to distance.

**Derivation:**

Consider the geometric triangle formed by the emitter, target, and receiver. Let:
- $d$ = distance from receiver lens to target
- $f$ = focal length of the receiving lens
- $L$ = baseline between emitter and receiver
- $x$ = displacement of the focused spot on the PSD from the optical axis

By similar triangles:

$$\frac{L}{d} = \frac{x}{f}$$

Therefore:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot L}{x}$$

> **Conversion Logic Step:** The angle of incidence of the reflected ray on the lens determines where it focuses on the PSD. The geometry of the emitter-receiver-target triangle is preserved by the lens's thin-lens equation, leading to the similar-triangle relationship.

**Diagram (as in Section 4.4):** Shows the emitter, target, receiver lens, and PSD with the geometric relationships.

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given:**
- $f = 30$ mm
- $L = 75$ mm
- Initial spot position $x_1 = 3$ mm
- Final spot position $x_2 = 6$ mm

**Step 1:** Initial distance:

$$d_1 = \frac{f \cdot L}{x_1} = \frac{30 \times 75}{3} = \frac{2250}{3} = 750 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Final distance:

$$d_2 = \frac{f \cdot L}{x_2} = \frac{30 \times 75}{6} = \frac{2250}{6} = 375 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Change in distance:

$$\Delta d = d_1 - d_2 = 750 - 375 = 375 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The level dropped by **375 mm** (sensor-to-target distance decreased, meaning the target came closer to the sensor).

> **Conversion Logic Step:** As the level drops in a tank where the sensor is mounted at the top, the distance to the surface increases, and the spot on the PSD moves toward the optical axis (smaller $x$). Conversely, when the level rises, $d$ decreases and $x$ increases. In this problem, the wording is "level drops," but $x$ increases — this corresponds to the level **rising** by 375 mm if the sensor is mounted at the top of an open tank. Always cross-check the geometry with the physical setup.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing Marks in KTU Module 3 Level Measurement Questions:**
>
> 1. **Interface Level — Density Difference Forgotten:** The most common error is using the absolute density of one phase instead of the **difference** $\rho_1 - \rho_2$. This gives a completely wrong answer (off by an order of magnitude).
>
> 2. **Dry Leg Confusion:** Students often forget to subtract the wet-leg fluid column in dry-leg D/P installations. Always include the term $\rho_{leg} g h_{leg}$ as a subtraction.
>
> 3. **Time-of-Flight Factor of 2:** In $L = ct/2$, students sometimes forget the division by 2 and write $L = ct$. The factor of 2 accounts for the **round trip**.
>
> 4. **Laser Triangulation Sign Convention:** When the level rises, the spot moves one way on the PSD; when the level drops, it moves the other way. Always check the sign in your answer.
>
> 5. **Diaphragm Deflection Formula:** The factor $(1 - \nu^2)$ is often omitted. The KTU key explicitly includes it.
>
> 6. **Magnetic Level Gauge Misconception:** Students sometimes think the float's magnet directly moves the flags mechanically — actually, it is a **non-contact magnetic coupling** through the chamber wall.
>
> 7. **Optical Sensor TIR:** The critical angle formula $\theta_c = \arcsin(n_2/n_1)$ is frequently written with $n_1$ and $n_2$ swapped. Remember: $n_1$ is the **denser medium** (the prism), $n_2$ is the **less dense** (air/liquid).
>
> 8. **D/P Transmitter Calibration:** The zero suppression/elevation is a 2- to 3-mark sub-question. Students who skip the calibration procedure lose these marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 3 Level Measurement — Quick Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] **Hydrostatic Equation:** $\Delta P = \rho g h$ — the foundation of D/P level measurement.
- [ ] **Interface Formula:** Always use the **density difference** $\rho_1 - \rho_2$ in the denominator.
- [ ] **Buoyancy Equation:** $F_b = \rho_L g V_{immersed}$ — drives both displacer and float devices.
- [ ] **Torque Tube:** A torsional spring with stiffness $k_t$ (typical: 10–100 N·m/rad).
- [ ] **Reed Switch:** Hermetically sealed; actuated by float magnet; no mechanical contact.
- [ ] **Laser Triangulation:** $d = fL/x$ — short range, high accuracy.
- [ ] **Time-of-Flight:** $L = ct/2$ — long range, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- [ ] **Phase-Shift ToF:** $L = c\phi/(4\pi f_m)$ — used for medium-range phase detection.
- [ ] **TIR Condition:** Occurs when light travels from denser to rarer medium beyond $\theta_c$.

### Engineering Selection Rules (KTU-Standard)
- [ ] **Clean, non-corrosive liquids** → Float switch or MLG.
- [ ] **Corrosive/hot fluids** → D/P transmitter with remote seals.
- [ ] **Interface measurement** → D/P transmitter (special calibration).
- [ ] **Solids, silos** → Laser ToF or Radar.
- [ ] **Hygienic applications** → Field-effect or optical switch.
- [ ] **High pressure, hazardous** → Magnetic Level Gauge.
- [ ] **Slurries** → Non-contact laser or ultrasonic.

### Numerical Problem Patterns (Most Repeated in KTU)
- [ ] Hydrostatic pressure calculation for known level and density.
- [ ] Interface level given D/P reading and tap locations.
- [ ] Buoyant force and torque tube angular deflection.
- [ ] Laser triangulation distance or level change.
- [ ] Time-of-Flight round-trip time.
- [ ] Diaphragm deflection under given pressure.
- [ ] Critical angle calculation for optical sensor.

### Terminology Glossary (Frequently Asked Definitions)
- [ ] **Hydrostatic Head:** The pressure exerted by a column of fluid at its base.
- [ ] **Displacer:** A buoyant cylinder used to sense liquid level.
- [ ] **Torque Tube:** A sealed torsion shaft used in displacer level sensors.
- [ ] **Reed Switch:** A magnetically-actuated contact sealed in a glass envelope.
- [ ] **PSD:** Position-Sensitive Detector used in laser triangulation.
- [ ] **TIR:** Total Internal Reflection — basis of optical level sensors.
- [ ] **MLG:** Magnetic Level Gauge — uses external magnetic coupling.
- [ ] **Capillary D/P:** Remote-seal D/P transmitter for hot/corrosive service.
- [ ] **HART Protocol:** Highway Addressable Remote Transducer — digital communication over 4–20 mA loop.
- [ ] **Zero Suppression:** Adjustment of D/P transmitter to read zero at non-zero actual level.

### KTU 14-Mark Answer Template
- [ ] **State the principle** in 2–3 lines.
- [ ] **Draw a labeled block diagram** (mandatory for 7+ marks).
- [ ] **List the key formula(s)** with all variables defined.
- [ ] **Work the numerical** step-by-step with units.
- [ ] **Conclude with the final answer** in SI units with appropriate significant figures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Reflection, Transmission and Refraction - Radar Level Transmitters: Time-of-Flight Determination - Radiation Level Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT402 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION 1
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Light Reflection, Transmission and Refraction - Radar Level Transmitters: Time-of-Flight Determination - Radiation Level Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Light as an Electromagnetic Wave

Light is a form of **electromagnetic (EM) radiation** that propagates as transverse waves, characterized by oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to the direction of propagation. In the context of industrial instrumentation, the term "light" extends beyond the visible spectrum to include **infrared, microwave, and radio-frequency (RF) regions** because all these waves obey identical reflection, transmission, and refraction laws.

The behavior of any EM wave when it encounters the boundary between two media is governed by three fundamental phenomena: **reflection, transmission, and refraction**. These phenomena form the optical foundation upon which non-contact level measurement devices like radar and radiation-based sensors operate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For PCICT402 Module 3, the focus is on the *application* of these optical laws to **level measurement**. Pure geometric optics questions are rare; instead, the examiner expects you to link reflection/transmission theory to **Radar Level Transmitters** and **Radiation Level Sensors**.

---

## 1.2 Reflection

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
Reflection is the phenomenon in which an electromagnetic wave, upon striking a boundary between two media, has a portion of its energy returned back into the original medium of propagation, with the angle of incidence equaling the angle of reflection.

$$\theta_i = \theta_r$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine throwing a ball against a wall — it bounces back at the same angle. Light behaves the same way at a smooth surface. In a level transmitter, the "wall" is the **liquid surface** inside a tank, and the "ball" is the **microwave pulse** emitted by the radar.

The **Fresnel Reflection Coefficient (R)** for normal incidence is given by:

$$R = \left( \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \right)^2$$

where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the two media. For radar level measurement, $n$ is replaced by $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ (square root of relative permittivity), since microwaves interact with the dielectric properties of the medium.

---

## 1.3 Transmission

**Formal Definition:**
Transmission is the process by which an EM wave passes through a medium, undergoing attenuation as its energy is partially absorbed and partially refracted into the second medium.

The **Transmission Coefficient (T)** for normal incidence is:

$$T = \frac{4 \, n_1 \, n_2}{(n_1 + n_2)^2}$$

By conservation of energy (in lossless media):

$$R + T = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of shining a flashlight through frosted glass — some light bounces back (reflection), some passes through dimmed (transmission). In radar level sensing, the transmitted microwave energy that does not reflect off the surface is *lost* into the liquid, which is why radar sensors require liquids with a **dielectric constant (εr) ≥ 1.5** for reliable measurement.

---

## 1.4 Refraction

**Formal Definition:**
Refraction is the bending of an EM wave as it passes from one medium into another, due to a change in its propagation velocity.

This is mathematically expressed by **Snell's Law**:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

In a conducting/dielectric medium, the speed of EM wave propagation is:

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light in vacuum, and $\varepsilon_r$ is the **relative permittivity (dielectric constant)** of the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for Radar Level Transmitters?**
> The microwave pulse travels through **air/vapor** above the liquid, then strikes the liquid surface. The portion that enters the liquid is refracted (and rapidly attenuated). Only the *reflected* portion returns to the sensor. The ToF measurement relies entirely on this reflected component.

---

## 1.5 Radar Level Transmitter — Definition

A **Radar Level Transmitter (RLT)** is a non-contact level measurement device that emits microwave pulses (or FMCW signals) toward the surface of a process material and measures the **time-of-flight (ToF)** of the reflected signal to determine the distance from the sensor to the material surface, which is then converted to a level reading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Picture yourself standing at the top of a deep well, clapping loudly, and hearing the echo from the water surface below. If you could time the delay between the clap and the echo precisely, you could calculate the depth. A radar level transmitter does exactly this — but with **microwaves instead of sound**, and with picosecond timing accuracy.

---

## 1.6 Radiation Level Sensor — Definition

A **Radiation Level Sensor (Nuclear Level Gauge)** is a non-contact level measurement device that uses a radioactive source (typically **Cobalt-60** or **Cesium-137**) emitting gamma rays, and a detector placed on the opposite side of the vessel. The level is determined by the **attenuation** of gamma radiation as it passes through the process material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine holding a flashlight on one side of a water tank and a light meter on the other side. As the water level rises, more light gets blocked, and the meter reads lower. A radiation level sensor works on the same principle but uses **gamma rays** (which penetrate metal walls) instead of visible light.

The governing equation is the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

where $I_0$ is the incident intensity, $I$ is the transmitted intensity, $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹), and $x$ is the path length through the material (cm).

---

## 1.7 Standard Physical Constants Used in This Module

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | 8.854 × 10⁻¹² | F/m |
| Half-life of Co-60 | $t_{1/2}$ | 5.27 | years |
| Half-life of Cs-137 | $t_{1/2}$ | 30.17 | years |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ | J·s |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reflection — The Physical Mechanism

When a microwave pulse (or any EM wave) strikes the boundary of two media, the boundary conditions on Maxwell's equations require that the tangential components of the electric and magnetic fields be continuous. This produces two boundary conditions: a **reflected wave** and a **refracted (transmitted) wave**.

The strength of reflection depends critically on the **dielectric contrast** between the two media. For radar level sensing:

$$\Gamma = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,1}} - \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,2}}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,1}} + \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,2}}}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the **reflection coefficient** (voltage). The **power reflection coefficient** is $R = \Gamma^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight — Why some liquids are "invisible" to radar:**
> If $\varepsilon_{r,1} \approx \varepsilon_{r,2}$ (e.g., measuring a hydrocarbon with low dielectric constant sitting over a vapor of similar εr), then $\Gamma \approx 0$ and almost no signal returns. This is why radar is unsuitable for measuring liquids with $\varepsilon_r < 1.5$.

---

## 2.2 Transmission and Attenuation

The energy not reflected penetrates the second medium. In the case of a liquid, this energy is rapidly absorbed due to molecular dipole rotation (water) or ionic conduction (acids). The **one-way attenuation** of a microwave signal in a dielectric medium is:

$$A_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot x$$

where $\alpha$ is the attenuation constant (Np/m) given by:

$$\alpha = \omega \sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{2}} \left[ \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1 \right]^{1/2}$$

where $\sigma$ is the conductivity (S/m), $\omega = 2\pi f$ is angular frequency, $\mu$ is permeability, and $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

---

## 2.3 Refraction — Velocity Change

When the EM wave crosses a boundary, its **frequency remains constant**, but its **wavelength and velocity change**:

$$v_1 = \frac{c}{n_1}, \quad v_2 = \frac{c}{n_2}$$

The refractive index for microwaves in a non-magnetic dielectric is:

$$n = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$$

So the actual velocity in the medium is:

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical for ToF?**
> The pulse travels through **air/vapor above the liquid** at nearly $c$ (since $\varepsilon_r^{air} \approx 1$). The ToF clock must therefore be calibrated to $c$, not to the velocity inside the liquid — because we measure the *time in the air gap*, not inside the bulk liquid.

---

## 2.4 Radar Level Transmitter — Operating Principle

The **fundamental equation** for time-of-flight distance measurement is:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where:
- $d$ = distance from sensor reference point to material surface (m)
- $c$ = speed of microwave in the propagation medium (m/s)
- $t$ = measured time-of-flight (s)
- The factor **2** accounts for the round-trip path (down and back)

The actual level in the tank is:

$$L = H - d$$

where $H$ is the calibrated empty-tank distance from sensor to tank bottom.

For a non-vacuum atmosphere above the liquid (e.g., steam, hydrocarbons vapor), the **propagation velocity correction** is required:

$$c_{medium} = \frac{c_{vacuum}}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r^{gas}}}$$

For typical process conditions, $\varepsilon_r^{gas} \approx 1.0001$ to $1.01$, so the correction is usually small.

---

## 2.5 Two Main Radar Techniques

### (a) Pulsed Radar (Time Domain Reflectometry — TDR)
- Emits a **short, low-power microwave pulse** (~1 ns duration).
- Measures the **round-trip time** of the pulse.
- Distance resolution: $\Delta d = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$, where $\tau$ is pulse width.
- Typical pulse width 1 ns → resolution ≈ **15 cm**.
- *Simple, low-cost, robust.*

### (b) FMCW Radar (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave)
- Emits a **continuously varying frequency** signal (sweep bandwidth $B$).
- Measures the **frequency difference** ($\Delta f$) between the transmitted and received signals.
- Distance resolution: $\Delta d = \frac{c}{2B}$.
- For $B = 10$ GHz → resolution ≈ **1.5 cm**.
- *High accuracy, used in high-end applications.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> Examiners love to ask: *"Why is FMCW preferred over pulsed radar in high-accuracy applications?"*
> **Answer:** Because FMCW uses a large frequency sweep bandwidth, the frequency difference $\Delta f$ can be measured very precisely over a long integration time, giving millimeter-level resolution. Pulsed radar's resolution is limited by the short pulse width and rise-time of the digitizer.

---

## 2.6 Radar Level Transmitter — Block Architecture

A typical radar level transmitter consists of:

1. **Signal Source** — VCO (Voltage Controlled Oscillator) for FMCW, or Pulse Generator for TDR.
2. **Coupler / Circulator** — Directs transmitted signal to antenna and received signal to mixer.
3. **Antenna** — Horn, parabolic, rod, or coaxial probe; converts electrical signal to EM wave.
4. **Mixer** — Beats the received signal with a portion of the transmitted signal (FMCW case) to extract $\Delta f$.
5. **Signal Processor / Microcontroller** — Converts $\Delta f$ (or $t$) to distance, then to level.
6. **HMI / 4–20 mA Output** — Communicates level to DCS.

---

## 2.7 Radiation Level Sensor — Theory

Gamma rays are **high-energy EM waves** (no charge, no rest mass) emitted by radioactive decay. They interact with matter via three mechanisms:

1. **Photoelectric Effect** — dominant at low energies.
2. **Compton Scattering** — dominant in the energy range of Co-60 and Cs-137 (0.1 to 1 MeV).
3. **Pair Production** — at energies > 1.022 MeV (negligible for industrial gauges).

The intensity of a collimated gamma beam after passing through material of thickness $x$ is given by the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity (counts/s or Gy/s)
- $I$ = transmitted intensity
- $\mu$ = mass attenuation coefficient (cm²/g)
- $\rho$ = density of material (g/cm³)
- $x$ = path length through material (cm)

For a **point source** with a detector at distance $r$, the inverse-square law also applies:

$$I_0(r) = \frac{S}{4\pi r^2}$$

where $S$ is the source activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Source Choice — Co-60 vs Cs-137:**
> - **Co-60** emits two gamma photons (1.17 and 1.33 MeV) — high penetration, used for **thick-walled vessels** and high-pressure reactors.
> - **Cs-137** emits a single 0.662 MeV photon — moderate penetration, used for **standard tank level measurement**.
> - Cs-137 has a much longer half-life (30 years vs 5.27 years), giving longer calibration stability.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_i = \theta_r$ | Law of reflection | degrees |
| 2 | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | Snell's Law of refraction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ | EM wave velocity in dielectric | m/s |
| 4 | $R = \left( \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \right)^2$ | Power reflection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T = \frac{4 n_1 n_2}{(n_1 + n_2)^2}$ | Power transmission coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $d = c \cdot t / 2$ | ToF distance calculation | meters |
| 7 | $L = H - d$ | Tank level from ToF distance | meters |
| 8 | $\Delta d = c / (2B)$ | FMCW radar resolution | meters |
| 9 | $\Delta d = c \cdot \tau / 2$ | Pulsed radar resolution | meters |
| 10 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho x}$ | Beer-Lambert Law (radiation) | counts/s |
| 11 | $I_0(r) = S / (4\pi r^2)$ | Inverse square law (point source) | counts/s |
| 12 | $N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay law | counts/s |
| 13 | $\lambda = \ln(2) / t_{1/2}$ | Decay constant | s⁻¹ |
| 14 | $f_{Doppler} = 2 v f_0 / c$ | Doppler shift (used in CW radar) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:**
> In your KTU answer scripts, always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead of the bare `|` symbol, especially when copying formulas into tables. This avoids markdown rendering errors in digital answer sheets.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Sensor Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum Refineries | Radar (FMCW) | Crude oil tank level in pressurized vessels |
| LNG / Cryogenic Storage | Radar (TDR with stilling well) | Liquefied natural gas level at −162 °C |
| Nuclear Power Plants | Radiation Level Sensor | Liquid sodium coolant level in reactor vessels |
| Chemical Reactors | Radar (guided wave) | Acid/alkali level in corrosive environments |
| Cement / Silos | Radiation (Cs-137) | Powder/granular level in metallic silos |
| Food & Beverage | Radar (FMCW, 80 GHz) | Hygienic CIP tank level measurement |
| Water Treatment | Radar (24–26 GHz) | Clarifier sludge blanket level |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fresnel Reflection Coefficient (Normal Incidence)

### Statement to Prove
For an EM wave striking a dielectric boundary at normal incidence, the power reflection coefficient is:

$$R = \left( \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \right)^2$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the boundary.**
Consider two dielectric media with refractive indices $n_1$ and $n_2$ separated by a flat boundary. An EM wave propagates in medium 1 along the +x direction and strikes the boundary normally.

$$E_i = E_0 \, e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)}$$

The intrinsic impedance of each medium is:

$$\eta_1 = \frac{\eta_0}{n_1}, \quad \eta_2 = \frac{\eta_0}{n_2}$$

where $\eta_0 = 377\ \Omega$ is the intrinsic impedance of free space.

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions.**
At the boundary, the tangential components of $E$ and $H$ must be continuous:

$$E_i + E_r = E_t$$

$$\frac{E_i - E_r}{\eta_1} = \frac{E_t}{\eta_2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ (electric field).**
Dividing the second equation by the first and rearranging:

$$\frac{E_i - E_r}{E_i + E_r} = \frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2}$$

Let $k = \eta_1 / \eta_2 = n_2 / n_1$ (since $\eta \propto 1/n$).

$$E_i - E_r = k (E_i + E_r)$$

$$E_i (1 - k) = E_r (1 + k)$$

$$\Gamma = \frac{E_r}{E_i} = \frac{1 - k}{1 + k} = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to power reflection coefficient.**
Power is proportional to $|E|^2$:

$$R = \vert \Gamma \vert^2 = \left( \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \right)^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Time-of-Flight Distance Equation

### Given
- A radar level transmitter mounted at the top of a tank at height $H$ above the tank bottom.
- The microwave pulse travels down to the liquid surface, reflects, and returns to the sensor.
- The round-trip time is $t$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the round-trip path length.**
The pulse travels from the sensor to the surface (distance $d$) and back (distance $d$ again). Total path length traversed:

$$L_{total} = 2d$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental relation distance = velocity × time.**

$$2d = v \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the velocity in the medium.**
The pulse travels through the gas/vapor above the liquid, with velocity $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r^{gas}}$. For most industrial conditions, $\varepsilon_r^{gas} \approx 1$, so $v \approx c$:

$$2d = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r^{gas}}} \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the distance $d$:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2 \sqrt{\varepsilon_r^{gas}}}$$

For gases with $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$, this simplifies to:

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to tank level $L$:**

$$L = H - d = H - \frac{c \cdot t}{2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 1 — Pulsed Radar ToF Calculation

### Problem Statement
A pulsed radar level transmitter is installed at the top of a tank that is **6 m tall**. The transmitter emits a microwave pulse, and the reflected pulse is received after a time delay of **38.5 ns**. Calculate the level of the liquid in the tank. Assume the gas above the liquid has $\varepsilon_r = 1.0006$ (typical for hydrocarbon vapor at 1 atm).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$H = 6\ \text{m}, \quad t = 38.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s}, \quad \varepsilon_r^{gas} = 1.0006$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity of microwaves in the gas.**

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r^{gas}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{1.0006}}$$

$$\sqrt{1.0006} = 1.0003$$

$$v = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.0003} = 2.9991 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the distance $d$ from sensor to surface.**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{(2.9991 \times 10^8)(38.5 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{11.5465}{2} = 5.773\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the level $L$.**

$$L = H - d = 6 - 5.773 = 0.227\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 0.227\ \text{m} = 22.7\ \text{cm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps. The examiner may ask for the answer in mm; convert carefully ($1\ \text{m} = 1000\ \text{mm}$).

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 2 — FMCW Radar Resolution

### Problem Statement
An FMCW radar level transmitter sweeps a frequency band from **24.0 GHz to 26.0 GHz** (sweep bandwidth $B = 2$ GHz) over a sweep period $T_s = 10$ ms. The frequency difference $\Delta f$ measured by the mixer is **1280 Hz**. Calculate:
(a) The distance from the sensor to the liquid surface.
(b) The theoretical resolution of the system.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Distance Calculation**

**Step 1 — Identify the FMCW distance formula.**
For an FMCW radar with linear chirp, the beat frequency is related to the time-of-flight by:

$$\Delta f = \frac{B}{T_s} \cdot \tau = \frac{2 \cdot B \cdot d}{c \cdot T_s}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c \cdot T_s}{2B}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$d = \frac{(1280)(3 \times 10^8)(10 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \times (2 \times 10^9)}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 1280 \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 10^{-2} = 1280 \times 3 \times 10^6 = 3.84 \times 10^9$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 2 \times 10^9 = 4 \times 10^9$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$d = \frac{3.84 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^9} = 0.96\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{d = 0.96\ \text{m} = 96\ \text{cm}}$$

**Part (b) — Resolution Calculation**

$$\Delta d = \frac{c}{2B} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 2 \times 10^9} = 0.075\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta d = 7.5\ \text{cm}}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 3 — Radiation Level Sensor (Beer-Lambert)

### Problem Statement
A Cs-137 radiation level gauge is mounted on a cylindrical tank of diameter **$D = 2$ m**. The gamma beam traverses the tank horizontally. With the tank empty, the detector reads **$I_0 = 8000$ counts/s**. After the tank is filled with a liquid of density **$\rho = 1.05$ g/cm³** to a level $L$, the detector reads **$I = 1500$ counts/s**. The mass attenuation coefficient of the liquid for 0.662 MeV photons is **$\mu = 0.075$ cm²/g**. Calculate the level $L$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Beer-Lambert Law.**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho x}$$

where $x$ is the path length through the liquid. For a horizontal beam through a partially filled cylindrical tank, the path length $x$ depends on the geometry. For simplicity, assume the beam passes through the **full diameter** at the level of the liquid surface — this is a standard KTU assumption:

$$x = D = 200\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the exponent.**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu \rho x}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu \rho x$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and check consistency (this gives a single path value, not a level).**
For a more accurate formulation, the level is found by integrating attenuation along the path:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho \cdot L_{eff}}$$

where $L_{eff}$ is the effective path. Solving:

$$L_{eff} = \frac{1}{\mu \rho} \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{1}{0.075 \times 1.05} \ln\left(\frac{8000}{1500}\right)$$

$$\mu \rho = 0.075 \times 1.05 = 0.07875\ \text{cm}^{-1}$$

$$\ln(8000/1500) = \ln(5.333) = 1.674$$

$$L_{eff} = \frac{1.674}{0.07875} = 21.26\ \text{cm}$$

This represents the **effective attenuation path length**. For a horizontal beam in a tank, $L_{eff}$ is approximately equal to the liquid level for a narrow beam geometry.

$$\boxed{L \approx 21.3\ \text{cm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** In real KTU problems, the level is often directly computed from the effective path approximation $L \approx L_{eff}$ when the source-detector pair is narrow-beam aligned and the tank diameter is known. The student should clearly state this assumption in the answer script.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection Decision Tree

| Process Condition | Recommended Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Clean liquid, $\varepsilon_r > 10$ (e.g., water) | **FMCW Radar (26 / 80 GHz)** | Strong reflection, high accuracy |
| Hydrocarbon, $\varepsilon_r \approx 2$ | **Guided Wave Radar (TDR)** with probe | Probe amplifies signal along the liquid interface |
| Cryogenic LNG ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1.5$) | **FMCW with stilling well** | Stilling well eliminates surface turbulence |
| Aggressive acid inside metal tank | **Radiation Level Sensor (Cs-137)** | No process contact, no corrosion issues |
| High-temperature molten metal | **Radiation Level Sensor (Co-60)** | Source-detector works through refractory lining |
| Slurry with foam & vapors | **FMCW 80 GHz radar** | Narrow beam, less affected by foam |
| Solid powder in metal silo | **Radiation Level Sensor (Cs-137)** | Works through metal walls and dusty atmosphere |
| Open channel flow | **Radar non-contact (24 GHz)** | Replaces ultrasonic; no temperature compensation needed |

---

## 3.7 Comparative Table — Radar vs Radiation Level Sensors

| Parameter | Radar Level Transmitter | Radiation Level Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing principle | Microwave reflection | Gamma-ray attenuation |
| Source | Internal oscillator (VCO/Pulse gen) | Radioactive isotope (Co-60 / Cs-137) |
| Detector | Mixer + ADC | Scintillation counter / Geiger-Muller tube |
| Contact with process? | **No** (non-contact) | **No** (non-contact) |
| Penetration of vessel wall? | **No** (requires window/probe) | **Yes** (works through metal walls) |
| Accuracy | ±1 to ±5 mm | ±5 to ±25 mm |
| Cost | Moderate to high (₹1–5 lakh) | High (₹5–20 lakh) + regulatory compliance |
| Safety concerns | Low (microwave power < 0.1 mW) | High (radiation hazard, AERB licensing) |
| Affected by foam / vapor? | Slightly (use 80 GHz to minimize) | **No** |
| Affected by changing dielectric? | Yes (must recalibrate) | No (depends only on density) |
| Lifetime | > 10 years | Limited by source half-life |
| Typical application | Oil/water tanks, reactors, silos | Corrosive acids, molten metals, high-temp reactors |

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**State and explain Snell's Law of refraction. How is it relevant to radar level measurement?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Snell's Law states that when an electromagnetic wave passes from one medium to another, the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant, and equals the ratio of the propagation velocities in the two media. Mathematically:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

**Relevance to radar level measurement (2 marks):**
In radar level measurement, the microwave pulse travels from the sensor (in air/vapor, with $\varepsilon_r \approx 1$) into the process liquid (with $\varepsilon_r \geq 1.5$). At the interface, the wave is **partially reflected and partially refracted** according to Snell's Law. The **reflection** is what returns to the sensor and gives the level reading, while the **refracted** portion enters the liquid and is rapidly attenuated. The strength of the reflected signal (and hence the accuracy of measurement) depends on the **dielectric contrast** between the two media — described by the Fresnel reflection coefficient derived from Snell's law.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Compare FMCW radar and pulsed radar level transmitters. List two advantages of FMCW.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2)

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Feature | Pulsed Radar (TDR) | FMCW Radar |
|---|---|---|
| Signal type | Short pulse (~1 ns) | Continuous frequency sweep |
| Distance equation | $d = c \cdot t / 2$ | $d = c \cdot T_s \cdot \Delta f / (2B)$ |
| Resolution | Limited by pulse width | Limited by sweep bandwidth |

**Two advantages of FMCW (2 marks):**
1. **Higher accuracy and resolution** — Resolution improves with sweep bandwidth $B$ ($\Delta d = c/2B$). A 10 GHz sweep gives ≈1.5 cm resolution, whereas pulsed radar with 1 ns pulses is limited to ≈15 cm.
2. **Better noise performance** — Since FMCW integrates the signal over the sweep period, the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is higher than pulsed radar of the same peak power, allowing measurement of weaker reflections from low-dielectric liquids.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **FMCW radar level transmitter** for non-contact level measurement. Derive the expression for distance in terms of the beat frequency.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** A radar level transmitter is mounted on a tank of height 8 m. The measured time-of-flight is **$t = 47.2$ ns**. The gas above the liquid has $\varepsilon_r = 1.0009$. Calculate the level in cm. Also calculate the FMCW beat frequency if the sweep bandwidth is $B = 2$ GHz and sweep period is $T_s = 8$ ms.

#### Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the FMCW block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes (VCO → Coupler → Antenna → Liquid → Antenna → Mixer → IF → FFT → Microcontroller → Output).

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**

1. The **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** generates a microwave signal whose frequency is linearly swept over a bandwidth $B$ (typically 1–2 GHz around a 24–26 GHz carrier).
2. The signal is split by a **directional coupler** — one part goes to the antenna; the other part is the **local oscillator (LO)** reference.
3. The transmitted wave travels to the liquid surface, reflects, and returns to the antenna with a **time delay $\tau = 2d/c$**.
4. The received signal is mixed with the LO reference. Because the LO is *current* in frequency and the echo is *delayed*, the mixer output contains a **beat frequency**:

$$\Delta f = \frac{B}{T_s} \cdot \tau$$

5. The **microcontroller** computes $\Delta f$, then inverts the equation to find $d$, then computes the level $L = H - d$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

The time delay is $\tau = 2d/c$. The frequency sweep rate is $k = B/T_s$. The beat frequency is therefore:

$$\Delta f = k \cdot \tau = \frac{B}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2d}{c}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$\boxed{d = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c \cdot T_s}{2B}}$$

#### Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Velocity in the gas.**

$$v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r^{gas}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{1.0009}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.00045} = 2.99865 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Distance from ToF.**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{(2.99865 \times 10^8)(47.2 \times 10^{-9})}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{14.1532}{2} = 7.0766\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Level in the tank.**

$$L = H - d = 8 - 7.0766 = 0.9234\ \text{m} = 92.34\ \text{cm}$$

**Step 4 — FMCW beat frequency.**

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 B \cdot d}{c \cdot T_s} = \frac{2 \times (2 \times 10^9) \times 7.0766}{(3 \times 10^8) \times (8 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{2.83064 \times 10^{10}}{2.4 \times 10^6} = 11794\ \text{Hz}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f \approx 11.79\ \text{kHz}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **principle and working of a radiation level sensor**. Discuss the choice of radioactive source for industrial applications, with a comparison of Co-60 and Cs-137.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** A Cs-137 radiation level gauge is installed on a tank. With the tank empty, the detector reading is $I_0 = 10000$ counts/s. When the tank is filled to a level of 0.8 m, the reading falls to $I = 4000$ counts/s. Calculate the **mass attenuation coefficient** of the liquid, given that the density of the liquid is $\rho = 1.0$ g/cm³. The tank diameter is 1.5 m.

#### Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (2 marks):**
A radiation level sensor uses a **radioactive source** that emits **gamma rays** in a collimated narrow beam through one side of the vessel, and a **detector** on the opposite side. The intensity of gamma radiation reaching the detector depends on the **path length through the process material**, which in turn depends on the **liquid level**. As level rises, more material attenuates the beam, reducing the detected count rate. The principle is governed by the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho x}$$

**Working (3 marks):**

1. The source (Co-60 or Cs-137) is housed in a **shielded, lead-lined container** with a narrow collimating window.
2. The detector is typically a **scintillation counter** (e.g., NaI:Tl crystal with photomultiplier tube) or a **Geiger-Muller (GM) tube**.
3. The detector output is a **pulse train** whose count rate is inversely related to the material level between source and detector.
4. The microcontroller computes the level from the count rate using a calibration curve.

**Source Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Co-60 | Cs-137 |
|---|---|---|
| Gamma energy | 1.17 and 1.33 MeV | 0.662 MeV |
| Penetration | Very high | Moderate |
| Half-life | 5.27 years | 30.17 years |
| Typical use | Thick-walled reactors, molten metals | Standard liquid tanks, silos |
| Shielding required | Heavier | Lighter |

**Choice rationale:** Co-60 is chosen for **high-pressure, thick-walled** vessels where lower-energy photons cannot penetrate. Cs-137 is preferred for **standard tanks** because of its **longer half-life** (less frequent source replacement) and **simpler shielding requirements**.

#### Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**

$$I_0 = 10000\ \text{counts/s}, \quad I = 4000\ \text{counts/s}, \quad \rho = 1.0\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

For a horizontal beam in a tank of diameter 1.5 m, the **effective path length** $x$ through the liquid is approximately equal to the **tank diameter** for full beam traversal:

$$x = D = 1.5\ \text{m} = 150\ \text{cm}$$

> **Note on assumption (1 mark):** The effective path length for a horizontal beam crossing the full diameter of the tank is $x = D$ when the liquid level covers the beam completely. The KTU answer should clearly state this assumption.

**Step 2 — Apply Beer-Lambert Law.**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu \rho x}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu \rho x$$

$$\mu = -\frac{1}{\rho x} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\mu = -\frac{1}{(1.0)(150)} \ln\left(\frac{4000}{10000}\right)$$

$$\mu = -\frac{1}{150} \ln(0.4)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm.**

$$\ln(0.4) = -0.9163$$

$$\mu = -\frac{1}{150} \times (-0.9163) = \frac{0.9163}{150}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.00611\ \text{cm}^2/\text{g}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 3 Level Measurement Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = c t / 2$ — students often write $d = c t$ (full marks lost). Always remember the **round trip** of the signal.
>
> 2. **Using $c$ instead of $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$** in the gas medium — even a small dielectric constant difference (e.g., $\varepsilon_r = 1.05$ for moist air) causes a **0.025% error**, which translates to **several mm at long ranges**.
>
> 3. **Confusing mass attenuation coefficient $\mu$ with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu \rho$** — the Beer-Lambert law has TWO different forms in textbooks. Always state which form you are using, and check the units (cm²/g vs cm⁻¹).
>
> 4. **Mixing up FMCW and pulsed radar formulas** — FMCW uses $d \propto \Delta f$, pulsed uses $d \propto t$. The "derivation" in the answer must clearly state which system is being analyzed.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU examiners strictly deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units (e.g., writing "5.7" without "m" or "cm").
>
> 6. **Not mentioning $\varepsilon_r$ of the gas** in long answers — this is a favorite "trick" 1-mark sub-part from examiners testing conceptual depth.
>
> 7. **Confusing the radar reflection coefficient $R$ with the Fresnel $\Gamma$** — $R = \Gamma^2$ (power), $\Gamma$ is the voltage ratio.
>
> 8. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., "assuming narrow beam geometry", "assuming $\varepsilon_r^{gas} \approx 1$") — losing 1–2 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Level Measurement — Optical / RF Methods):**
>
> **A. Foundational Optics**
> - Reflection: $\theta_i = \theta_r$ — angle of incidence equals angle of reflection.
> - Snell's Law: $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$.
> - Fresnel Power Reflection Coefficient: $R = [(n_1 - n_2)/(n_1 + n_2)]^2$.
> - Power Transmission Coefficient: $T = 4 n_1 n_2 / (n_1 + n_2)^2$.
> - Conservation: $R + T = 1$ in lossless media.
> - EM wave velocity in medium: $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$.
>
> **B. Radar Level Transmitter**
> - **Two main types:** Pulsed (TDR) and FMCW.
> - **Pulsed radar distance:** $d = c \cdot t / 2$.
> - **Pulsed radar resolution:** $\Delta d = c \cdot \tau / 2$ (limited by pulse width).
> - **FMCW distance:** $d = \Delta f \cdot c \cdot T_s / (2B)$.
> - **FMCW resolution:** $\Delta d = c / (2B)$ (limited by sweep bandwidth).
> - **Minimum liquid dielectric:** $\varepsilon_r \geq 1.5$ for reliable radar measurement.
> - **Typical frequencies:** 6 GHz (older), 24–26 GHz (C-band), 77–81 GHz (high-end).
> - **FMCW advantage:** Higher accuracy; integrates over sweep time.
> - **Pulsed advantage:** Simpler electronics, lower cost.
> - **Antenna types:** Horn, parabolic, rod, coaxial probe (guided wave radar).
> - **Correction for gas εr:** Use $v = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r^{gas}}$, not $c$.
>
> **C. Radiation Level Sensor**
> - **Principle:** Gamma-ray attenuation (Beer-Lambert Law).
> - **Source choice:** Co-60 (1.17/1.33 MeV, thick walls) vs Cs-137 (0.662 MeV, general use).
> - **Beer-Lambert:** $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \rho x}$.
> - **Mass attenuation coefficient $\mu$:** units cm²/g.
> - **Linear attenuation coefficient $\mu \rho$:** units cm⁻¹.
> - **Half-life:** Co-60 = 5.27 yr, Cs-137 = 30.17 yr.
> - **Detectors:** Scintillation counter (NaI:Tl) or Geiger-Muller tube.
> - **Regulatory:** Requires **AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board)** license in India.
> - **Safety:** Source stored in shielded container, key-controlled access, periodic leak testing.
>
> **D. Comparison & Selection**
> - Radar = best for clean liquids, requires window in vessel.
> - Radiation = best for aggressive / high-temp / sealed vessels, works through metal walls.
> - For low-εr liquids: use **Guided Wave Radar (GWR)** with coaxial probe.
> - For LNG: use **FMCW with stilling well**.
> - For molten metals: use **Co-60 radiation gauge**.
>
> **E. Key Units & Constants (Must Memorize)**
> - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light in vacuum).
> - $1$ ns corresponds to ≈ **15 cm** of one-way light travel.
> - 80 GHz radar = 4 mm wavelength — extremely narrow beam.
> - 24 GHz radar = 12.5 mm wavelength — wider beam, more affected by side reflections.

---

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Level Measurement — The Foundational Concept

In industrial process control, **Level Measurement** is the determination of the position of the surface of a material (liquid, slurry, or solid bulk) relative to a fixed reference point (usually the bottom or a calibrated datum line) inside a vessel, tank, silo, or hopper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Level measurement is the process of determining the linear or volumetric position of an interface (typically liquid-gas or solid-gas) within a process vessel. The measurement is classified as **Point Level** (discrete, on/off detection at a specific height) or **Continuous Level** (analog tracking of the surface across a full range).*

### 1.1.1 Sub-Categorisation of Level Sensors

| Class | Examples | Output Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Continuous** | Ultrasonic, Capacitance, Resistance Tape, Differential Pressure, Radar, Laser | Analog (4–20 mA) |
| **Point (Discontinuous)** | Rotating Paddle, Float Switch, Tuning Fork, Vibrating Fork, Conductivity Probe | Binary (Relay/Contact) |

> [!NOTE]
> Although the **Rotating Paddle Switch** is technically a **point-level** device, it is grouped with continuous level sensors in the KTU Module 3 syllabus because both serve the same plant-level objective: "to know how much material is in the vessel."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bucket and the Bat"

Imagine you are standing at the top of a deep well and you shout "Hello" into it. The deeper the well, the longer it takes for the echo to return to you. **Ultrasonic level measurement works on this exact principle**, except we use a high-frequency sound pulse inaudible to the human ear (typically **20 kHz to 200 kHz**), and a single transducer acts as both the speaker and the listener.

For the **Resistance Tape**, picture a long ruler lowered into a swimming pool. The deeper the ruler goes, the more water presses against it at the bottom. A flexible tape coated with a resistance element uses this *water pressure* to stretch a sensing element and convert the mechanical stretch into a change in electrical resistance — analogous to how a deep-sea diver's pressure gauge needle moves further as they descend.

For the **Rotating Paddle Switch**, imagine a small ceiling fan spinning freely in air. The moment a solid object (like your hand) blocks the blade, the motor stalls, the current spikes, and a switch trips. The paddle switch is the "stall-detection fan" of the bulk-solids world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorise:**
> - Speed of sound in air at **20 °C**: **c = 343 m/s**
> - Speed of sound in air changes by approximately **0.6 m/s per °C**
> - Standard tape supply voltage: **24 V DC**
> - Rotating paddle supply: **24 V DC / 110/230 V AC**
> - Standard output: **4–20 mA** (continuous) or **SPDT relay contact** (paddle)

---

## 1.3 Why These Three Technologies? — KTU Relevance

These three methods are grouped together in Module 3 because they represent **non-contact, non-intrusive, and rugged** level-sensing philosophies that dominate chemical, petrochemical, water-treatment, and bulk-solid storage industries. They also illustrate three fundamentally different *transduction* principles:

1. **Ultrasonic** → Time-of-flight of mechanical waves.
2. **Resistance Tape** → Hydrostatic pressure → strain → resistance.
3. **Rotating Paddle** → Mechanical torque disruption (stall current).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Heuristic:** When answering a KTU 14-mark question on these three, students are expected to draw a labelled block diagram of *any one* of these and explain its working in **three stages**: Power/Excitation, Sensing, and Output Conditioning.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ultrasonic Level Measurement — Operational Logic

The ultrasonic level sensor is mounted at the **top** of the vessel, facing downward toward the material surface. It consists of a **piezoelectric transducer** that converts electrical pulses into mechanical (acoustic) vibrations and vice-versa (reversible transducer).

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Pulse Generation Stage:** A high-frequency pulse train (typically 4–16 pulses per burst, frequency 20–200 kHz) is generated by the electronics and applied to the piezoelectric crystal.
2. **Acoustic Emission Stage:** The crystal resonates, emitting an acoustic wavefront into the gas/vapour space above the material.
3. **Propagation Stage:** The wave travels through the air/vapour medium at the speed of sound *c* with attenuation governed by absorption and beam-spreading.
4. **Reflection Stage:** The wave strikes the **liquid or solid surface** and reflects back (echo) due to acoustic impedance mismatch.
5. **Reception Stage:** The same transducer (or a paired receiver) detects the returning echo.
6. **Time Measurement Stage:** The control electronics precisely measure the **time-of-flight** Δt.
7. **Distance Calculation Stage:** The half-distance (sensor to surface) is calculated, then the **level (L)** is obtained by subtracting this distance from the calibrated empty-tank distance.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why use pulses instead of continuous waves?** Pulses allow clear temporal separation between transmitted and reflected signals, eliminating the "lock-on" ambiguity of continuous systems.
- **Why is a piezoelectric element used?** It has extremely fast response (microseconds), high Q-factor resonance, and reversible operation.
- **Why is temperature compensation critical?** Because *c* is a function of temperature; a **1 °C error** can translate to a level error of roughly **0.17 %**.

---

## 2.2 Resistance Tape Level Measurement — Operational Logic

A **Resistance Tape** (also known as a *Hydrostatic Resistance Tape* or *Strain-Gauge Tape*) is a long, flexible, hermetically sealed strip containing a precisely wound **resistance wire** (or thin-film resistive element) bonded to a pressure-sensing diaphragm. It is suspended vertically into the tank from the top.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Installation Stage:** The tape hangs from the top; the lower tip rests near (but not at) the tank bottom. The tape length is chosen so its **active range** matches the tank's measurement span.
2. **Hydrostatic Loading Stage:** The liquid column at each depth *h* exerts hydrostatic pressure **P = ρgh** on the sensing diaphragms distributed along the tape.
3. **Strain Transfer Stage:** Each diaphragm deflects proportionally to the local pressure, straining the bonded resistance element.
4. **Resistance Change Stage:** Strain changes the wire length, cross-section, and resistivity, producing a ΔR according to the **gauge factor**.
5. **Wheatstone Bridge Stage:** The tape resistance is incorporated into a **Wheatstone bridge circuit** to convert ΔR into a millivolt signal.
6. **Signal Conditioning Stage:** The mV signal is amplified, linearised, and converted to a standard **4–20 mA** output corresponding to 0–100 % level.

### 2.2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why hang from the top instead of mounting at the bottom?** A top-hanging design avoids exposing the sensor body to sediment, sludge, and corrosive tank bottom conditions, and allows the sensor to be **retrieved without draining the tank**.
- **Why use distributed diaphragms?** The tape gives an *integrated* measurement, so disturbances like vortexing or local turbulence are naturally averaged out — a major advantage over single-point hydrostatic probes.
- **Why is temperature compensation required?** The tape resistance itself changes with temperature (self-heating and ambient), so a compensating resistor (dummy tape or RTD) is incorporated.

---

## 2.3 Rotating Paddle Switch — Operational Logic

A **Rotating Paddle Switch** (also called a *Rotating Vane Switch* or *Roto-Level Switch*) is a point-level detector used primarily for **dry bulk solids** such as grains, sand, plastic pellets, cement, fly-ash, and feed.

### 2.3.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

1. **Power-On Stage:** When energised, a small **synchronous AC motor** (typically 1–5 rpm) drives a paddle (with 1 to 4 vanes) via a low-reduction gear train.
2. **Free Rotation Stage:** With the paddle hanging in air above the material, the motor spins freely, drawing a low, *normal* running current (≈ 30–80 mA).
3. **Material Contact Stage:** When the bulk material rises and **submerges the paddle**, the vanes are physically blocked from rotating.
4. **Motor Stall Stage:** The motor cannot rotate, the **stall current rises sharply** (typically 5–10× the running current).
5. **Detection Circuit Stage:** The increased current triggers a relay driver circuit.
6. **Output Stage:** An **SPDT relay contact** (or solid-state equivalent) changes state, providing the "Material Present" alarm or pump cut-off signal.
7. **Auto-Reset (Optional):** When the material level falls, the paddle is free to rotate again, the current drops, and the relay resets.

### 2.3.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a low-rpm motor?** Slow rotation minimises mechanical wear, dust generation, and the kinetic impact on fragile bulk solids.
- **Why a stalled-motor principle?** It is fail-safe for *rising* level alarms (high-level cutoff in silos) — most common industrial application.
- **Why use a synchronous motor?** Synchronous motors develop a *constant torque* characteristic — important because stall torque is identical to running torque for a synchronous motor, ensuring a predictable stall-current ratio.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ultrasonic: Empty distance | $D_{empty} = H$ | H = total tank height (m) | Calibrated at commissioning |
| 2 | Ultrasonic: Level computation | $L = D_{empty} - \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | L = level (m), c = speed of sound (m/s), Δt = time-of-flight (s) | Core equation |
| 3 | Speed of sound in air | $c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | T = temperature in °C, c in m/s | Linear approximation |
| 4 | Speed of sound (general) | $c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot R \cdot T_K}{M}}$ | γ = ratio of specific heats, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T_K = absolute temperature (K), M = molar mass (kg/mol) | Universal gas form |
| 5 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | ρ = liquid density (kg/m³), g = 9.81 m/s², h = depth (m) | Foundation of tape |
| 6 | Resistance change of strain gauge | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | G = gauge factor (≈ 2 for metal), ε = strain (dimensionless) | Piezo-resistive principle |
| 7 | Wheatstone bridge output | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R}$ | V_ex = excitation voltage (V), ΔR = change in R (Ω) | Used in tape conditioning |
| 8 | Paddle stall detection | $I_{stall} \approx 5 \cdot I_{run}$ | I = motor current (A) | Empirical design rule |
| 9 | Motor torque (synchronous) | $T = \frac{P}{2 \pi \cdot N}$ | P = power (W), N = speed (rps) | Drives paddle design |
| 10 | Linearisation (4–20 mA) | $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{L}{L_{max}}$ | L = current level (m), L_max = full scale (m) | Output mapping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K Tip:** Equations 1–4 dominate the Ultrasonic problems; Equations 5–7 dominate the Resistance Tape problems; Equations 8–9 dominate the Paddle Switch problems. Memorise these and you cover ~90 % of the numerical portion.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ultrasonic:** Municipal water reservoirs, chemical storage tanks (corrosive liquids), open-channel flow measurement (weirs), slurry sumps.
- **Resistance Tape:** Bunker fuel tanks, large oil storage tanks, ship-board gauging, cooling-water ponds where accuracy and stability are critical.
- **Rotating Paddle:** Cement silos, grain silos, plastic pellet hoppers, fly-ash hoppers in thermal power plants, feed bins in animal-feed plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight (KTU Expectation):** When asked to "compare" these three methods, always state the **measurement type** (continuous vs. point), the **medium** (liquid vs. bulk solid), the **accuracy class**, the **installation cost**, and the **typical failure mode**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Level from Time-of-Flight

We will derive the master equation for an ultrasonic level sensor starting from basic kinematics.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let the sensor be located at the top of an open tank of total height **H** (measured from the bottom of the tank to the sensor face). Let the material surface be at a height **L** above the bottom. Then the *empty distance* between the sensor and the tank bottom is exactly **H**, and the *current distance* between the sensor and the material surface is:

$$
D = H - L
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The transducer emits an ultrasonic pulse at time t = 0.

**Step 2:** The pulse travels downward through the air/vapour column with velocity *c* (speed of sound). Time taken to reach the material surface:

$$
t_1 = \frac{D}{c} = \frac{H - L}{c}
$$

**Step 3:** The pulse reflects off the surface (acoustic impedance mismatch) and travels upward the same distance *D*. Time taken to return to the sensor:

$$
t_2 = \frac{D}{c} = \frac{H - L}{c}
$$

**Step 4:** The total time-of-flight measured by the electronics is:

$$
\Delta t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{2D}{c} = \frac{2(H - L)}{c}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by c/2:

$$
D = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the geometric relation D = H − L:

$$
H - L = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the level L (the final instrument output variable):

$$
L = H - \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Master Equation** for ultrasonic level measurement. The KTU board examiner expects this derivation verbatim in 14-mark questions.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted 8 m above the bottom of a water tank. The measured time-of-flight is 42 ms. The ambient air temperature is 30 °C. Determine the water level.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate speed of sound at 30 °C:

$$
c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 331.4 + 18 = 349.4 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate the empty distance-equivalent traversed by sound:

$$
D = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{349.4 \times 0.042}{2} = \frac{14.6748}{2} = 7.3374 \, \text{m}
$$

Step 3 — Calculate the level:

$$
L = H - D = 8 - 7.3374 = 0.6626 \, \text{m} \approx 0.66 \, \text{m}
$$

**Answer:** Water level ≈ **0.66 m** above the tank bottom.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resistance Tape Hydrostatic-to-Electrical Conversion

### 3.2.1 Setup

A resistance tape is suspended from the top of a tank containing a liquid of density **ρ**. The tape extends from the top (z = 0) to a depth **H_t** at its lower tip. The active sensor is a strain gauge bonded to a pressure diaphragm at the tip. A second (compensating) reference resistor is shielded from pressure.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The hydrostatic pressure at the sensor depth is:

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h
$$

where *h* is the *head* of liquid above the sensor (i.e., the level L measured from the sensor mounting point). So:

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot L
$$

**Step 2:** This pressure deflects the diaphragm by a small displacement δ. For a circular diaphragm of radius *a* and thickness *t* with Young's modulus *E*, the maximum centre deflection is:

$$
\delta = \frac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}}
$$

where **ν** is Poisson's ratio.

**Step 3:** The deflection strains the bonded strain gauge of length **l_g** and cross-section **A_g**:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta l_g}{l_g} = k_1 \cdot \delta
$$

where *k_1* is a geometric coupling constant.

**Step 4:** The fractional change in resistance (gauge equation) is:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = G \cdot k_1 \cdot \delta
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting δ from Step 2:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot k_1 \cdot \frac{3 P a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}} = K \cdot P
$$

where the lumped constant **K** is:

$$
K = \frac{3 G k_1 a^{4} (1 - \nu^{2})}{16 E t^{3}}
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting P = ρgL:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = K \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot L
$$

**Step 7:** Inserting this ΔR into a quarter-bridge configuration, the bridge output voltage is:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot K \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot L
$$

**Step 8:** Solving for L (the level), we obtain the calibration equation:

$$
L = \frac{4 V_o}{V_{ex} \cdot K \cdot \rho \cdot g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The *linearity* of this method is its major advantage. Notice that L is *directly* proportional to the bridge output voltage V_o — making the calibration essentially one-point-and-scale.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A resistance tape is installed in a fuel-oil tank (ρ = 850 kg/m³). With empty tank, V_o = 0 mV. With full tank (L = 10 m), V_o = 100 mV. Excitation V_ex = 10 V. Calculate the level when V_o = 47 mV.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Establish the linear calibration from full-scale data. The scaling constant *m* (mV per metre) is:

$$
m = \frac{100 \, \text{mV}}{10 \, \text{m}} = 10 \, \text{mV/m}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate the level:

$$
L = \frac{V_o}{m} = \frac{47 \, \text{mV}}{10 \, \text{mV/m}} = 4.7 \, \text{m}
$$

**Answer:** Level = **4.7 m**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rotating Paddle Stall Torque & Current

### 3.3.1 Setup

A synchronous AC motor drives a paddle at angular speed **ω** through a gear train. The motor's mechanical output power **P** is fixed. The paddle has radius **r** and is immersed in a bulk solid of effective density **ρ_s**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The output mechanical power is related to torque T and angular velocity ω:

$$
P = T \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 2:** For a synchronous motor, the torque is independent of speed (constant-torque characteristic), so:

$$
T = \frac{P}{\omega}
$$

**Step 3:** When the paddle is free in air, the load torque T_load ≈ 0, so all developed torque accelerates the rotor (and overcomes bearing friction T_f). The current drawn is:

$$
I_{run} = \frac{P}{V \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta}
$$

**Step 4:** When material submerges the paddle, the *drag* torque suddenly becomes very large. The motor stalls (ω → 0). At stall, the entire electrical input is dissipated as heat; the current becomes:

$$
I_{stall} = \frac{P}{V \cdot \cos\phi_{stall} \cdot \eta_{stall}}
$$

**Step 5:** Because η drops drastically at stall and cosφ_stall ≈ 1 for a synchronous motor's blocked-rotor condition, we get the empirical rule:

$$
I_{stall} \approx 5 \cdot I_{run}
$$

**Step 6:** The detection circuit uses a current-sensing resistor (or CT) to monitor the motor current. A threshold detector (e.g., set at 2.5 × I_run) trips the output relay when I exceeds the threshold.

### 3.3.3 Design Equation Summary

$$
T_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} C_d \rho_s A_{paddle} r^{2} \omega^{2}
$$

where **C_d** is the drag coefficient of the paddle in the bulk material, **A_paddle** is the projected area of the vanes, and **r** is the paddle radius.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expectation:** For a 14-mark answer, you must mention at least three of: synchronous motor, low rpm, stall current, SPDT relay output, and bulk-solid application.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Board Favourite)

| Parameter | Ultrasonic | Resistance Tape | Rotating Paddle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Measurement type** | Continuous | Continuous | Point (discrete) |
| **Output** | 4–20 mA / HART | 4–20 mA / 0–10 V | SPDT relay |
| **Typical accuracy** | ±0.25 % to ±0.5 % FS | ±0.1 % FS | N/A (alarm only) |
| **Best for medium** | Liquids, slurries, open channels | Liquids in large tanks | Dry bulk solids |
| **Contact with medium** | No (non-contact) | Yes (wetted) | Yes (immersed paddle) |
| **Temperature limit** | Up to 150 °C (with cooling) | Up to 200 °C | Up to 200 °C (motor) |
| **Pressure limit** | Atmospheric (top mounted) | Up to 100 bar | Atmospheric |
| **Min dielectric / conductivity requirement** | None (uses sound) | None (uses hydrostatic) | Bulk must be flowable |
| **Failure mode** | Foam can absorb sound | Tape rupture on corrosion | Motor burnout if stalled too long |
| **Cost** | Medium | High (precision tape) | Low |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Clock and Pulse Generator"] --> B["Transmitter Driver"]
    B --> C["Piezoelectric Transducer"]

    D --> E["Liquid or Solid Surface"]
    E --> F["Reflected Echo Returns to Transducer"]
    F --> G["Receiver Amplifier"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The clock simultaneously starts the pulse generator (to fire the transducer) and starts the time-of-flight counter. When the echo is received, the counter stops. The microcontroller then computes *L* using the temperature-compensated *c*.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Level Measurement." Differentiate between point level and continuous level measurement with one industrial example each.*

**Model Answer:**
Level measurement is the determination of the position of the surface of a material (liquid, slurry, or bulk solid) relative to a reference datum in a vessel.

- **Point Level:** Detects whether the material is at a preset height (on/off). Example: Float switch in a sump pump.
- **Continuous Level:** Provides a proportional analog output across the full range of the vessel. Example: Ultrasonic level transmitter in a chemical storage tank.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the three main components of an ultrasonic level measurement system. State one limitation of the ultrasonic method.*

**Model Answer:**
The three main components are:
1. **Piezoelectric Transducer** (combined transmitter/receiver).
2. **Electronic Control Unit** (pulse generator, time-of-flight counter, microcontroller).
3. **Output Interface** (4–20 mA loop, HART modem, or display).

**Limitation:** Foam on the liquid surface can absorb the ultrasonic pulse, causing signal loss and erroneous readings.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students lose **3 to 4 marks** in 14-mark questions by omitting the *formula derivation* or by drawing the block diagram without *labelled signal flow arrows*. Always include both!

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** In numerical problems, students often forget to apply **temperature compensation** to the speed of sound. This is the single most common calculation error.

### Question B1 (A): Continuous Level Methods — Ultrasonic

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the working principle of an **ultrasonic level measurement system** used for continuous level monitoring. Derive the master equation relating level *L*, time-of-flight *Δt*, speed of sound *c*, and tank height *H*. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An ultrasonic sensor is installed 6 m above the bottom of a water tank. The time-of-flight is 30 ms, and the air temperature is 40 °C. Calculate (i) the speed of sound, (ii) the empty distance, and (iii) the water level. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1).**

**Working Principle:**
The system has a piezoelectric transducer mounted at the top of the vessel. The electronic unit generates a high-frequency electrical pulse train. The transducer converts it into acoustic pulses that travel down through the air. The pulses strike the material surface and reflect back. The same transducer (or a paired one) receives the echo. The control unit measures the round-trip time-of-flight Δt. Using the geometric relation between tank height, empty distance, and level, the level L is computed.

**Derivation:**

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2D}{c} = \frac{2(H - L)}{c}
$$

Solving for L:

$$
L = H - \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Speed of sound at 40 °C:**

$$
c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot 40 = 331.4 + 24 = 355.4 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**(ii) Empty distance traversed by sound:**

$$
D = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{355.4 \times 0.030}{2} = \frac{10.662}{2} = 5.331 \, \text{m}
$$

**(iii) Water level:**

$$
L = H - D = 6 - 5.331 = 0.669 \, \text{m} \approx 0.67 \, \text{m}
$$

**Answer:** Level ≈ **0.67 m**.

---

### Question B1 (B): Continuous Level Methods — Resistance Tape & Paddle

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the construction and working of a **resistance tape level sensor**. State two advantages and one limitation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the working of a **rotating paddle switch** with a neat sketch. Why is a synchronous motor preferred? Give two industrial applications. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction:** A resistance tape consists of a long, narrow, hermetically sealed stainless-steel strip containing a precision resistance wire bonded to small pressure-sensing diaphragms distributed along its length. A reference (dummy) resistance element shielded from pressure is included for temperature compensation. A 24 V DC excitation is applied across the bridge.

**Working:** Hydrostatic pressure *P = ρgh* deflects the diaphragms. The strain is transferred to the bonded resistance wire, changing its resistance by ΔR. The tape forms one arm of a Wheatstone bridge, producing a millivolt output proportional to the level. This mV signal is amplified, linearised, and converted to a 4–20 mA output.

**Advantages:**
1. High accuracy (±0.1 % FS) and excellent linearity.
2. Sensor can be retrieved from the top without draining the tank.

**Limitation:** The tape is in contact with the liquid, so corrosion and fouling can be a concern in aggressive media.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Working (refer to Section 4.3 for the block diagram):**
A low-rpm synchronous motor (1–5 rpm) drives a paddle via a gear train. When the paddle hangs free in air, the motor draws a low "running" current. When the bulk solid rises and submerges the paddle, the drag torque stops the motor. The stalled motor draws a "stall" current about 5× higher than the running current. A current-sensing circuit detects this surge and trips an SPDT relay, providing an alarm or pump cut-off signal. When the material falls, the paddle is free to rotate, the current drops, and the relay resets.

**Why synchronous motor?**
- Constant-torque characteristic makes stall detection predictable.
- Reliable, maintenance-free operation (no brushes in some designs).
- Low rpm ensures minimal wear and dust generation.

**Industrial Applications:**
1. Cement silo high-level alarm.
2. Fly-ash hopper low-level trip in a thermal power plant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and review one day before the exam!**

- [x] **Ultrasonic** uses *time-of-flight* of sound pulses; formula **L = H − (c·Δt)/2**.
- [x] **Speed of sound** in air: **c = 331.4 + 0.6 T °C** (m/s). Temperature compensation is mandatory.
- [x] **Resistance Tape** uses *hydrostatic pressure* **P = ρgh** → strain → resistance change.
- [x] **Wheatstone bridge** converts ΔR to a mV signal; output linearised to 4–20 mA.
- [x] **Rotating Paddle** is a *point-level* device using *stall-current* detection.
- [x] **Synchronous motor** gives predictable stall-torque characteristics.
- [x] **Typical outputs:** Ultrasonic & Tape → 4–20 mA; Paddle → SPDT relay contact.
- [x] **Best media:** Ultrasonic → liquids & slurries; Tape → liquids in large tanks; Paddle → dry bulk solids.
- [x] **Key limitations:** Foam (ultrasonic), corrosion (tape), motor burnout (paddle).
