- [x] **Common KTU error:** Forgetting to halve the time-of-flight. Always remember the **2 in the denominator**.
- [x] **Accuracy order:** Resistance Tape > Ultrasonic >> Rotating Paddle (point only).
- [x] **Industrial brands to mention (optional):** Siemens SITRANS, Endress+Hauser, Vega, Honeywell — these are the actual manufacturers students will encounter in the field.
- [x] **Revision tip:** Be ready to draw a *labelled block diagram* of any one of the three within 3 minutes — this single answer can fetch 7 marks!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotational
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT402 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rotational

# 🔧 Rotational Measurements in Industrial Instrumentation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rotational measurement** is the branch of industrial instrumentation concerned with the precise quantification of parameters associated with rotating machinery, including **rotational speed (N)**, **angular velocity (ω)**, **angular acceleration (α)**, **torque (T)**, **shaft power (P)**, and **angular position/displacement (θ)**. These parameters are essential for monitoring, controlling, and diagnosing rotating equipment such as turbines, motors, gearboxes, pumps, and machine tool spindles.

The fundamental rotational kinematic relationships are:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha = \frac{d\omega}{dt} \text{ rad/s}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 focuses on *transducers for rotational quantities* — specifically mechanical, electrical, and electronic tachometers; torque measurement methods; and shaft power computation. Students must know operating principles, derivations, characteristic equations, and selection criteria.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Speedometer of a Car**
> Imagine driving a car: the speedometer needle indicates how *fast* the wheels rotate (rotational speed). The odometer tracks *how far* the wheels have turned (angular displacement). Meanwhile, the engine's "torque" is what twists the wheels, producing acceleration. Together, speed × force = power delivered to the road.
> In industry, **rotational transducers** are the "speedometers, odometers, and force gauges" of every motor, turbine, and gearbox. Without them, we cannot know if a centrifugal pump is running at 2950 rpm or 1450 rpm — a critical distinction for pump affinity laws.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Engineering Unit |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| Rotational speed | $N$ | rad/s | **rpm (revolutions per minute)** |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | rad/s | rad/s |
| Torque | $T$ | N·m | N·m |
| Moment of inertia | $J$ | kg·m² | kg·m² |
| Shaft power | $P$ | W (watt) | **W, kW, HP (1 HP = 745.7 W)** |
| Angular acceleration | $\alpha$ | rad/s² | rad/s² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Rotational Transducers

Rotational measurement systems are broadly classified into three functional families:

1. **Speed/Angular Velocity Sensors (Tachometers)** — Output a signal proportional to $\omega$ or $N$.
2. **Torque Sensors** — Measure the twisting moment transmitted through a shaft.
3. **Angular Position/Displacement Sensors** — Encoders, resolvers, synchros, and Inductosyns.

> [!TIP]
> **Why three families?** Because in a rotating system, you need to know *how fast* (speed), *how forcefully* (torque), and *where* (position). The product of speed × torque = power, which is the final quantity most industrial users care about.

---

## 2.2 Mechanical Tachometers

### 2.2.1 Centrifugal (Governor-type) Tachometer

A centrifugal tachometer uses the **centrifugal force** developed on flyweights pivoted on a rotating spindle. As speed increases, the flyweights swing outward against a spring, and this displacement is mechanically linked to an indicating needle.

**Operating Principle:**

$$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

where $m$ = mass of flyweight, $r$ = radius of rotation at equilibrium, $\omega$ = angular velocity.

The spring force balances the centrifugal component:
$$k \cdot x = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

**Pointer deflection is proportional to $\omega^2$** — a non-linear (square-law) characteristic.

### 2.2.2 Drag-Cup Totorque Tachometer (Eddy-Current Type)

A permanent magnet (or electromagnet) rotates inside a non-magnetic conducting drag cup (usually aluminium). The relative motion induces eddy currents in the cup, which interact with the magnetic field to produce a **torque proportional to speed**.

$$T_{drag} = K \cdot \omega$$

The cup is held against a calibrated spring; the angular twist of the cup (relative to the magnet) is indicated on a dial. This is a **direct-reading speedometer**.

### 2.2.3 Friction/Cronometer Tachometer (Revolution Counter)

Counts the number of revolutions over a fixed time interval using a gear train and stop-watch mechanism. The accumulated count divided by time yields speed in rpm.

---

## 2.3 Electrical Tachometers

### 2.3.1 DC Tachogenerator

A **permanent-magnet DC generator** coupled to the rotating shaft. The output EMF is given by Faraday's law:

$$E = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$

where $K$ = machine constant, $\phi$ = flux per pole, $\omega$ = angular velocity. The terminal voltage is read by a voltmeter calibrated directly in rpm.

**Characteristics:**
- Linear output voltage vs. speed.
- Direction of rotation reverses polarity → can indicate direction.
- **Ripple** in output must be smoothed (filter capacitor or interpoles).
- Used in servo systems for feedback.

### 2.3.2 AC Tachogenerator (Induction Tachometer)

A two-phase induction motor with one phase excited by a fixed AC reference and the other phase providing the speed-proportional output. The rotor is a thin aluminium cup (drag-cup rotor) which acts like a shorted secondary.

$$V_{out} = K \cdot \omega \cdot V_{ref}$$

**Advantages:** No brushes, low maintenance, no commutator ripple.
**Disadvantage:** Phase-sensitive — requires phase-sensitive demodulation.

### 2.3.3 Eddy-Current (Drag-Pickup) Tachometer

A non-contact probe senses the variation in magnetic reluctance as ferrous gear teeth pass by. Each tooth produces one pulse:

$$f = \frac{N \cdot Z}{60} \text{ Hz}$$

where $N$ = shaft rpm, $Z$ = number of teeth. The frequency is converted to a DC voltage via frequency-to-voltage (F/V) converter.

---

## 2.4 Electronic Tachometers

### 2.4.1 Magnetic Pickup (Variable Reluctance)

A permanent magnet with a wound coil is placed near a toothed wheel mounted on the shaft. As teeth pass the pole-piece, the flux alternates, inducing an alternating EMF of frequency:

$$f = \frac{N \cdot Z}{60}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Favourite:** Derivation of the frequency equation for a toothed-wheel magnetic pickup appears frequently in the ESE. Memorize the units conversion: 1 rev = $Z$ pulses, so pulses per second = $N \cdot Z / 60$.

### 2.4.2 Photoelectric (Optical) Tachometer

A slotted disc mounted on the shaft interrupts a light beam falling on a photodiode/phototransistor. Each slot generates one electrical pulse.

**Advantage:** Independent of material; works with any opaque disc.
**Disadvantage:** Sensitive to dust and contamination on the optical path.

### 2.4.3 Stroboscopic Tachometer

A gas-discharge lamp (Xenon) is flashed at an adjustable, known frequency. When the flash rate equals the rotation rate, the rotating object *appears stationary* (stroboscopic effect). This is a **non-contact** method and is exceptionally accurate for high-speed shafts.

$$N_{shaft} = 60 \cdot f_{flash} \text{ rpm}$$

or for pattern with $k$ events per revolution:

$$N_{shaft} = \frac{60 \cdot f_{flash}}{k} \text{ rpm}$$

### 2.4.4 Encoder-Based Tachometer

Quadrature encoders produce two square-wave outputs (A and B channels) phase-shifted by 90°. Speed is computed by:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot f_{pulse}}{P}$$

where $P$ = pulses per revolution.

---

## 2.5 Torque Measurement

### 2.5.1 Mechanical Methods (Absorption Dynamometers)

**Prony Brake:** A friction band clamped around a rotating pulley; the reaction force is measured by a spring balance. The torque is:

$$T = (F_1 - F_2) \cdot R \cdot g$$

where $F_1, F_2$ are spring balance readings (kgf) and $R$ is the effective pulley radius.

**Rope Brake:** Similar to Prony, but uses rope wrapped around the brake drum with tightening bolts.

**Hydraulic Dynamometer:** A water-filled chamber with rotating vanes acts as a controlled hydraulic load. The torque is:

$$T = F \cdot L$$

where $F$ = force on the dynamometer arm, $L$ = arm length.

**Eddy-Current Dynamometer:** A non-contact method using induced eddy currents in a rotor to provide a controllable retarding torque proportional to excitation current. Widely used in engine test beds.

### 2.5.2 Electrical Methods (Transmission Dynamometers)

**Strain-Gauge Torque Sensor:** Four strain gauges bonded to a shaft (typically arranged in a full-Wheatstone-bridge at 45° to the shaft axis, the principal stress directions for pure torsion). The shaft twists by an angle $\theta$ under torque $T$:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot G}$$

where $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ is the polar moment of inertia, $G$ is the shear modulus, $L$ is the gauge length.

The strain $\varepsilon$ measured by the gauge relates to torque via:

$$T = \frac{\pi \cdot G \cdot d^3 \cdot \varepsilon}{16}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Slip-ring vs. Rotary Transformer Telemetry:** For rotating shafts, the strain-gauge signal must be transmitted off the shaft. Slip-rings introduce noise; rotary transformers or wireless telemetry are preferred in modern systems.

**Magnetoelastic Torque Sensor:** Measures the change in permeability of ferromagnetic material under stress (Villari effect). Non-contact; rugged.

---

## 2.6 Shaft Power Calculation

Once $T$ and $N$ are measured, shaft power follows directly:

$$P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T \quad \text{[with N in rev/s, T in N·m, P in W]}$$

or equivalently:

$$P = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} \quad \text{[with N in rpm]}$$

In HP:

$$P_{HP} = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60 \cdot 745.7}$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Conditions | Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ | $N$ = rpm | rad/s |
| 2 | Angular acceleration | $\alpha = \frac{d\omega}{dt}$ | — | rad/s² |
| 3 | Centrifugal force | $F_c = m r \omega^2$ | $m$ = mass, $r$ = radius | N |
| 4 | Drag-cup torque | $T = K \omega$ | Linear in $\omega$ | N·m |
| 5 | DC tachogenerator EMF | $E = K \phi \omega$ | $K$ = machine constant | V |
| 6 | Magnetic-pickup frequency | $f = \frac{N Z}{60}$ | $Z$ = number of teeth | Hz |
| 7 | Stroboscope speed | $N = \frac{60 f_{flash}}{k}$ | $k$ = events/rev | rpm |
| 8 | Prony brake torque | $T = (F_1 - F_2) R g$ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | N·m |
| 9 | Torsional twist | $\theta = \frac{T L}{J G}$ | $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | rad |
| 10 | Polar moment of inertia | $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $d$ = shaft diameter | m⁴ |
| 11 | Shaft power | $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m | W |
| 12 | Power in HP | $P_{HP} = \frac{P_W}{745.7}$ | 1 HP = 745.7 W | HP |
| 13 | Encoder speed | $\omega = \frac{2\pi f_{pulse}}{P}$ | $P$ = pulses/rev | rad/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Pitfall:** Mixing rpm and rad/s is the most common source of errors in KTU numerical problems. Always convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) by multiplying by $2\pi/60$ **before** substituting into equations.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Power plants:** Stroboscopic and eddy-current pickups monitor turbine speeds (3000/3600 rpm).
- **Automotive test cells:** Eddy-current dynamometers measure engine torque–speed maps.
- **CNC machine tools:** Optical encoders provide closed-loop position feedback for spindle and servo axes.
- **Pumps and compressors:** Magnetic pickups trigger anti-surge control loops.
- **Aerospace:** Magnetoelastic torque sensors in helicopter rotor shafts (no slip-rings, high reliability).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of a DC Tachogenerator

A DC tachogenerator is essentially a DC generator with a constant field flux $\phi$ produced by permanent magnets. The EMF induced in the armature follows from Faraday's law applied to each conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a flux density $B$:

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot v \text{ (per conductor)}$$

For a total of $Z$ armature conductors connected in $A$ parallel paths, the total EMF is:

$$E = \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot N_{cond}}{60 \cdot A}$$

Recognizing that linear velocity at the armature periphery is $v = \pi D n / 60$ where $n$ is in rps, and that the angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot P \cdot n}{A} \\
&= \left(\frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot P}{2\pi A}\right) \cdot (2\pi n) \\
&= K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega
\end{aligned}$$

where $K = \frac{Z P}{2\pi A}$ is the **machine construction constant**, $P$ = number of poles, and $\omega$ is in rad/s.

**Key conclusion:** $E \propto \omega$ — the output voltage is directly proportional to the angular velocity. A DC voltmeter across the terminals can be calibrated linearly in rpm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** In KTU board papers, you may be asked to comment on the *linearity* of this relationship. Mention that the constant $\phi$ is maintained by permanent magnets (no field current variation), so linearity is good within the demagnetization limit. Above a certain speed, armature reaction and saturation introduce non-linearity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency of a Magnetic Pickup

Consider a ferromagnetic toothed wheel of $Z$ teeth mounted on the shaft rotating at $N$ rpm. A pickup coil is positioned such that the air-gap flux changes once per tooth passage.

**Step 1 — Revolutions per second:**
$$n = \frac{N}{60} \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2 — Teeth passing per second (one tooth per cycle of flux change):**
$$f = n \cdot Z$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**
$$f = \frac{N \cdot Z}{60} \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging for shaft speed:**
$$N = \frac{60 \cdot f}{Z} \text{ rpm}$$

This is the canonical magnetic-pickup equation. The amplitude of the induced pulse is given by:

$$e = -N_c \frac{d\phi}{dt} = -N_c \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot \omega_{tooth} \cdot \cos(\omega_{tooth} t)$$

where $\omega_{tooth} = 2\pi f$ is the electrical angular frequency and $N_c$ is the number of turns of the pickup coil.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Torsional Strain in a Shaft Under Torque

Consider a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ and length $L$ subjected to a pure torque $T$ at one end (other end fixed).

**Step 1 — Shear stress at the surface (pure torsion):**
The polar moment of inertia of a solid circular cross-section:
$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

The maximum shear stress at the surface:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4/32} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

**Step 2 — Shear strain at the surface (Hooke's law in shear):**
$$\gamma = \frac{\tau_{max}}{G} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3 G}$$

**Step 3 — Principal strains (45° rosettes on the surface):**
Under pure shear $\tau$, the principal stresses are $\pm \tau$. The corresponding principal strains are:
$$\varepsilon_1 = -\varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau_{max}}{2G} (1 + \nu) = \frac{8 T (1+\nu)}{\pi d^3 G}$$

**Step 4 — Strain-gauge arrangement:**
A strain gauge bonded at 45° to the shaft axis measures the principal tensile strain $\varepsilon_1$ directly.

**Step 5 — Solving for torque:**
$$T = \frac{\pi d^3 G}{8 (1+\nu)} \cdot \varepsilon_1$$

If Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.3$ for steel, $1+\nu = 1.3$, and with $G \approx 80$ GPa for steel:

$$T \approx \frac{\pi d^3 \cdot 80 \times 10^9}{8 \cdot 1.3} \cdot \varepsilon_1 \text{ (in N·m when d is in m)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Tip:** A full Wheatstone bridge with four active gauges (two at +45°, two at −45°, all on the shaft) **doubles the sensitivity** and **provides temperature compensation** automatically. This is the standard industrial torque-cell configuration.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Guide for Tachometers

| Application | Recommended Tachometer | Justification |
|-------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Portable hand-held measurements | Stroboscopic / mechanical hand-tach | No shaft modification, battery-powered |
| Industrial fixed monitoring (50–5000 rpm) | Magnetic pickup + F/V converter | Rugged, contactless, inexpensive |
| Servo-system feedback (DC motor) | DC tachogenerator | Linear, bidirectional, fast response |
| High-precision CNC spindle | Optical incremental encoder | Sub-arcsecond resolution, quadrature |
| Hazardous / explosive area | Eddy-current or magnetic pickup | Intrinsically safe (no sparking) |
| Very high speed (>50,000 rpm) | Stroboscope or electronic frequency counter | No mechanical contact wear |
| Continuous duty, brushless preferred | AC induction tachogenerator | No commutator, long life |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A shaft of diameter 50 mm is transmitting torque. Four strain gauges (gauge factor 2.1) are mounted at 45° in a full bridge. The bridge excitation is 10 V. If the bridge output is 12 mV, calculate: (a) the strain, (b) the torque, given $G = 80$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Strain:**
For a four-arm active bridge with all gauges experiencing strain $\varepsilon$ (two tensile, two compressive), the output is:
$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $G_f$ = gauge factor. Solving for strain:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ex} \cdot G_f} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 2.1} = 5.714 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Part (b) — Torque:**
$$T = \frac{\pi d^3 G}{8 (1+\nu)} \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting $d = 0.05$ m:
$$d^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$T = \frac{\pi \cdot (1.25 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (80 \times 10^9)}{8 \cdot 1.3} \cdot (5.714 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{\pi \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 80 \times 10^9}{10.4} \cdot 5.714 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= \frac{3.1416 \cdot 10^7}{10.4} \cdot 5.714 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 3.0208 \times 10^6 \cdot 5.714 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 1726 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification with shaft-power check:** If $N = 1500$ rpm and $T = 1726$ N·m, then $P = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1500 \cdot 1726}{60} \approx 271$ kW — reasonable for an industrial drive shaft.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Rotational Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotating Shaft\nSource of Motion] --> B[Sensor / Transducer\nMechanical / Electrical / Optical]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning\nAmplifier / Filter / F-V Converter]
    C --> D[Display / Recorder\nAnalog / Digital Indicator]
    C --> E[Controller / PLC\nFeedback Loop]
    E --> F[Actuator / Drive\nMotor / Brake]
    F --> A
    D -.Optional.-> G[Data Logger / SCADA]
    E -.Optional.-> G
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: DC Tachogenerator Speed Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prime Mover\nor Motor Shaft] --> B[DC Tachogenerator\nPermanent Magnet Field]
    B --> C[Commutator and Brushes]
    C --> D[Filter Capacitor\nRipple Smoothing]
    D --> E[Voltmeter\nCalibrated in rpm]
    E --> F[Speed Indication\nto Operator / Controller]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Magnetic Pickup Tachometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotating Shaft] --> B[Toothed Wheel\nZ teeth]
    B --> C[Magnetic Pickup\nPermanent Magnet + Coil]
    C --> D[Preamplifier\nPulse Shaping]
    D --> E[Frequency-to-Voltage\nConverter F-V]
    E --> F[Voltmeter\nCalibrated in rpm]
    F --> G[Linear Speed Output]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Strain-Gauge Torque Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Source\nMotor / Engine] --> B[Drive Shaft\nWith Strain Gauges]
    B --> C[Slip Rings\nor Rotary Transformer\nor Telemetry]
    C --> D[Wheatstone Bridge\nFull Active 4-Gauge]
    D --> E[Differential Amplifier\nInstrumentation Amp]
    E --> F[ADC\nAnalog to Digital]
    F --> G[Microcontroller / DSP\nTorque and Power Calculation]
    G --> H[Display HMI\nor SCADA]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Eddy-Current Dynamometer Test Bed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Under Test] --> B[Coupling Flange]
    B --> C[Rotating Rotor\nwith Field Coil]
    C --> D[Stationary Stator\nForce Transducer on Arm]
    D --> E[Load Cell / Force Sensor]
    E --> F[Strain Indicator\nTorque T = F x L]
    B --> G[Speed Pickup\nMagnetic or Optical]
    G --> H[Tachometer Display\nrpm]
    F --> I[Power Computer\nP = 2 pi N T / 60]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Performance Map\nTorque vs Speed]
```

## 4.6 Functional Architecture: Comparison of Speed Measurement Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Contact_Methods [Contact Methods]
        C1[DC Tachogenerator\nLinear, Bidirectional]
        C2[AC Tachogenerator\nBrushless]
        C3[Mechanical Tachometer\nFriction / Chronometric]
    end
    subgraph NonContact_Methods [Non-Contact Methods]
        N1[Magnetic Pickup\nFrequency Output]
        N2[Optical Encoder\nQuadrature Pulses]
        N3[Eddy Current Drag\nCup Tachometer]
        N4[Stroboscope\nFlashing Light]
    end
    subgraph Derived_Methods [Derived from Position Encoders]
        D1[Incremental Encoder\n+ Counter]
        D2[Absolute Encoder\n+ Decoder]
    end
    Shaft[Rotating Shaft] --> Contact_Methods
    Shaft --> NonContact_Methods
    Shaft --> Derived_Methods
    Contact_Methods --> Display1[Analog or Digital Indicator]
    NonContact_Methods --> Display2[Frequency or Voltage Indicator]
    Derived_Methods --> Display3[Digital Counter Display]
```

## 4.7 Block Diagram: Prony Brake (Mechanical Torque Measurement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Pulley\nRadius R] --> B[Friction Band\nClamped on Drum]
    B --> C[Spring Balance F1]
    B --> D[Tightening Bolt]
    B --> E[Lower Spring Balance F2]
    C --> F[Subtraction F1 minus F2]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Multiply by R and g\nT = F1 - F2 R g]
    G --> H[Torque Output N m]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of a stroboscopic tachometer. State two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

A stroboscopic tachometer measures rotational speed by illuminating a rotating object with a flashing light whose frequency is **adjustable**. The flash rate is varied until the rotating object **appears stationary** to the observer. This occurs when the flash period equals an integer multiple of the rotation period, i.e.,

$$N = \frac{60 \cdot f_{flash}}{k}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

where $N$ is in rpm, $f_{flash}$ is the flash frequency, and $k$ is the number of stable images per revolution. The lamp is a xenon flash tube, and frequency is set by a precision oscillator.

**Advantages:**
1. **Non-contact measurement** — no mechanical loading or wear on the shaft.
2. **Suitable for very high speeds** (>50,000 rpm) where contact methods are impractical.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**List any three types of tachometers and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Tachometer Type | Distinguishing Feature | Application |
|---|-----------------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **DC Tachogenerator** | Linear voltage output, brush-type | Servo-motor speed feedback in CNC machines |
| 2 | **Magnetic Pickup** | Frequency output via toothed wheel | Industrial conveyor and pump speed monitoring |
| 3 | **Stroboscopic** | Non-contact, flashing-light principle | High-speed turbine rotor balancing in power plants |

---

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is offered within the module.

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a DC tachogenerator. Derive the relationship between output voltage and angular velocity. Mention its limitations.

**Model Solution:**

A DC tachogenerator is a small permanent-magnet DC generator coupled to the rotating shaft whose speed is to be measured. The permanent magnets produce a constant flux $\phi$ per pole. As the armature rotates in this flux, an EMF is generated which is proportional to the angular velocity.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Prime Mover / Motor Shaft]
        |
        v
[DC Tachogenerator — Permanent Magnet Field]
        |
        v
[Brushes & Commutator]
        |
        v
[Filter Capacitor (Ripple Smoothing)]
        |
        v
[DC Voltmeter — Calibrated in rpm]
```

**Derivation:**

Let the armature have $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, and $A$ parallel paths. The flux per pole is $\phi$. As derived in Section 3.1:

$$E = \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot P \cdot n}{A}$$

With $\omega = 2\pi n$:

$$E = \frac{\phi \cdot Z \cdot P}{2\pi A} \cdot \omega = K \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$$

where $K = \frac{Z P}{2\pi A}$ is the **machine constant**.

**Result:** The output EMF $E$ is **directly proportional** to angular velocity $\omega$. A voltmeter across the armature terminals can therefore be graduated linearly in rpm.

**Limitations:**
1. **Brush and commutator wear** — requires periodic maintenance; introduces noise.
2. **Output ripple** — due to commutation; needs capacitive filtering.
3. **Armature reaction** at high speeds causes non-linearity and possible demagnetization of the permanent magnets.
4. **Temperature drift** affects permanent-magnet flux and hence calibration.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A magnetic pickup is fitted to monitor a 12-tooth wheel mounted on a motor shaft. If the pickup output frequency is measured as 240 Hz, calculate the shaft speed in rpm. If the motor is also developing a torque of 75 N·m, find the shaft power in kW and HP.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Z = 12$ teeth, $f = 240$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Shaft speed:**
$$N = \frac{60 \cdot f}{Z} = \frac{60 \cdot 240}{12} = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to rev/s for power computation:**
$$n = \frac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft power:**
$$P = 2\pi \cdot n \cdot T = 2\pi \cdot 20 \cdot 75 = 3000\pi \approx 9424.78 \text{ W}$$

$$P \approx 9.42 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to HP:**
$$P_{HP} = \frac{9424.78}{745.7} \approx 12.64 \text{ HP}$$

**Answer:** Shaft speed = **1200 rpm**; Power = **9.42 kW ≈ 12.64 HP**.

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the working of a strain-gauge torque sensor mounted on a rotating shaft. Explain how the four-gauge Wheatstone bridge provides temperature compensation and doubled sensitivity.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** Four identical strain gauges are bonded onto the surface of a rotating shaft, oriented at **+45° and −45° to the shaft axis** — these are the principal stress directions for pure torsion. They are connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** with **all four arms active**.

**Working Principle:**

When torque $T$ is applied, the shaft twists. The +45° gauges experience **tensile principal strain** $\varepsilon$, and the −45° gauges experience **compressive principal strain** $-\varepsilon$ of equal magnitude.

For an active full-bridge configuration, the output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$$

With the gauges arranged such that two tensile and two compressive of equal magnitude $\varepsilon$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon - (-\varepsilon) + \varepsilon - (-\varepsilon)) = 4 \cdot V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Doubled sensitivity (×2 over half-bridge):** Active full bridge produces 4× the output of a single gauge and 2× the output of a half-bridge with two active gauges.

**Temperature compensation (automatic):** When ambient temperature changes, all four gauges on the same shaft undergo the *same* thermal expansion. The bridge terms (tensile − tensile) cancel because both gauges drift equally and oppositely. Only the *differential* strain (induced by torque) survives — thermal strain is rejected.

**Signal transmission:** The bridge output (which is on the rotating shaft) is brought off the shaft via **slip rings** (noisy, low-cost) or **rotary transformer / wireless telemetry** (noise-free, preferred for precision).

**Block diagram** (rotary transformer telemetry):

```
[Rotating Shaft with 4 strain gauges]
        |   (rotating side)
        v
[Rotary Transformer Primary]
        <--- air gap --->
[Rotary Transformer Secondary]
        |   (stationary side)
        v
[Instrumentation Amplifier]
        v
[ADC + Microcontroller]
        v
[Display or PC]
```

#### (b) [7 Marks] A hydraulic dynamometer arm of length 0.5 m reads a net force of 800 N when the engine is running. If the engine speed is 2800 rpm, calculate the torque, shaft power in kW, and HP. Also, calculate the polar moment of inertia of a solid shaft of diameter 60 mm.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Torque:**
$$T = F \cdot L = 800 \times 0.5 = 400 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft power in watts:**
$$P = \frac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2800 \cdot 400}{60} = \frac{7,036,158}{60} \approx 117,269 \text{ W}$$

$$P \approx 117.27 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Power in HP:**
$$P_{HP} = \frac{117,269}{745.7} \approx 157.27 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 4 — Polar moment of inertia:**
$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.06)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32}$$

$$J \approx 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Answers:** $T = 400$ N·m, $P = 117.27$ kW, $P_{HP} = 157.27$ HP, $J = 1.272 \times 10^{-6}$ m⁴.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $2\pi/60$ factor:** Writing $P = N \cdot T$ instead of $P = (2\pi N T)/60$. This yields answers off by a factor of **188.5** — instant loss of 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units in strain-gauge problems:** Gauge factor is dimensionless; strain is dimensionless; voltage is in volts. Students often write $V = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ (dimensionally wrong); the correct relation is $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$.
> 3. **Forgetting $g$ in Prony-brake formulas:** When $F_1, F_2$ are read in **kgf**, you must multiply by $g = 9.81$ to convert to newton. Many students omit $g$ and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Not specifying direction of rotation in DC tachogenerator questions:** Examiners expect a comment on polarity reversal indicating direction.
> 5. **Wrong polar moment of inertia:** Some students use $J = \pi d^4/16$ (which is for a thin ring) instead of the solid-shaft formula $J = \pi d^4/32$. Check whether the question states *solid* or *hollow* shaft.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Three core families:** Speed sensors, torque sensors, and angular position sensors.
- ✅ **Speed measurement methods** — Mechanical (centrifugal, drag-cup, chronometric), Electrical (DC/AC tachogenerator, eddy-current), Electronic (magnetic pickup, photoelectric, stroboscope, encoder).
- ✅ **DC tachogenerator output** is **linear** in $\omega$: $E = K \phi \omega$.
- ✅ **Magnetic pickup frequency** — $f = NZ/60$; memorize and be able to rearrange.
- ✅ **Stroboscope relation** — $N = 60 f_{flash}/k$, with $k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$
- ✅ **Torque — Prony brake:** $T = (F_1 - F_2) R g$ (forces in kgf) or $T = (F_1 - F_2) R$ (forces in N).
- ✅ **Torque — Strain gauge:** Orient at 45° to shaft axis. Full active bridge → 4× sensitivity of single gauge; automatic temperature compensation.
- ✅ **Polar moment of inertia (solid shaft):** $J = \pi d^4/32$.
- ✅ **Torsional twist:** $\theta = TL/(JG)$.
- ✅ **Shaft power:** $P = (2\pi N T)/60$ watts when $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m.
- ✅ **1 HP = 745.7 W** (not 746 — use 745.7 for KTU precision).
- ✅ **Selection rule of thumb:** Contact methods for direct, rugged industrial use; non-contact (magnetic, optical, stroboscopic) for high speed or where loading is unacceptable.
- ✅ **Quadrature encoders** give both speed and direction (A and B channels 90° apart).
- ✅ **Drag-cup tachometer** output torque $T \propto \omega$ — linear characteristic.
- ✅ **Centrifugal tachometer** deflection $\propto \omega^2$ — **square-law** characteristic.
- ✅ **Signal-off-rotating-shaft** options: slip rings (cheap, noisy) or rotary transformer / wireless telemetry (clean, preferred).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saybolt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT402 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Saybolt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Saybolt Viscometer** is a standardized, efflux-type capillary viscometer used primarily in the petroleum industry to measure the **kinematic viscosity** of liquid petroleum products (oils, fuels, road oils) under controlled temperature conditions. The instrument determines viscosity empirically by measuring the time (in seconds) required for a fixed volume (**60 mL**) of the test liquid to flow freely through a precisely calibrated short-length orifice under gravity.

The measured efflux time, when reported at a standard temperature, defines the **Saybolt Universal Viscosity (SUV)** or **Saybolt Furol Viscosity (SFV)** of the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Saybolt viscometer falls under the topic of "Viscosity Measurement" and is classified as an **empirical viscometer** because it does not directly compute the absolute viscosity from fundamental physical dimensions — instead, it expresses viscosity in **arbitrary time units (seconds)** tied to a specific apparatus geometry.

### 1.2 Classification of Saybolt Viscometers

There are **two principal variants** standardized by ASTM (D88) and IP (British Standard):

| Variant | Orifice Diameter | Standard Test Temperature | Typical Application | ASTM Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Saybolt Universal Viscometer (SUV)** | **0.1765 cm** (0.0695 in) | **100 °F (37.8 °C)** or 210 °F (98.9 °C) | Lighter oils, lubricants, distillate fuels | ASTM D88 / D2161 |
| **Saybolt Furol Viscometer (SFV)** | **0.315 cm** (0.124 in) | **122 °F (50 °C)** or 210 °F (98.9 °C) | Heavy fuels, road oils, bitumen | ASTM D88 / D2161 |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Furol"** is a contraction of the phrase **"Fuel and Road Oil"**, indicating its industrial application domain. The Furol orifice is approximately **1.8 times larger** than the Universal orifice, allowing more viscous fluids to flow in measurable time.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have two identical glass funnels placed over measuring cylinders. You pour the same volume (**60 mL**) of two different cooking oils into each funnel. Oil A (light, like refined sunflower oil) drains out almost in 25 seconds. Oil B (thick, like cold mustard oil) takes 200 seconds. The **efflux time** itself becomes a practical, repeatable "viscosity fingerprint" of the oil.

The **Saybolt viscometer works on exactly this principle** — but with extreme engineering precision:
- Constant liquid level (always 12 cm above the orifice)
- Strictly controlled bath temperature (± 0.05 °C)
- Standardized calibrated orifice

The longer the fluid takes to drain, the higher its viscosity.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Fixed efflux volume (V):** 60 mL
- **Standard bath temperature (T):** **37.8 °C** (100 °F) for Universal; **50 °C** (122 °F) for Furol
- **Standard liquid head above orifice:** **12 cm**
- **Kinematic viscosity conversion reference fluid:** Water at 20 °C → **1 cSt** (centistokes)
- **Empirical conversion constant (for SUS > 100):** ≈ **0.226** (cSt per second)
- **Universal orifice diameter:** **1.765 mm**
- **Furol orifice diameter:** **3.15 mm**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** "**U**niversal = **U**nctuous light oils" (small hole) and "**F**urol = **F**uel oil" (big hole, Furol = Fuel-Road-Oil).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic Steps

1. **Sample Loading:** The liquid sample is poured into the Saybolt receiving tube (a vertical cylindrical cup) until the liquid level is exactly **12 cm above the upper edge of the orifice**. Excess fluid flows out through an overflow gallery, ensuring consistent hydrostatic head.
2. **Temperature Equilibration:** The entire tube is surrounded by a precisely thermostated water/oil bath maintained at the standard test temperature (37.8 °C for SUV or 50 °C for SFV). The sample is allowed to soak for at least **3 minutes** for thermal equilibrium.
3. **Orifice Clearing:** A cork or poppet valve is removed from the bottom of the orifice, simultaneously starting a precision stopwatch.
4. **Efflux Observation:** The fluid drains into a **60 mL** volumetric receiving flask positioned directly below the orifice. The flask has a scribed marking at exactly 60 mL.
5. **Time Measurement:** The stopwatch is stopped the instant the meniscus of the draining fluid passes the 60 mL mark.
6. **Reading Reporting:** This recorded time (in seconds) is the **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)** or **Saybolt Furol Seconds (SFS)**, which is reported as the Saybolt viscosity.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why fixed 60 mL?** To ensure the **potential energy** released by gravity (∝ volume × g × height) is constant for every test.
- **Why 12 cm head?** Standardizes the hydrostatic pressure at the orifice, removing variability in driving force.
- **Why thermostated bath?** Viscosity is **exponentially temperature-dependent**; a 1 °C drift can change viscosity by 5–10 % for oils.
- **Why use such a short orifice?** Unlike true capillary viscometers, Saybolt is an **efflux-time instrument**; it does NOT aim to apply the Hagen–Poiseuille law rigorously — it is a **comparative, empirical measure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Critique:** Strictly, Poiseuille's law $\left( \nu = \frac{\pi g r^4 h t}{8 L V} \right)$ would predict that efflux time is proportional to kinematic viscosity only for laminar, fully-developed, steady flow. In Saybolt, the orifice is so short that the flow is neither fully developed nor free of kinetic energy corrections. Hence the **empirical conversion equations** (next section) are used rather than the theoretical formula.

## 2.3 Conversion Between Saybolt Viscosity and Kinematic Viscosity (ν)

Since Saybolt viscosity is reported in seconds but engineering practice requires **kinematic viscosity in cSt (mm²/s)**, ASTM has established empirical equations:

### For Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS) where **SUS > 100 s**:
$$\nu \;(\text{cSt}) = 0.226 \cdot t_{\text{SUS}} - \frac{195}{t_{\text{SUS}}}$$

### For SUS ≤ 100 s (high-fluidity fluids):
$$\nu \;(\text{cSt}) = 0.226 \cdot t_{\text{SUS}} - \frac{32}{t_{\text{SUS}}}$$

### For Saybolt Furol Seconds (SFS):
$$\nu \;(\text{cSt}) = 2.24 \cdot t_{\text{SFS}} - \frac{184}{t_{\text{SFS}}}$$

### Approximate Ratio (rule of thumb):
$$\text{SFS} \approx 10 \times \text{SUS}$$
This is because the Furol orifice has roughly $\sqrt{10}$ times the area, giving ~10× the flow rate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = 0.226\,t_{SUS} - \dfrac{195}{t_{SUS}}$ | SUS → cSt (for $t_{SUS} > 100$ s) | ASTM D2161 standard |
| 2 | $\nu = 0.226\,t_{SUS} - \dfrac{32}{t_{SUS}}$ | SUS → cSt (for $t_{SUS} \le 100$ s) | ASTM D2161 standard |
| 3 | $\nu = 2.24\,t_{SFS} - \dfrac{184}{t_{SFS}}$ | SFS → cSt | ASTM D2161 standard |
| 4 | $t_{SFS} \approx 10 \cdot t_{SUS}$ | Approximate ratio (rule of thumb) | Quick estimation |
| 5 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic–Dynamic viscosity relation | Pre-/post-processing |
| 6 | $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ | Dynamic viscosity from kinematic viscosity | SI/cgs conversions |
| 7 | $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$ | SI conversion | Unit shifting |
| 8 | $1 \text{ Poise} = 100 \text{ cP}$ | Dynamic viscosity CGS unit | Engineering handbooks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Vigilance:** Always express $t$ in **seconds** and $\nu$ in **centistokes (cSt)** when using ASTM equations. Conversions: $1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 10^6 \text{ cSt}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lubricant QC:** Engine oil grades (SAE 10W, 20W, 30, 40, 50) are partially classified by Saybolt viscosity at 100 °C and 0 °C.
- **Petroleum Refining:** Refineries use Saybolt as the **online compliance test** for kerosene, diesel, and bunker fuels before dispatch.
- **Crude Oil Trading:** International contracts still quote viscosity in SUS for crude oil viscosity specifications.
- **Bitumen / Asphalt:** Saybolt Furol Viscosity is the standard for measuring paving-grade bitumen.
- **Aviation Fuel Specification:** Aviation turbine fuels (Jet A/A-1) require SUS measurement at −20 °C as part of the cold-flow specification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Efflux Time Relationship (First Principles)

We start from **Hagen-Poiseuille Law** for laminar, steady, fully-developed flow through a circular capillary:

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \, r^4 \, \Delta P}{8 \, L \, Q}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $r$ = orifice radius (m)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa)
- $L$ = orifice length (m)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)

**Step 1:** Express the volumetric flow rate in terms of the measured efflux time.

The volume of fluid collected is $V = 60 \text{ mL} = 60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$.

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-6}}{t}$$

**Step 2:** The driving pressure difference is the hydrostatic head of the liquid column.

$$\Delta P = \rho \, g \, h$$

with $h = 0.12 \text{ m}$ (12 cm standardized head) and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 3:** Substitute $\Delta P$ and $Q$ into the Hagen-Poiseuille law.

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \, r^4 \, (\rho g h)}{8 \, L \, (V/t)}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \, r^4 \, \rho \, g \, h \, t}{8 \, L \, V}$$

**Step 4:** Divide by density to obtain the kinematic viscosity.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\pi \, r^4 \, g \, h \, t}{8 \, L \, V}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that for the Saybolt viscometer, the orifice is so short that the formula is NOT exact. We therefore write it in the generic form:

$$\nu = K \cdot t$$

where $K$ is an apparatus constant. For the **Universal** orifice, empirical fitting to NIST-traceable viscosity standards yields:

$$K_{SUS} \approx 0.226 \text{ cSt/s} \quad \text{with a non-linear correction term}$$

This is the theoretical justification for the empirical ASTM conversion:

$$\nu = 0.226 \, t - \frac{195}{t}$$

The $\frac{195}{t}$ term corrects for **kinetic energy losses** (entrance/exit effects) and the **non-Newtonian turbulence** at the orifice mouth — neither of which are captured by the ideal Hagen-Poiseuille law.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A petroleum oil sample drains through a Saybolt Universal Viscometer at **100 °F (37.8 °C)** in **315 seconds**. Calculate:
> (a) The Saybolt Universal Viscosity (SUS).
> (b) The kinematic viscosity $\nu$ in cSt and m²/s.
> (c) The dynamic viscosity $\mu$ in cP, given oil density $\rho = 0.88$ g/cm³.

### Part (a) — Saybolt Universal Viscosity

By definition, the efflux time of **60 mL** in seconds is reported as SUS.

$$\boxed{t_{SUS} = 315 \text{ s}}$$

### Part (b) — Kinematic Viscosity

Since $t_{SUS} = 315 > 100$ s, use the appropriate ASTM equation:

$$\nu = 0.226 \cdot t - \frac{195}{t}$$

**Algebraic step 1:** Multiply the time by 0.226.

$$0.226 \times 315 = 71.190$$

**Algebraic step 2:** Compute the correction term.

$$\frac{195}{315} = 0.6190$$

**Algebraic step 3:** Subtract.

$$\nu = 71.190 - 0.619 = 70.571 \text{ cSt}$$

**Conversion to SI units:**

$$1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\nu = 70.571 \times 10^{-6} = 7.0571 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

### Part (c) — Dynamic Viscosity

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$$

**Unit consistency check:** $\rho = 0.88 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$. $\nu = 7.0571 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

$$\mu = 880 \times 7.0571 \times 10^{-5} = 0.06210 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Convert to centipoise (cP):** $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 1000 \text{ cP}$.

$$\mu = 0.06210 \times 1000 = 62.10 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 62.1 \text{ cP}}$$

## 3.3 Reverse Conversion Worked Example

> **Problem:** A hydraulic oil has a kinematic viscosity of **45 cSt at 40 °C**. Estimate the expected Saybolt Universal Seconds reading.

We invert the ASTM formula (algebraically approximate by ignoring the $\frac{195}{t}$ correction first):

$$t \approx \frac{\nu + \sqrt{\nu^2 + 4 \cdot 0.226 \cdot 195}}{2 \times 0.226}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the discriminant.

$$4 \times 0.226 \times 195 = 176.28$$

$$45^2 + 176.28 = 2025 + 176.28 = 2201.28$$

**Step 2:** Compute the square root.

$$\sqrt{2201.28} \approx 46.92$$

**Step 3:** Combine.

$$t \approx \frac{45 + 46.92}{0.452} = \frac{91.92}{0.452} \approx 203.4 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{SUS} \approx 203 \text{ s}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Saybolt vs. Other Viscometers

| Feature | **Saybolt** | Redwood (UK) | Engler (Continental Europe) | Capillary (Ubbelohde) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Orifice type | Short, calibrated | Short, calibrated | Short, calibrated | Long, true capillary |
| Efflux volume | 60 mL | 50 mL | 200 mL (or 50 mL) | — (time-of-flow meniscus) |
| Std. temperature | 37.8 °C / 50 °C | 60 °C | 25 °C, 50 °C | Variable, precise |
| Reporting unit | SUS, SFS | RWS (Redwood Seconds) | E° (Engler degrees) | cSt (absolute) |
| Calibration | Empirical | Empirical | Empirical | Absolute (Poiseuille) |
| Repeatability | ± 1 % | ± 2 % | ± 3 % | ± 0.1 % |
| Application | Petroleum (USA) | Petroleum (UK) | Petroleum (Europe) | Scientific/lab |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion shortcuts for international exams:**
> - $1 \text{ RWS} \approx 0.935 \text{ SUS}$
> - $1 \text{ Engler degree} \approx 14 \text{ SUS}$ (at 50 °C, for $t > 10$ s)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram of Saybolt Viscometer Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Thermostat Bath with Heater] --> B[Water/Oil Circulating Jacket]
    B --> C[Saybolt Receiving Tube]
    C --> D[Liquid Sample - 60 mL]
    C --> E[Orifice 0.1765 cm SUV or 0.315 cm SFV]
    F[Cork/Poppet Valve] --> E
    E --> G[Drainage Stream]
    G --> H[60 mL Volumetric Flask]
    I[Stopwatch] -.Time.-> H
    J[Overflow Gallery] --> C
    K[Standard Liquid Head 12 cm] --> C
    subgraph Standardization_Loop[Standardization Conditions]
        L[Test Temperature 37.8 C SUV]
        M[Test Temperature 50 C SFV]
    end
    L --> A
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow (Sensor to Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sample Inlet] --> S2[Level Standardization 12 cm]
    S2 --> S3[Thermal Equilibration in Bath]
    S3 --> S4[Orifice Opening Trigger]
    S4 --> S5[Gravity-Driven Efflux]
    S5 --> S6[60 mL Volumetric Capture]
    S6 --> S7[Stopwatch Time t in seconds]
    S7 --> P1[Apply ASTM D2161 Conversion]
    P1 --> P2[Output: Kinematic Viscosity in cSt]
    P2 --> P3[Optional: Convert to Dynamic Viscosity via Density]
    subgraph Correction_Logic[Non-Ideal Corrections]
        C1[Kinetic Energy Loss at Orifice] --> P1
        C2[Turbulence Transition Loss] --> P1
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sankey of Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Physical Quantity: Viscosity] --> Stage1[Conversion to Efflux Time]
    Stage1 --> Stage2[Time t in seconds]
    Stage2 --> Stage3[ASTM Empirical Formula]
    Stage3 --> Output[Kinematic Viscosity cSt]
    Stage4[Sample Density rho] --> Output
    Output --> Final[Dynamic Viscosity cP]
    style Input fill:#e1f5ff
    style Final fill:#d4f4dd
```

## 4.4 Comparison Architecture: Universal vs Furol

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUV[Saybolt Universal Viscometer]
        A1[Orifice 1.765 mm] --> A2[Std Temp 37.8 C]
        A2 --> A3[SUS Reading]
    end
    subgraph SFV[Saybolt Furol Viscometer]
        B1[Orifice 3.15 mm] --> B2[Std Temp 50 C]
        B2 --> B3[SFS Reading]
    end
    A3 --> C[SFS approx 10 times SUS]
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Final Kinematic Viscosity cSt]
    style SUV fill:#fff4e6
    style SFV fill:#e8f5e9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the working principle of a Saybolt Universal Viscometer. State the standard test temperature and orifice diameter used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Saybolt Universal Viscometer (SUV) is an **efflux-type viscometer** that measures the kinematic viscosity of a liquid indirectly. A **60 mL** sample is heated in a thermostatically controlled water bath until it reaches the standard test temperature of **37.8 °C (100 °F)**. A cork closing the bottom orifice is removed, and a stopwatch is started. The time in seconds required for the fluid to fill a **60 mL volumetric flask** placed below the orifice is recorded. This time, expressed in **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)**, defines the Saybolt viscosity of the fluid. The **Universal orifice** has a diameter of **0.1765 cm (1.765 mm)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Reporting the bath temperature as just "100 °C" instead of **100 °F (37.8 °C)**. This is a **guaranteed 1-mark loss** in KTU valuation. Always quote both °F and °C.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Saybolt Universal Viscometer and Saybolt Furol Viscometer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **Saybolt Universal (SUV)** | **Saybolt Furol (SFV)** |
|---|---|---|
| Orifice diameter | 0.1765 cm (smaller) | 0.315 cm (larger, ~1.8×) |
| Standard temperature | 37.8 °C (100 °F) | 50 °C (122 °F) |
| Typical use | Light oils, lubricants, distillate fuels | Heavy fuels, road oils, bitumen |
| Notation | SUS (seconds) | SFS (seconds) |

The "Furol" name is a contraction of **"Fuel and Road Oil"**, indicating its industrial application for heavy petroleum fractions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]`**

(a) With a neat functional diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Saybolt Universal Viscometer**. Explain why the temperature is strictly controlled. **(7 marks)**

(b) A lubricating oil has a Saybolt Universal Viscosity (SUS) of **420 seconds at 100 °F**. If the density of the oil is **0.90 g/cm³**, determine:
   (i) Kinematic viscosity in **cSt**
   (ii) Kinematic viscosity in **m²/s**
   (iii) Dynamic viscosity in **Pa·s** and **centipoise (cP)**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Saybolt Universal Viscometer consists of:
1. A **vertical cylindrical receiving tube** into which the test oil is poured, with an overflow gallery to maintain a constant head.
2. A **calibrated orifice** at the bottom of the tube, with diameter **1.765 mm**.
3. A **water bath** surrounding the tube, equipped with an electric heater, stirrer, and precision thermostat.
4. A **60 mL volumetric receiving flask** placed directly below the orifice.
5. A **stopcock or cork** sealing the orifice.
6. A **precision stopwatch** triggered at cork removal.

**Working:**
- The oil is poured until it overflows, ensuring a **12 cm head** above the orifice.
- The bath maintains **37.8 °C (100 °F)** to within ± 0.05 °C.
- After 3 minutes of thermal equilibration, the cork is pulled and the stopwatch is started.
- The time for the oil to fill the 60 mL flask is recorded as the **SUS reading**.

**Why strict temperature control?**
Viscosity is exponentially temperature-dependent. For most oils, a **1 °C drift** can cause a **5–10 % change** in measured viscosity. Without strict bath control, repeatability is destroyed and the SUS reading loses its physical meaning. ASTM D88 therefore mandates ± 0.05 °C tolerance.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $t_{SUS} = 420$ s, $\rho = 0.90$ g/cm³ = 900 kg/m³.

**(i) Kinematic viscosity in cSt** — since $t = 420 > 100$ s, use:

$$\nu = 0.226 \, t - \frac{195}{t}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply.

$$0.226 \times 420 = 94.92$$

**Step 2:** Correction term.

$$\frac{195}{420} = 0.4643$$

**Step 3:** Subtract.

$$\nu = 94.92 - 0.4643 = 94.456 \text{ cSt}$$

**(ii) Kinematic viscosity in m²/s:**

$$\nu = 94.456 \times 10^{-6} = 9.4456 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Dynamic viscosity:**

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$$

$$\mu = 900 \times 9.4456 \times 10^{-5} = 0.08501 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Convert to cP:**

$$\mu = 0.08501 \times 1000 = 85.01 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 85.0 \text{ cP}}$$

---

### Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]`**

(a) Explain the **empirical relationship** between Saybolt viscosity (in SUS) and kinematic viscosity. State the **two ASTM equations** with their applicability conditions. **(7 marks)**

(b) A sample of fuel oil has a Saybolt Furol Viscosity of **250 seconds at 50 °C**. Determine:
   (i) Its kinematic viscosity in cSt.
   (ii) If the density of the oil is 0.95 g/cm³, calculate the dynamic viscosity in **centipoise**.
   (iii) What would be the approximate SUS value for the same oil if measured at 37.8 °C? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Saybolt viscometer is an **empirical, efflux-time instrument**; therefore, the relationship between efflux time $t$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is **not** derived from first principles but is **empirically fitted** to NIST-traceable viscosity standards.

The two ASTM equations (per ASTM D2161) are:

**For $t_{SUS} > 100$ s (typical for lubricating oils):**

$$\nu \;(\text{cSt}) = 0.226 \, t_{SUS} - \frac{195}{t_{SUS}}$$

**For $t_{SUS} \le 100$ s (light distillates, kerosene):**

$$\nu \;(\text{cSt}) = 0.226 \, t_{SUS} - \frac{32}{t_{SUS}}$$

The non-linear correction term $\frac{195}{t_{SUS}}$ (or $\frac{32}{t_{SUS}}$) accounts for the **kinetic energy losses** at the orifice entry and exit and the **turbulence effects** that violate the laminar-flow assumption of the Hagen-Poiseuille law. The constant **0.226** is the apparatus constant determined experimentally.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $t_{SFS} = 250$ s at 50 °C, $\rho = 0.95$ g/cm³ = 950 kg/m³.

**(i) Kinematic viscosity (using SFV equation):**

$$\nu = 2.24 \, t_{SFS} - \frac{184}{t_{SFS}}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply.

$$2.24 \times 250 = 560$$

**Step 2:** Correction term.

$$\frac{184}{250} = 0.736$$

**Step 3:** Subtract.

$$\nu = 560 - 0.736 = 559.26 \text{ cSt}$$

**(ii) Dynamic viscosity:**

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$$

Convert $\nu$ to SI: $\nu = 559.26 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.

$$\mu = 950 \times 559.26 \times 10^{-6} = 0.5313 \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\mu = 531.3 \text{ cP}$$

**(iii) Approximate SUS value:**

Using the rule of thumb $t_{SFS} \approx 10 \cdot t_{SUS}$:

$$t_{SUS} \approx \frac{250}{10} = 25 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the non-linear correction term** ($\frac{195}{t}$ or $\frac{184}{t}$) is a guaranteed **2-mark loss** in KTU. Always include the correction in the final answer.
> 2. **Using the wrong ASTM equation** (Universal vs. Furol) is another common error. Match the orifice type first, then select the correct formula.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** If density is given in g/cm³, convert to **kg/m³** before multiplying with ν in m²/s to get μ in Pa·s. Otherwise the answer will be off by 1000×.
> 4. **Misreporting SUS as viscosity in cSt** — remember: SUS is a **time in seconds**, NOT a viscosity. Always convert to cSt before any viscosity-based design calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Saybolt viscometer is an empirical efflux-time instrument** — it does NOT measure viscosity directly; it measures the time for **60 mL** to drain.
- **Two variants:** Universal (SUV, orifice 1.765 mm, 37.8 °C) and Furol (SFV, orifice 3.15 mm, 50 °C).
- **Standardized liquid head:** 12 cm above orifice.
- **ASTM D88 / D2161** govern the procedure and conversion equations.
- **Two key conversion equations to memorize:**
  - SUS > 100 s: $\nu = 0.226\,t - \frac{195}{t}$
  - SUS ≤ 100 s: $\nu = 0.226\,t - \frac{32}{t}$
  - SFS: $\nu = 2.24\,t - \frac{184}{t}$
- **Approximate rule:** $t_{SFS} \approx 10 \cdot t_{SUS}$.
- **Temperature control is critical** — viscosity varies ~5–10 % per °C for most oils.
- **Hagen-Poiseuille** provides the theoretical foundation, but the **kinetic energy correction term** ($\frac{K}{t}$) makes the equation empirical in practice.
- **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ cSt} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$; $1 \text{ Pa·s} = 1000 \text{ cP}$.
- **Applications:** Lubricant grading, refinery QC, aviation fuel specs, bitumen/asphalt testing, crude oil trading.
- **Examiner traps to avoid:**
  - Reporting bath temp in °C alone (always state °F + °C)
  - Forgetting the $\frac{195}{t}$ correction term
  - Confusing SUS/SFS (always check orifice type first)
  - Mixing cgs/SI units in dynamic viscosity calculations

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Redwood
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT402 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION 1
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Redwood

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Redwood Viscometer** is a standard capillary-type viscometer used primarily in the petroleum and lubricant industries to measure the **kinematic viscosity** of oils and similar fluids. It determines viscosity indirectly by measuring the **time (in seconds)** taken by a fixed volume of fluid to flow through a standard orifice under a constant hydrostatic head. The instrument is standardized by the **Institute of Petroleum (IP)** and **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**.

There are **two standard models** of the Redwood viscometer used in KTU laboratory and industrial practice:

- **Redwood Viscometer No. 1** — Used for low-viscosity fluids (kinematic viscosity below ~10 cm²/s or 100 cSt), such as light lubricating oils, kerosene, and gasoline.
- **Redwood Viscometer No. 2** — Used for high-viscosity fluids (viscosity above 10 cSt), such as heavy fuel oils, bitumen emulsions, and heavy lubricants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The question "Explain Redwood Viscometer with neat diagram" or "Derive the relation between kinematic viscosity and Redwood time" is a **high-frequency 14-mark question** in the KTU University Examination (Module 4). Mastery of the formula derivation is non-negotiable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small cup with a tiny hole at the bottom, filled with honey. The thicker the honey, the slower it drips out. The Redwood viscometer works on **exactly this principle** but with **engineering precision**:
- A precisely machined **standard orifice** replaces the "hole."
- A precisely measured **50 mL volume** replaces the "cup content."
- Gravity provides the **driving force** (head pressure).
- A **stopwatch-grade timer** measures the time taken to discharge the fixed volume.

The slower the fluid drains, the **higher its viscosity**. By knowing the time (called *Redwood Seconds*) and the geometry of the orifice, we can compute the kinematic viscosity in standard units (**cm²/s** or **Stokes**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards:**
> - Standard test temperature: **60°C (for No.1)** and **100°C (for No.2)** in some standards, but **room temperature experiments** are common in B.Tech labs.
> - Standard sample volume: **50 mL**
> - Orifice diameter: **1.62 mm (No.1)** and **3.80 mm (No.2)**
> - Unit of kinematic viscosity: **1 Stoke = 1 cm²/s = 100 Centistokes (cSt)**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 981 cm/s² (9.81 m/s²)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Redwood viscometer uses **Hagen-Poiseuille's law** as its theoretical foundation. Let's break down the operational sequence:

1. **Fluid Loading:** The sample fluid is poured into the **cup** up to a precisely marked level (filling exactly **50 mL**).
2. **Initial Head Setup:** A **ball valve** at the bottom is closed. The fluid attains a hydrostatic head $h$ above the orifice.
3. **Discharge Phase:** When the valve opens, fluid flows out through the **jet (orifice)** under gravity. Simultaneously, the **level of fluid in the cup drops**, decreasing the head $h$.
4. **Time Measurement:** A **50 mL Kohlrausch flask** (special receiving flask) is placed below. The time $t$ (in seconds) for **50 mL of fluid** to collect in the flask is recorded. This is the **Redwood No. 1 (or No. 2) time**.
5. **Computation:** The kinematic viscosity $\nu$ is calculated using an empirical relation involving the time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a falling head (not constant head)?** Unlike a flowmeter, the head $h$ is **not constant** — it falls as the fluid drains. This is why the formula derived below contains a *correction term* $-B/t$. The empirical fit accounts for the loss of kinetic energy, entry/exit losses, and the non-constant head effect.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains **all the formulas** you must memorize for KTU 2024 scheme exams on the Redwood viscometer.

| **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** |
|:------------|:---------------------|:----------|
| $\nu = A t - \dfrac{B}{t}$ | General empirical Redwood equation (kinematic viscosity) | $\nu$ in stokes, $t$ in seconds |
| $\nu = 0.0026\,t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ | Redwood **No. 1** specific equation | $\nu$ in stokes, $t$ in seconds |
| $\nu = 0.26\,t - \dfrac{172}{t}$ | Redwood No. 1 in centistokes (cSt) | $\nu$ in cSt, $t$ in seconds |
| $\nu = 0.027\,t - \dfrac{20}{t}$ | Redwood **No. 2** specific equation | $\nu$ in stokes, $t$ in seconds |
| $\nu = 2.7\,t - \dfrac{2000}{t}$ | Redwood No. 2 in centistokes | $\nu$ in cSt, $t$ in seconds |
| $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity definition | $\nu$ in stokes |
| $\mu = \pi\, r^{4}\, h\, \rho\, g \, /\, (8\, L\, V)\, \cdot t$ | Hagen-Poiseuille derived form | CGS units |
| $Q = \dfrac{\pi\, r^{4}\, \Delta P}{8\, \mu\, L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille fundamental law | SI / CGS |
| $1\,\text{Stoke} = 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}\text{/s}$ | Stoke to SI conversion | Unit conversion |
| $1\,\text{cSt} = 0.01\,\text{Stoke}$ | Centistoke to Stoke | Unit conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** When using the formulas, the time $t$ must be in **seconds**, not minutes. Many students mistakenly use minutes and get wrong answers in numericals. Always recheck the units before substituting.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refineries:** Quality control of crude oil, diesel, gasoline, and lubricants.
- **Lubricant Manufacturing:** Grading engine oils (SAE 10W, 20W, etc.) requires precise viscosity measurement.
- **Paint & Varnish Industry:** Ensuring correct flow properties for brush/spray application.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Food Industry:** Quality check for syrups, oils, and pastes.
- **Power Plants:** Monitoring the viscosity of fuel oil for proper atomization in boilers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Kinematic Viscosity and Redwood Time

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 4. We derive the empirical form $\nu = At - B/t$ from the Hagen-Poiseuille equation.

### Step 1: Starting with Hagen-Poiseuille Law

For laminar flow of a viscous fluid through a capillary tube, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is governed by:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

where $r$ is the radius of the capillary, $\Delta P$ is the pressure difference across the capillary, $\mu$ is the **dynamic (absolute) viscosity**, and $L$ is the length of the capillary.

### Step 2: Expressing Pressure in Terms of Fluid Head

The pressure head is the height $h$ of the fluid column above the orifice, with $\rho$ as fluid density and $g$ as gravitational acceleration. The pressure difference is:

$$\Delta P = h \, \rho \, g$$

Substituting back into the flow rate equation:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, h \, \rho \, g}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

### Step 3: Setting Up the Volume Balance

If $V$ is the volume discharged in time $t$, then $Q = V/t$. So:

$$\dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, h \, \rho \, g}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

### Step 4: Isolating the Kinematic Viscosity $\nu = \mu / \rho$

Rearranging to isolate $\mu / \rho$ (i.e., the kinematic viscosity $\nu$):

$$\dfrac{\mu}{\rho} = \nu = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, h \, g \, t}{8 \, L \, V}$$

### Step 5: Acknowledging the Falling Head Effect

In the Redwood viscometer, the head $h$ is **not constant** during discharge. A complete hydrodynamic analysis (considering kinetic energy correction and varying head) yields a corrected form:

$$\nu = C_{1} \, h_{0} \, t - \dfrac{C_{2}}{t}$$

where $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ are **instrument constants** that depend on the geometry of the cup, orifice, and fluid column. Combining the constants into $A$ and $B$:

$$\boxed{\nu = A \, t - \dfrac{B}{t}}$$

### Step 6: Substituting the Redwood-Specific Constants

For **Redwood Viscometer No. 1**, experimentally determined constants are $A = 0.0026$ and $B = 1.72$ (in CGS units: stokes and seconds). This gives:

$$\nu_{\text{R1}} = 0.0026\,t - \dfrac{1.72}{t} \quad \text{[in stokes]}$$

Multiplying by **100** to convert to **centistokes**:

$$\nu_{\text{R1}} = 0.26\,t - \dfrac{172}{t} \quad \text{[in centistokes]}$$

For **Redwood Viscometer No. 2**, the constants are $A = 0.027$ and $B = 20$:

$$\nu_{\text{R2}} = 0.027\,t - \dfrac{20}{t} \quad \text{[in stokes]}$$

$$\nu_{\text{R2}} = 2.7\,t - \dfrac{2000}{t} \quad \text{[in centistokes]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** For KTU 14-mark questions, you are expected to derive the relation **starting from the Hagen-Poiseuille equation**, then explain the falling-head correction, and **finally substitute the Redwood No. 1 or No. 2 constants** as per the question. Skipping the starting equation costs 3-4 marks immediately.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A sample of lubricating oil at 60°C takes **450 seconds** to flow through a Redwood No. 1 viscometer. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in **(i) Stokes** and **(ii) Centistokes**. Also find the dynamic viscosity if the density of the oil is **0.88 g/cm³**.

### Solution:

**(i) Kinematic viscosity in Stokes (using Redwood No. 1 equation):**

$$\nu = 0.0026\,t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$$

Substituting $t = 450$ s:

$$\nu = (0.0026 \times 450) - \dfrac{1.72}{450}$$

$$\nu = 1.17 - 0.003822$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 1.1662\,\text{stokes}}$$

**(ii) Converting to centistokes (multiply by 100):**

$$\nu = 1.1662 \times 100 = \boxed{116.62\,\text{cSt}}$$

**(iii) Dynamic viscosity $\mu$:**

Recall $\nu = \mu / \rho$, so $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$:

$$\mu = 1.1662 \times 0.88 = \boxed{1.026\,\text{poise}}$$

Note: $1\,\text{poise} = 0.1\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, so $\mu \approx 0.1026\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Functional Architecture of a Redwood Viscometer

The Redwood viscometer cannot be drawn physically in Mermaid, so the following **block-level functional architecture** maps every component and the flow of fluid, energy, and information.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SAMPLE FLUID INLET - Top filling port"]
    nodeB["LEVEL INDICATOR - Point gauge and pointer"]
    nodeC["OIL CUP - Brass reservoir holding 50 mL"]
    nodeD["THERMOMETER WELL - For temperature probe"]
    nodeE["BALL VALVE - Closes the orifice before test"]
    nodeF["STANDARD ORIFICE JET - 1.62 mm or 3.80 mm"]
    nodeG["JET SUPPORT - Holds the capillary jet"]
    nodeH["CONSTANT HEAD WATER BATH - For temperature control"]
    nodeI["CUP LEVELLING SCREWS - Three screws for horizontal balance"]
    nodeJ["KOHLRAUSCH FLASK - 50 mL receiving flask"]
    nodeK["STOPWATCH - Records time of flow in seconds"]
    nodeL["DATA COMPUTATION - Apply formula nu equals At minus B over t"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeH -.heats.-> nodeC
    nodeB -.monitors level.-> nodeC
    nodeI -.levels.-> nodeC
```

## 4.2 Working Sequence Diagram (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["STEP 1 - Clean the oil cup and orifice jet thoroughly"]
    step2["STEP 2 - Level the instrument using levelling screws"]
    step3["STEP 3 - Close the ball valve and fill cup with 50 mL fluid"]
    step4["STEP 4 - Insert thermometer and wait for thermal equilibrium"]
    step5["STEP 5 - Place empty 50 mL Kohlrausch flask below jet"]
    step6["STEP 6 - Lift ball valve and simultaneously start stopwatch"]
    step7["STEP 7 - Stop watch when flask fills exactly to 50 mL mark"]
    step8["STEP 8 - Record time t in seconds - this is the Redwood time"]
    step9["STEP 9 - Compute kinematic viscosity using nu equals 0.0026 t minus 1.72 over t"]
    step10["STEP 10 - Repeat three times and take average for accuracy"]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 --> step10
```

## 4.3 Text Description of the Physical Instrument (For KTU Diagram Questions)

Although the question often asks for a "neat diagram," the following textual description serves as your **mental blueprint** if a hand-drawn diagram is required:

1. A **cylindrical brass oil cup** sits vertically, with a capacity slightly more than 50 mL.
2. The cup has a **pointer** at the top to indicate the 50 mL fill level (the fluid surface just touches the pointer tip).
3. Inside the bottom of the cup is a **replaceable brass jet** — the orifice — held in place by a screw-threaded retainer.
4. Below the jet, a **ball valve** controls flow; when the ball is lifted (using a string/lever from outside), fluid flows out.
5. The entire cup is enclosed in a **water bath** (or oil bath) to maintain constant temperature, with a **stirrer** to ensure uniform temperature distribution.
6. A **thermometer** is inserted into the bath to monitor the temperature.
7. A **Kohlrausch flask** of 50 mL capacity stands below the jet to collect the discharged fluid.
8. The complete assembly rests on a sturdy base with **levelling screws**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** If asked to "explain with neat diagram," a labelled cross-section drawing showing the **cup, jet, ball valve, water bath, thermometer, and Kohlrausch flask** fetches full marks. The diagram is worth **3-4 marks** out of 14 in such questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a Redwood viscometer? Name its two models.**

**Model Answer:**
The Redwood viscometer is a **capillary-type viscometer** used to measure the kinematic viscosity of petroleum and lubricating oils. It works on the principle of measuring the time (in seconds) taken by a fixed volume (**50 mL**) of fluid to flow through a standard orifice under gravity. The two models are:
1. **Redwood Viscometer No. 1** — for low-viscosity fluids (orifice = 1.62 mm).
2. **Redwood Viscometer No. 2** — for high-viscosity fluids (orifice = 3.80 mm).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the empirical formula for kinematic viscosity using Redwood No. 1 viscometer and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The empirical formula is:

$$\nu = 0.0026\,t - \dfrac{1.72}{t} \quad \text{[in stokes]}$$

where:
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of the fluid (in stokes, cm²/s).
- $t$ = time of flow of 50 mL fluid through the orifice (in seconds).
- The constant $0.0026$ accounts for the **calibrated head and geometry**, and $1.72$ is the **kinetic energy correction constant** for the instrument.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Redwood Viscometer No. 1** with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relation $\nu = At - B/t$ for a Redwood viscometer starting from the Hagen-Poiseuille equation. Mention the values of $A$ and $B$ for Redwood No. 1 and No. 2 viscometers. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction:**
The Redwood Viscometer No. 1 consists of the following main parts:

1. **Oil Cup:** A brass cylindrical cup of capacity slightly more than 50 mL, fitted with a pointer at the top to mark the standard filling level.
2. **Orifice Jet:** A replaceable brass capillary of standard internal diameter **1.62 mm**, fitted at the bottom of the cup.
3. **Ball Valve:** A valve at the bottom of the jet to start/stop the flow of fluid.
4. **Water Bath:** A surrounding bath filled with water to maintain constant temperature, equipped with a stirrer and heater.
5. **Thermometer:** A standard mercury thermometer to monitor the bath temperature.
6. **Kohlrausch Flask:** A 50 mL receiving flask placed below the jet.
7. **Levelling Screws:** Three screws at the base to keep the instrument perfectly vertical and level.

**Working Principle:**
- The cup is filled with the test oil up to the pointer level (50 mL).
- The ball valve is closed initially. The water bath is maintained at the desired test temperature.
- A clean Kohlrausch flask is placed beneath the jet.
- The ball valve is opened and a stopwatch is started simultaneously.
- The time $t$ (in seconds) taken for **50 mL of oil to collect** in the flask is noted.
- The kinematic viscosity is calculated using the formula.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The derivation is as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result:

$$\nu = A\,t - \dfrac{B}{t}$$

- For **Redwood No. 1:** $A = 0.0026$, $B = 1.72$ (stokes).
- For **Redwood No. 2:** $A = 0.027$, $B = 20$ (stokes).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A lubricating oil takes **680 seconds** to flow through a **Redwood No. 2 viscometer** at 100°C. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in **(i) Stokes** and **(ii) centistokes**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **differences** between **Redwood No. 1** and **Redwood No. 2** viscometers in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

Given: $t = 680\,\text{s}$, Redwood No. 2.

**(i) Kinematic viscosity in stokes:**

$$\nu = 0.027\,t - \dfrac{20}{t} = 0.027 \times 680 - \dfrac{20}{680}$$

$$\nu = 18.36 - 0.0294$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 18.3306\,\text{stokes}}$$

**(ii) Converting to centistokes:**

$$\nu = 18.3306 \times 100 = \boxed{1833.06\,\text{cSt}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table: Redwood No. 1 vs Redwood No. 2**

| **Parameter** | **Redwood No. 1** | **Redwood No. 2** |
|:--------------|:------------------|:------------------|
| Orifice diameter | **1.62 mm** | **3.80 mm** |
| Orifice length | 10 mm | 50 mm |
| Sample volume | 50 mL | 50 mL |
| Application | Low-viscosity fluids (kerosene, light oils) | High-viscosity fluids (heavy oils, bitumen) |
| Constant A | 0.0026 (stokes) | 0.027 (stokes) |
| Constant B | 1.72 (stokes) | 20 (stokes) |
| Formula (stokes) | $\nu = 0.0026\,t - 1.72/t$ | $\nu = 0.027\,t - 20/t$ |
| Test temperature | 60°C (standard) | 100°C (standard) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Converting 50 mL to 50 cm³ is fine, but if the time is given in minutes, students often forget to convert to seconds — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Viscometer Number:** Using Redwood No. 1 constants for a No. 2 question (or vice versa) gives a wildly wrong answer. **Always re-read the question carefully.**
> 3. **Skipping Diagram:** Even if a question says "explain with diagram," students often write 4 pages of text without a single diagram. **Always include a labelled cross-section** of the Redwood viscometer.
> 4. **Forgetting the Empirical Origin:** The formula $\nu = At - B/t$ is empirical. Examiners expect the **Hagen-Poiseuille starting point** plus a note on the falling-head correction. Just stating the formula without derivation costs 3-4 marks.
> 5. **Density Confusion:** In dynamic viscosity problems, students confuse **density ($\rho$)** with **specific gravity (SG)**. The formula $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$ requires $\rho$ in **g/cm³** for CGS or **kg/m³** for SI. Unit consistency is critical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Redwood viscometer** is a **capillary-type, falling-head viscometer** used for petroleum and lubricating oils.
- It exists in **two versions**: **No. 1** (1.62 mm orifice, for low viscosity) and **No. 2** (3.80 mm orifice, for high viscosity).
- The fundamental equation is **empirical**:
  - $\nu = 0.0026\,t - 1.72/t$ (No. 1, stokes)
  - $\nu = 0.027\,t - 20/t$ (No. 2, stokes)
- The derivation starts from the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** with a **falling-head correction** giving the term $-B/t$.
- Standard **sample volume** is **50 mL** collected in a **Kohlrausch flask**.
- The **time $t$** is the **Redwood seconds** — a direct measure of viscosity.
- Convert **Stokes to cSt** by multiplying by **100**.
- Convert **Stokes to SI (m²/s)** by multiplying by **$10^{-4}$**.
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu = \nu \cdot \rho$, with $\rho$ as fluid density.
- The instrument must be **levelled** and **thermally equilibrated** before testing.
- Three trials are taken and **averaged** for accuracy in lab practice.
- The Redwood No. 1 is tested typically at **60°C**; the No. 2 at **100°C**.
- A **neat labelled diagram** of the instrument carries **3-4 marks** by itself in a 14-mark question.
- Always check whether the question gives time in **seconds or minutes** before computing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** For maximum marks, structure your 14-mark answer as: **(1) Diagram (3 marks) + (2) Construction/Working (4 marks) + (3) Derivation (5 marks) + (4) Numerical or Formula Summary (2 marks).** This coverage guarantees full credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Falling Ball and Capillary Tube Viscometers - Industrial Types - Capillary Type of Viscosity Measurements - Falling Piston-Float Viscometer - Oscillating Blade - Turbidity Measurement - Forward Scattering Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
