# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Q: Define turbidity. What is the role of Formazin in turbidity measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Turbidity is an optical property of a fluid caused by the presence of suspended and colloidal matter, which results in the **scattering and absorption** of light passing through the medium. It is measured in **Nephelometric Turbidity Units (NTU)**.
- **Formazin role (2 marks):** Formazin is a **polymer suspension** synthesized by reacting **hydrazine sulfate with hexamethylenetetramine**. It is the **primary reference standard** for turbidity calibration as per **ISO 7027**, with a defined turbidity of **4000 NTU** at the stock concentration. It is used to calibrate nephelometric, backscatter, and ratio turbidimeters through serial dilution.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Q: Why is the 90° detector angle used in nephelometry insufficient for high-turbidity wastewater, and what alternative geometry is used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Limitation of 90° (1 mark):** At high turbidity (> 100 NTU), the incident beam undergoes **multiple scattering events**, leading to **signal saturation, non-linearity, and shadowing effects** in the 90° detector. The forward-traveling beam is completely extinguished, and the side-scatter signal no longer tracks concentration linearly.
- **Alternative (2 marks):** A **backscatter (140°–180°) detector geometry** is used. The detector is placed near the source, collecting light that returns from the first few millimetres of the sample. This geometry **avoids multiple-scatter saturation**, works in the range **0 – 4000+ NTU**, and is suitable for **in-situ immersion probes** in activated sludge and industrial effluents.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Backscatter Turbidity Analyzer. Compare it with a nephelometric turbidity analyzer.**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Block Diagram** *(refer to SECTION 4 Mermaid diagram)*

**Working Principle — Stepwise Explanation:**

1. **Light Source (2 marks):** A **Near-Infrared LED at 860 nm** (per ISO 7027) is driven by a pulsed current source to provide a stable, modulated beam. The pulsed operation rejects ambient light interference.

2. **Optical Window and Sample Volume (2 marks):** The LED beam enters the sample through a **sapphire window**, which is highly scratch-resistant. Suspended particles in the sample scatter the photons. The geometry is such that the detector is positioned at **~140°–180°** from the incident beam, capturing the backscattered component.

3. **Detection and Signal Processing (2 marks):** The backscattered photons strike a **silicon photodiode** with an integrated **narrow-band optical filter at 860 nm**. The photodiode current is amplified by a **transimpedance amplifier (TIA)**, synchronously demodulated, and the dark current is subtracted.

4. **Calibration and Output (1 mark):** The processed signal is mapped to **NTU** using a multi-point Formazin calibration curve, and output is provided as a **4–20 mA loop, HART, or Modbus** signal.

#### Part (b) — Comparison with Nephelometric Analyzer (7 marks)

| Parameter | Nephelometric (90°) | Backscatter (140°–180°) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Detector angle** | 90° from incident beam | 140° – 180° from incident beam |
| **Optimal range** | 0 – 40 NTU | 0 – 4000+ NTU |
| **Application** | Drinking water, pharmaceutical WFI | Wastewater, sludge, mining |
| **Susceptibility to color** | High | Low (short optical path) |
| **Sample preparation** | Required (cuvette) | None (in-situ immersion) |
| **Sensitivity to small particles** | High (Rayleigh regime) | Moderate (Mie regime) |
| **Self-cleaning capability** | No | Yes (wiperless ultrasonic variants) |
| **Signal linearity** | Excellent in low range | Polynomial correction needed in high range |
| **Standards compliance** | EPA 180.1 (visible white light) | ISO 7027 (NIR 860 nm) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Nephelometric analyzers are preferred for **low-turbidity, high-accuracy** applications, while backscatter analyzers are the **industrial standard for high-turbidity** process monitoring due to their wide range, in-situ capability, and robustness.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, May 2024]`

**Q: A backscatter turbidity analyzer is calibrated using a 100 NTU Formazin standard. The dark voltage is 0.02 V, and the output for the 100 NTU standard is 1.10 V. A wastewater sample gives an output of 3.74 V.**

**(a) Calculate the turbidity of the wastewater sample in NTU. (7 marks)**

**(b) What volume of 4000 NTU Formazin stock is required to prepare 250 mL of the 100 NTU standard? Describe the procedure and state the precautions. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:**

- $V_{dark} = 0.02$ V
- $T_1 = 100$ NTU
- $V_1 = 1.10$ V (at 100 NTU)
- $V_x = 3.74$ V (sample)

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$T_x = T_1 \cdot \frac{V_x - V_{dark}}{V_1 - V_{dark}}$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$T_x = 100 \times \frac{3.74 - 0.02}{1.10 - 0.02}$$

**Computation (3 marks):**

$$T_x = 100 \times \frac{3.72}{1.08}$$

$$T_x = 100 \times 3.4444$$

$$\boxed{\,T_x \approx 344.4 \; \text{NTU}\,}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** This value is typical of **secondary-treated domestic wastewater effluent**, indicating that the BTA is operating correctly and the sample does not require additional dilution for measurement.

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**

- $C_{stock} = 4000$ NTU
- $C_{diluted} = 100$ NTU
- $V_f = 250$ mL

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$C_{diluted} = C_{stock} \cdot \frac{V_p}{V_f}$$

**Substitution and solution for $V_p$ (2 marks):**

$$V_p = \frac{C_{diluted} \cdot V_f}{C_{stock}} = \frac{100 \times 250}{4000}$$

$$V_p = \frac{25{,}000}{4000} = 6.25 \; \text{mL}$$

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Take a clean, dry **250 mL Class-A volumetric flask**.
2. Use a **calibrated 10 mL Class-A pipette** to transfer exactly **6.25 mL** of the 4000 NTU Formazin stock.
3. Add **particle-free (0.2 µm filtered, deionized) water** to approximately two-thirds volume.
4. Swirl gently to homogenize (avoid vigorous shaking that introduces air bubbles).
5. Make up to the **250 mL calibration mark** with particle-free water at 25 °C.
6. Stopper and invert 25 times for uniform mixing.

**Precautions (1 mark):**

- Use **freshly prepared** standards (Formazin standards degrade within 1–2 weeks).
- Maintain **temperature at 25 ± 2 °C**.
- Discard if **visible flocculation or settling** is observed.
- Use **glassware cleaned with chromic acid** and rinsed with particle-free water to avoid contamination.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in BTA Questions (Compiled from 2018–2024 KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing FNU with NTU** — 1 FNU ≈ 1 NTU for Formazin, but in **StablCal (polymer bead) standards** the equivalence is **1 NTU ≈ 0.9 FNU**. Misuse loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting dark-current subtraction** — In the calibration numerical, if a student writes $T_x = T_1 \times V_x / V_1$, they will get ~340 NTU, but the correct answer is **344.4 NTU**. Examiners award 5 of 7 marks only — the missing 2 marks are for the dark offset.
> 3. **Stating the wrong wavelength** — Writing "white light" or "660 nm" instead of the **ISO 7027-mandated 860 nm** is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Detector angle error** — Backscatter is **140° – 180°**, NOT 180° only. Some light is collected from 140° upward for higher signal-to-noise.
> 5. **In comparison questions, failing to mention the range** — Always state **"BTA is suitable for high-turbidity ranges (up to 4000+ NTU)"**. This single statement is worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing "in-situ" with "online"** — In-situ means the probe is **immersed directly in the process stream**; online means sample is **withdrawn and transported** to an external analyzer. BTA can be either, but **in-situ immersion is its key advantage**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Turbidity unit:** NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Unit); FNU is the ISO 7027 equivalent.
- [x] **Primary standard:** Formazin polymer at **4000 NTU** (from hydrazine sulfate + hexamethylenetetramine).
- [x] **ISO 7027 wavelength:** **860 nm ± 30 nm (NIR)**.
- [x] **EPA 180.1 wavelength:** 400–600 nm (white light) — *not used for BTA*.
- [x] **Backscatter geometry:** Detector at **140° – 180°**; ideal for **0 – 4000+ NTU**.
- [x] **Beer–Lambert form:** $I = I_0 \, e^{-\tau L}$, with $\tau \approx 0.0025 \times C_{NTU}$ (in m⁻¹).
- [x] **Linear response model:** $T_x = T_1 (V_x - V_{dark}) / (V_1 - V_{dark})$ — **always subtract dark voltage**.
- [x] **Formazin dilution formula:** $C_{diluted} = 4000 \times V_p / V_f$.
- [x] **Rayleigh regime:** Particle size $d < 0.1\lambda$, $I_s \propto 1/\lambda^4$.
- [x] **Mie regime:** Particle size $d \approx \lambda$, weak $\lambda$ dependence.
- [x] **Key advantage of BTA:** In-situ, no sample transport, handles high NTU, low color sensitivity.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Multiple scattering causes non-linearity above ~500 NTU — polynomial correction required.
- [x] **Applications:** Activated sludge (MLSS), wastewater effluent, mining tailings, pulp & paper, food clarification.
- [x] **Calibration check frequency:** Weekly for process analyzers; daily for drinking water applications.
- [x] **Self-cleaning methods:** Ultrasonic transducer, mechanical wiper, air-purge systems on modern probes.
